park_name: Abraham Lincoln Birthplace National Historical Park
description: The entirety of Abraham Lincoln Birthplace National Historic Park receives potable water from the Larue County Water District.  The Park's distribution systems are connected to the Larue County Water System at the Abraham Lincoln Birthplace, Picnic Area, and Knob Creek sites.  This system serves the Picnic Area Comfort Station, Visitor Center Comfort Station, Knob Creek Comfort Station, Knob Creek Ranger Station, Visitor Center/Park Headquarters, two staff residences, and Maintenance Shop.  The Picnic Area and Knob Creek Comfort Stations are seasonal and close between December and February.
point_of_use: Picnic Area Comfort Station, Visitor Center Comfort Station, Knob Creek Comfort Station, Knob Creek Ranger Station, Visitor Center/Park Headquarters, two staff residences, and Maintenance Shop

description: This system serves a public restroom. It consists of two wells, both located outdoors with submersible pumps, sanitary well caps, and well casings that were raised above ground at least 12 inches. Treatment consists of disinfection with sodium hypochlorite. The chemical feed pump was LMI Model P131-88HV, 0.42 GPH. Chlorinated water is pumped to the 10,000 gallon steel, ground level storage tank. Then treated water flows to the system by gravity and then is boosted at the restroom by a pressure tank and booster pump. Storage tank level controls activate the well pump. The chlorine feed pump is controlled by a flow switch (i.e. Dwyer Flotect). This is a seasonal system.

description: The seasonal water system provides water to the Jordan Pond dorms from April to October. The water system consists of a well located outdoors with a casing that extends at least 12 inches above ground. It has a submersible pump (Goulds Model 25EL304; 3 HP) and a sanitary well cap with vent. The well pump is activated by a pressure tank. The water is pumped to a treatment room (adjacent storage closets), is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite (chemical feed pump was LMI P-151-198 with adjustable speed and stroke settings), passed through two fiberglass contact tanks (270 gallons each), and four fiberglass pressure tanks, then goes to the building fixtures. The system is operated by the concessionaire with support from the ACAD Facilities Program.

water_system_name: Mt. Desert Island Municipal Water System
description:
This system consists of a municipally operated source and treatment facility (i.e., Mount Desert Water District, Seal Harbor, source Jordan Pond) and a NPS operated booster pump station and storage tanks at Blackwoods Campground (46,200-gallon tank) and Sand Beach (10,000-gallon tank). The site serves the following recreation areas and campgrounds: Wildwood Stables, Blackwoods Campground, and Sand Beach. Total length of service line was reported as 7 miles.

Blackwoods Campground is divided into two loops with both tent and RV sites mixed together. There are 281 sites in total (60 for RVs and 217 for tents) plus four group campsites (tents only). Most of the RV sites are located in Loop A.

In the future this municipal system may connect and serve the Jordan Pond House Restaurant and Dorm.
point_of_use: Wildwood Stables, Blackwoods Campground, and Sand Beach
source_notes:
Direct connection to health hazard with no/improper backfow protection in place. (DL 5)
Overfow on tank is not properly covered (#24 mesh or better) and thus increases pest and rodent risk issues. (DL 4)
Potential health hazard with no backfow device in place. (DL 4)
Deteriorated water distribution/ storage/treatment/ source facilities will likely fail. (DL 4)

description: This is a public water system. The system was reported as being acquired from the Navy in 2001. The source is a well. Treatment consists of a radon removal unit, chemical feed equipment for addition of sodium hypochlorite and a sequestering agent. There is a 5,000 gallon underground storage tank and a 150,000 gallon elevated storage tank for storage of finished water. Water is pumped from the well, flows to the treatment room, passes through a flow meter and aerator for radon removal, is injected with chlorine and orthophosphate, flows to underground storage tank with booster pumps, is pumped to the elevated storage tank, then flowed by gravity to the distribution system. The system serves the Schoodic Institute and Education Center with food service and offices, a small campground with 14 RV sites and 3 tent sites used primarily by volunteers and staff, a public restroom, a maintenance building, and a wastewater treatment plant.
point_of_use: Schoodic Institute and Education Center with food service and offices, a small campground with 14 RV sites and 3 tent sites used primarily by volunteers and staff

description: This is a seasonal system that serves the Schoodic Woods Campground. The source is two wells located outdoors with casings raised above ground (at least 12 inches). Each well has a submersible 18GPM Goulds pump and a steel pressure tank (20 gallons). The well pumps operate in lead and lag modes. Tank level sensors for the finished water storage tank activated the well pumps. Treatment consists of the addition of sodium hypochlorite for continuous disinfection (a Stenner Model SVP4, 5 GPD chemical feed pump was used). The finished water storage tank is a buried, concrete 6,000-gallon tank. Water flows by gravity to the campground, staff quarters, offices, and maintenance shop.
point_of_use: Schoodic Woods Campground, including the campground, staff quarters, offices, and maintenance shop

description:
The contact station at Alibates Quarry (completed in 2006) is provided with water from an onsite well and pressure system. A reverse osmosis (RO) system was installed in 2013 to provide additional treatment but the system is not approved as potable by the TCEQ.  The well pumps water to a 210 gallon plastic water tank in the contact station.  The water is treated before the tank with a water softener, RO system, and pH adjustment filter.  The well pump is controlled by floats in the water tank.  Sodium hypochlorite is injected into the water tank when the well and RO system are on.   The water is then pressurized using a 100 gallon pressure tank and booster pump which are controlled by a pressure switch.  A 120 gallon chlorine contact tank is also utilized to provide chlorine contact time.  The park provides bottled water to the public and signs access points as non-potable.  

A fire suppression system consists of a ground level storage tank and booster pumps that feed a sprinkler system and outside hookup for fire hoses.  A reduced pressure zone valve assembly (RPZ) backflow device is present on the fire suppression system.  There is also a double check valve for the irrigation system and a RPZ on the plumbing in the comfort station.
source_notes: According to Rockd, the entire park falls within the Quartermaster Formation.

description:
This Public Non-Community Transient water system serves two residences, a fish cleaning-station, visitor center, maintenance yard, two RV sites, and two comfort stations.  Both residences are now unoccupied and park administration has decided to discontinue providing employee residences at this site.  The water system consists of a 520-foot, 6-inch diameter well fitted with a pitless adapter.  Water is chlorinated with a hypochlorinator located in a pump room in the maintenance building prior to being delivered to three 9,000-gallon fiberglass storage tanks.  Well pump operation is controlled by a set of three float switches located in the water storage tanks. 
Water from the water storage tanks is pressurized with a 3 horsepower booster pump into a 250 gallon pressure tank.  The booster pump and pressure tank are located in the pumphouse.  A 25 horsepower fire pump is available to pump fire flows directly from the water storage tanks into the distribution system that consists of 6-inch ductile iron pipe.  The water tanks can be bypassed by closing the tank inlet valves, opening the bypass valve, and switching the toggle switch on the control panel.  This allows for the water system to be operated with the well pump pressurizing the water system with the tanks offline in the event that the booster pump fails.
point_of_use: two residences, a fish cleaning-station, visitor center, maintenance yard, two RV sites, and two comfort stations
source_notes: An 8-inch borehole was drilled to 150 feet and 5¬Ω" ID casing was cemented in place. The well was completed by drilling a 5-inch borehole to 300 feet. Water was encountered at 60 and 100 feet. After cementing, the borehole was dry until a depth of 180 feet where there was a small crevice in the limestone. Additional small water bearing crevice was encountered at 200 feet. A larger crevice was encountered at 240 feet. A "honeycomb" type limestone produced 30 to 70 gpm. Flow was very strong and cold, indicative of spring water.  Entire inter

description: This Public Non-Community Transient Water System serves the visitor center, fish cleaning station, comfort station, ranger station, one residence, and picnic area. This employee residence is unoccupied and park administration has decided to discontinue providing employee residences at this site. Water is obtained from a 625-foot deep, 8-inch diameter well and pumped into a 20,000-gallon bolted steel reservoir located in an adjacent building. The water is chlorinated by a hypochlorinator located in the pump room prior to entering the reservoir. The well pump is controlled with float switches located in the reservoir.  The reservoir supplies a hydropneumatic pumping system that is used to maintain distribution system pressure. The pressure system, located in the pump room adjacent to the reservoir, consists of three booster pumps (3 horsepower, single PHSse) and two pressure tanks (Well-X-Trol Model WX 251). A 5 horsepower fire pump located in the pump room bypasses the pressurization system and delivers water directly from the water storage tank to the distribution. The fire pump is operated manually.
point_of_use: visitor center, fish cleaning station, comfort station, ranger station, one residence, picnic area
source_notes: The well is 625 feet deep. Casing (6-5/8" OD) was cemented in the upper part of the well to a depth of 151.5 feet. The remainder of the well, from 151.5 to 625 feet is uncased. From 151.5 to 625 feet the well has a diameter of 5 inches. The well obtains most of its water from solution cavities and fractures in the Devils River Limestone. The main water bearing zone is from 595 to 625 feet below ground surface. Several minor water bearing zones (less than 5 gpm) occur at shallower depths (230, 325, 410, 430 feet). Static water level in the well was approximately 153.5 feet below the top of casing at the time the well was constructed, June 9, 1980.

description: In April 2019, the park administrative staff moved into a new GSA leased facility that is now park headquarters and a visitor center.  The park has no operational responsibilities for this water system.  The water system consists of a well and pressure tank.  No information is currently available on the depth of the well and water quality testing.  The water is not chlorinated.  The park staff exceeds 25 people for more than 6 months per year, so it appears that this water system should be classified as a public water system (non-community, non-transient).  The park is currently working with GSA to have TCEQ classify the water system to establish required water testing requirements and regulatory responsibilities.

description:
This Public Non-Community Transient System serves a Ranger Station, two comfort stations, a boat-launching ramp, a fish cleaning station, a RV dump station, picnic areas, and marina operated by the park.  The water source for this system is a 490-foot, 6-inch diameter well located inside the pumphouse.  A second well was drilled near the pumphouse, but remains offline.  Water from the well is chlorinated with a sodium hypochlorite and a chemical feed pump and then pumped to a 250,000-gallon concrete water tank located east of the entrance road.  Float switches in the water tank control the well pump. Operating signals are sent from the tank to the well pump via radio telemetry.  

Water from the reservoir is pressurized by a booster station located in a building adjacent to the water tank.  This booster station consists of two 10-horsepower (HP) pumps, one 5 HP booster pump, and a 250-gallon pressure tank.  The booster pumps have variable frequency drives (VFDs) that control pump operation.
point_of_use: Ranger Station, two comfort stations, a boat-launching ramp, a fish cleaning station, a RV dump station, picnic areas, marina operated by the park
source_notes: This well is also called "McNamara Well No. 2" in our files. The well is either 480 or 490 feet deep; both numbers are indicated in our files. The well is reported to have 10 feet of 8-inch casing at the surface. The remainder of the well, from 10 to 480 feet was drilled with a 7¬Ω" bit on a rotary drilling rig. The well was drilled by a firm named McNamara-Leavel and was used to furnish water for the grouting operations associated with construction of the dam.

description: This Public Non-Community Transient Water System supplies water to 7 drinking water faucets within the campground area and VIP RV site. The system consists of a 520-foot, 6-inch diameter well, a 211-gallon hydropneumatic pressure tank for storage and system pressure, and a hypochlorinator for chlorine disinfection. A pressure switch that operates the pump between 40 and 60 psi controls the well pump. A 50 micron filter cartridge is provided to remove sediment and sand. The distribution system consists of 2-inch galvanized piping that goes to the swim beach and shelter sites.

description: The water system serves the maintenance building, Micro Beach pavillion, amphitheater comfort station, administrative offices, visitor center, and comfort stations at the tennis courts, Smiling Cove marina, upper marina, and Micro Beach.  The water system consists of a connection to the Hyatt water supply, a 2-compartment 65,000-gallon above ground concrete water storage tank, a pressurization system with two booster pumps and a hydropneumatic tank, supplemental chlorination equipment, fire booster pump, and water distribution piping.  The water source is treated water from the Hyatt Hotel located adjacent to the park.  The Hyatt draws its water from a drilled well and treats it by reverse osmosis filtration and disinfection.  The Hyatt sells excess water to the park.  The water is stored in the park‚Äôs water storage tank.  Two booster pumps and a hydropneumatic tank pressurize the water for use within the park.

description:
This was a hand pump well along the Appalachian trail in Dutchess County, NY. The hand pump was by Baker Monitor, Model 11HD designed for drinking water. The casing extended above ground about 12 in (6 in protruded from a cement pad surrounding the casing). There was no underdrain system to divert pumped water away from the casing. There was a well log available. This was a bedrock well drilled in 1996. The total depth was 125 ft, the casing depth was 20 ft, it was grouted (no depth reported), bedrock was encountered at depth of 9 ft and it was water bearing at this depth, the static water level was 4 ft. There was no treatment for this well. There was a hiker shelter and moldering privy at this site.

Site was said to be inactive with funding requested for removal. Noted on 2/10/23

description: The well at Camp Nahmakanta is a bedrock well, 125 feet in total depth, with casing of 85 feet deep, and 84 feet of overburden, drilled in 2000 (info from Maine Geological Survey database). The well is equipped with a submersible pump (15 gpm, set at 50 feet) powered by a generator. The well pump is operated by a manual switch. There are no automatic well pump controls. There is no treatment provided for this system. About 1,500 gallons of water is being stored onsite; the tanks are elevated about seven feet off the ground. There are two steel 250 gallon tanks on the east side of the camp being stored together, a 250 gallon tank steel tank and a 250 gallon poly tank on the west side of the camp, and one 500 gallon tank for the main building with the kitchen and dining hall. Staff operates the well pump daily to keep tanks full. The system is operated by the lessee.

description:
This is a well. The system consists of a residence and outbuildings, with a trail shelter nearby, and lies within the portion of the Appalachian Trail maintained by the Blue Mountain Eagle Climbing and Canoeing Club.The water at 501 is chlorinated (starting in 2022). Water was sampled in 2023 and results revealed: 
Color elevated to 18 units (above the 15 units listed as a MCL)
pH was low at 6.23 ( lower than the 6.5 set limit)
Langlier Index was negative indicating corrosive water (-4.13 which is  below the -0.5)
Manganese, Total was high at 0.667 mg/L (above the 0.05 mg/L MCL)
Coliform and e. coli was tested in NOV 2021 - negative results. 

Water used by caretaker and hiker

description: This system serves the headquarters building, visitor center, five staff housing units, three RV sites, wastewater treatment plant, and the University of Maryland building. The system consists of two wells located outdoors with submersible pumps. Both wells had casings raised above ground (at least 18 inches) and sanitary well caps. Treatment consists of iron removal (sodium hypochlorite and two green sand filters designed to operate in parallel) and continuous disinfection (chlorination with sodium hypochlorite and chemical feed equipment operated by flow switch). There is an above ground contact/storage tank with capacity of 4,000 gallons. Water enters at top and exits at bottom of tank, and tank level sensors controlled the well pump. There are five booster pumps and three fiberglass pressure tanks for system pressure and distribution.
point_of_use: Headquarters building, visitor's center, staff housing (5 buildings with a fitness center), wastewater treatment plant, and the University of Maryland building

description:
Badlands National Park is now connected to the Mni Wiconi Rural Water System.  The water system brings potable water to many towns and rural areas in western South Dakota.  Water is pumped from the Missouri River, treated and then piped to users in the southwest quarter of the State of South Dakota.  The park is basically a service connection to the system.  The rural water system is responsible for water quality and testing.  All park areas are now connected to this rural water system.
Part of the system has an underground concrete water storage tank that receives water from the MNI Wiconi rural water system that is used fire suppression.  They also have a separate line that connects to the MNI Wiconi system for potable drinking water usage for the Ranger Station and Fee Station.
There are two ground water wells connected to MNI Wiconi rural water, which are not active.

description: The facilities in Frijoles Canyon and on the mesa, except the entrance station that has no water supply, are served by a water distribution system connected to a water main along NM highway 4.  Water from the City of Los Alamos Water System feeds into a 35,000 gallon glass-fused-to-steel bolted  water storage tank on top of the mesa.  This tank is located near the point of connection into the City's water supply distribution system and is used to equalize water pressure within the park when pressures fluctuate within the City's system. An electrically operated valve at the edge of the mesa controls water flow to a 20,000-gallon, two-compartment, below-grade concrete reservoir located above the Monument's Administration building. Probes in the reservoir signal the valve to open and close as necessary.  There is a bypass line with a PRV at the concrete tank so that it can be taken offline for maintenance.  Water then flows to the Canyon facilities through a distribution system.

This Public Non-Community Water System serves the Cottonwood Campground, staff housing, RV VIP sites, and the Historic District. Two (2) wells pump water through a treatment building where chlorine solution is injected for disinfection followed by a 1655 gallon chlorine contact tank.  Well #3 can only be pumped at 1 gpm or less, otherwise it runs dry.  Well #3 is primarily used since it has better overall water quality, but has the following water quality concerns:  
Well #3 Water Quality Concerns: 
-	Gross alPHS concentration = 21 pCi/L (exceeds the primary MCL of 15.0 pCi/L)
-	Arsenic = 10 parts per billion (ppb) (at the primary MCL of 10 ppb)  
-	Uranium = 27 ppb (just below the MCL of 30 ppb)
-	Sulfate = 463 mg/L (exceeds the secondary MCL of 250 mg/L)
-	Total dissolved solids (TDS) = 1010 mg/L (exceeds the secondary MCL of 500 mg/L)  

Well #1 pumps at a rate of approximately 5 gpm, but has lower overall water quality than Well #3 as follows:
Well #1 Water Quality Concerns: 
-	Manganese = 1.05 mg/L (exceeds the secondary MCL of 0.05 mg/L)
-	Sulfate = 543 mg/L (exceeds the secondary MCL of 250 mg/L)
-	TDS = 2360 mg/L (exceeds the secondary MCL of 500 mg/L)  
-	Arsenic = 9.2 ppb (just below the MCL of 10 ppb) 
-	Gross alPHS = 11 pCi/L (just below the MCL of 15 pCi/L) 

A third well, New Well #2 was drilled in 2022 and will be placed online soon.  The Old Well #2 has been abandoned.  The New Well #2 was reported to be pump tested at 100 gpm, but a 20 gpm pump will be installed.  New Well #2 has the following water quality concerns:
Well #2 Water Quality Concerns: 
-	Manganese = 0.432 mg/L (exceeds the secondary MCL of 0.05 mg/L)
-	Sulfate = 427 mg/L (exceeds the secondary MCL of 250 mg/L)
-	TDS = 1170 mg/L (exceeds the secondary MCL of 500 mg/L)  
-	Hardness = 366 mg/L as CaCO3 (> 180 mg/L is very hard) 
-	Color = 100 color units (exceeds the secondary MCL of 15 color units)

The chlorinated water is pumped to three 10,000-gallon storage tanks in the new building next to the housing area.  Prior to reaching the tanks, a lateral tees off to the campground and VIP sites.  Water from the tanks is pressured with a booster system consisting of two alternating pressure pumps, a fire pump, and a surge tank with check valve.  One small reverse osmosis system is in the treatment building and a potable water tap is provided outside the treatment building for residents to obtain potable water.  Another reverse osmosis system is at the campground housed in a building with an outside potable water spigot for drinking purposes.  The rest of the system is currently designated as non-potable.
point_of_use: Cottonwood Campground, staff housing, RV VIP sites, and the Historic District

description: This is a non-public water system that serves a single residence at Harte Ranch, where the park's airplane runway is located. Bottled water is hauled for drinking and cooking purposes.  A rainwater collection system from the roof of the residence flows into two 1,700 gallon plastic above ground water storage tanks.  Water flows through a course water filter, then a 1 micron absolute filter, and is then pressurized by a small pump and pressure tank for the residence (toilets, bathing, sinks, etc.).  When rainwater is not sufficient, water is hauled from the Panther Junction water system and is discharged into the two rainwater catchment water tanks.  The water system is not chlorinated.

description:
The Chisos Basin (CB) water system is a Public Non-Community, Transient Water System serves the Chisos Basin Lodge and Restaurant, convenience store, Park Service and concession staff housing, picnic area and campground.

The water source consists of a spring located at Oak Springs located at the base of Chisos Mountain, outside the ‚ Window .  The facilities include five horizontal pipes set into the spring, a concrete spring box, a concrete weir box with V-notch weir and flow recording instrument to measure flow, and overflow and bypass piping.  The flow from the spring varies considerably depending on rainfall in the recharge area.

Water is collected in a spring box and flows by gravity to a 25,000-gallon tank at Oak Springs.  Water is then transferred from the 25,000 gallon tank to a 500,000-gallon tank at Oak Springs via a small transfer pump located in an enclosure near the 25,000 gallon tank.  Two 60 horsepower booster pumps lift water from this tank at Oak Springs to a 125,000 gallon concrete tank above the basin via a dedicated water transmission line.  The water is chlorinated before entering the 125,000 gallon concrete tank with sodium hypochlorite.  Water is then filtered through a 5 micron cartridge filter followed by two filters in parallel measuring 2 microns (HC/170-LT2) to meet the requirements of the long term 2 enhanced surface water treatment rule before it is transferred to a 500,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank prior to distribution.

description: The Persimmon Gap (P-Gap) water system is a Public Non-Community System that serves two apartments, two VIP sites, and the visitor center. Water is hauled from the Panther Junction water system and is discharged into a 16,652 gallon bolted steel water storage tank with an aluminum roof.  The water is pressurized with two booster pumps and two pressure tanks and then water is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite, if needed, before it enters the distribution system.  A pressure switch set to operate between 40 and 60 psi pressure controls the booster pump.

description:
This Public Community Water System serves all facilities located at Panther Junction.  This includes park and concession staff residences, maintenance yard, horse stables, Panther Junction Visitor Center, service station/convenience store, a school, K-Bar Ranch House, and the Construction Camp.

The system has 3 pressure zones and consists of three (3) producing groundwater wells, three (3) water storage tanks, two (2) booster stations, disinfection treatment systems, and a distribution system.  

Two wells with submersible pumps are located by the K-Bar Ranch House, K-Bar 5 (KB5) and K-Bar 10 (KB10).  Each well has its own pumphouse where the water is chlorinated before being discharged into a 24,000-gallon glass-fused-to-steel water storage tank constructed in 2022. These wells are operated automatically by radio telemetry based on the water level in the 24,000 gallon tank.   
   
Two 10-horsepower booster pumps, housed in a booster station building in K-Bar next to the KB-10 well pumphouse, pump water from the 24,000 gallon water storage tank to a 250,000 gallon welded steel water storage tank via a dedicated fill line.  The booster pumps are run automatically by a radio telemetry system based on the water level in the 250,000 gallon tank.  Water then flows from the 250,000 gallon tank into a 100,000 gallon concrete water tank through an altitude valve and then flows by gravity flow to the community.  A 1.5 HP booster pump and 119 gallon pressure tank located in the KB-10 pumphouse provide pressure to the K-Bar Ranch House and Construction Camp.  A recirculation line is available for this pressure zone to maintain adequate chlorine residuals in K-Bar.  Water flows by gravity to most of the facilities at Panther Junction.  

Well PJ4 is located in the staff residential area.  PJ4, pumps water through a separate pumphouse where it is chlorinated before being discharged directly into the 250,000 gallon welded storage tank.  PJ4 is run manually as needed due to its slow recharge capacity.

A booster station with booster pumps and a pressure tank is used to pressurize water for the residences at higher elevations.
point_of_use: concession staff residences, maintenance yard, horse stables, Panther Junction Visitor Center, service station/convenience store, a school, K-Bar Ranch House, and the Construction Camp

Two 10-horsepower booster pumps, housed in a booster station building in K-Bar next to the KB-10 well pumphouse, pump water from the 24,000 gallon water storage tank to a 250,000 gallon welded steel water storage tank via a dedicated fill line.  The booster pumps are run automatically by a radio telemetry system based on the water level in the 250,000 gallon tank.  Water then flows from the 250,000 gallon tank into a 100,000 gallon concrete water tank through an altitude valve and then flows by gravity flow to the community.  A 1.5 HP booster pump and 119 gallon pressure tank located in the KB-10 pumphouse provide pressure to the K-Bar Ranch House and Construction Camp.  A recirculation line is available for this pressure zone to maintain adequate chlorine residuals in K-Bar.  Water flows by gravity to most of the facilities at Panther Junction.  

Well PJ4 is located in the staff residential area.  PJ4, pumps water through a separate pumphouse where it is chlorinated before being discharged directly into the 250,000 gallon welded storage tank.  PJ4 is run manually as needed due to its slow recharge capacity.

A booster station with booster pumps and a pressure tank is used to pressurize water for the residences at higher elevations.
point_of_use: concession staff residences, maintenance yard, horse stables, Panther Junction Visitor Center, service station/convenience store, a school, K-Bar Ranch House, and the Construction Camp

description:
This Non-Public water system serves the Barker House, a remodeled historic structure near Rio Grande Village that is used as a residence for researchers, park staff, and park guests.

An individual water well is housed in an aluminum enclosure adjacent to the residence. A 5 micron and 1 micron absolute filter are also housed in the aluminum enclosure to provide protection against giardia and cryptosporidium as the well may be under the influence of surface water.  Sodium hypochlorite is injected for disinfection followed by a 119 gallon FlexCon Mix Master chlorine contact tank with a baffling factor of 0.6 mg/L and a 80 gallon composite pressure tank to provide pressure to the house.

description:
The Rio Grande Village (RGV) water system serves the convenience store with shower and laundry facilities, 5 trailers, 10 houses, a trailer park, a campground with 100 sites and four comfort stations, an overflow campground area, an RV dump station, and the maintenance building.

The primary source for this system is a 798 foot deep well that was drilled in 2004 and placed into service in 2009.  The well is cased with 10-inch steel casing to a depth of 210 feet with the annual space grouted from 0 to 210 feet.  The well below 210 feet is finished in open borehole with a diameter of 6 inches.  Static water level in 2004 was 41 feet below grade.  The maximum long term sustainable well yield is estimated to be 15 gpm.  The only water quality issues with the well are secondary contaminants as follows: hardness = 720 mg/L (> 180 is considered hard), sulfates = 350 mg/L (secondary MCL = 250 mg/L), and total dissolved solids (TDS) = 670 mg/L (secondary MCL = 500 mg/L).   

Water is pumped from the well, through a treatment building where it is chlorinated, then to the 200,000 gallon glass-fused-to-steel water storage tank with an aluminum geodesic dome roof via a dedicated line.  The spring is available for backup when needed on an emergency basis only.  The spring can only be used for 24 hours per year to limit impact on the endangered gambusia pond nearby.  The spring is not equipped with filtration.  The supply line from the spring source is also routed through the treatment building for chlorination.  Control of the system is automated.

The well is located in a wash a short distance east of the tank.  The spring located about ¬Ω mile southeast of the Rio Grande Village Store.  The spring box is a stone and mortar type structure.   The water distribution system consists of 8 and 6-inch PVC water main.  The existing 8-inch ductile iron water main from the water storage tank to the distribution system was slip-lined with 6-inch PE pipe.  The treatment building contains two recirculating pumps that can circulate water from the tank (if it becomes stagnant during low water usage) and has controls for additional chlorine injection.  There is also a SCADA panel where parameters can be monitored and controlled.

A chlorine booster station building near the Berkley Cottage can inject additional chlorine into the waterline that runs to Boquillas Crossing.  This chlorine booster station has not been used to date.

description:
The Ewing-Snell Ranch contains a restored historical ranch house used by park personnel and researchers and other park sponsored groups.  There is public access to the site and a nearby parking area that can be used by the public.  There is no public water system, however, the original ranch water system collects untreated non-potable surface water from the near-by creek and pipes it to the ranch for use to irrigate the lawn.  There is a frost-free hydrant in the yard of the ranch house that is connected to the non-potable system and is used to connect hoses for lawn sprinklers.  The ranch property is often used to accommodate park-sponsored groups of campers.

There is a non-public potable water cistern that serves facilities in the main house is supplied with potable water hauled from the Lovell Wyoming municipal water system.  The house is a seasonal residence operated and maintained by the park Interpretation division.  A potable water hauling truck owned by the park is used for hauling the water to the cistern.  Water hauling is performed by Resource Management staff.

description:
Source:  The source is a spring (Tillet Spring) classified as groundwater.  The spring is located at the Tillet Fish Rearing Station which is operated by the Wyoming Game and Fish Department.   Water is collected in a concrete spring box and then piped ~1.7 miles under a state highway to a chlorination treatment building that is located within the park. The collection transmission main is 6 inch ductile iron that has been slip lined.   The spring water collection area is fenced and has a surface water diversion ditch.  Historic data and observation at Tillet Spring shows very little fluctuation of pH and water turbidity, indicating that the source is likely very stable and of high quality.  The collection structure is a 3x3x8' deep concrete vault with a gravel base.  Water collects in the vault and gravity flows to the chlorination building and on to the storage tank via a 4‚Äù poly main that was installed in 1996.  Two MPA tests were completed in the spring and fall of 2014 to assess the potential for surface water influence.  The MPA test results indicated a low risk for surface water influence and groundwater status was maintained.  In subsequent   communications from EPA it was made clear that EPA will continue to closely assess this source for potential surface water influence in the future and indicated that further MPA tests may be required based on survey assessments.  Proactive maintenance activities in order to maintain desirable vegetation, remove deep rooted woody vegetation, maintain fencing, maintain surface water diversion ditching and maintain the sanitary seal on the collection box hatch will be critical in order to maintain groundwater designation.

Treatment:  Water is chlorinated by chlorination injection pump system that is controlled by telemetric controls and a float switch in the reservoir.  After chlorination, water is delivered to the storage reservoir via gravity flow in a dedicated 4 inch poly line.  Chlorination can be by-passed in this system through use of a valve located outside the treatment building.  This by-pass system is used in the winter season to provide non-chlorinated water to the comfort stations for use by winter fishermen.  The winter-time water is labeled as NON-POTABLE in the comfort station.

Storage and Distribution:  Treated water is stored in a 100,000 gallon above-ground steel reservoir.  Water is distributed to a campground with two loops and two comfort stations, the marina, playground and swimming beach.  The campground has to two host RV sites with full service hook-ups and has recently had 10 full service RV sites in each loop.
point_of_use: campground with two loops and two comfort stations, the marina, playground and swimming beach.  The campground has to two host RV sites with full service hook-ups and has recently had 10 full service RV sites in each loop.

water_system_name: UTIL Ft Smith Ranger Station Water System
description:
This is a non-public water system serving a ranger contact station.  The water is treated as non-potable.  The facility has public restrooms and there are no drinking fountains.  A bottled water cooler is provided and the restroom sinks are posted as non-potable water.

Source:
The source is a well of unknown construction.  The well has significant problems with silt and sand.

Treatment & Distribution:
There is no disinfection on this system.  After being pumped from the well there is a foot valve inside the basement of the building which is followed by a filter and sand trap, 2 pressure tanks, a 150 gallon storage tank.  The line supplying water to the building restrooms has an additional filter on in.  A separate line supplies water for sprinkler irrigation and has an RPZ installed on it to separate it from the rest of the system.

Source:  There are two sources on this system, a developed spring and a well.  The spring has been classified as Ground Water Under the Direct influence of Surface Water (GWUDISW) since 2016.  The spring source was abandoned, and the system operated with the well as the only source from 2016-2019.  A new surface water treatment plant was completed in 2019-2020 and started operation in 2020.  The well which served as the primary source during the surface water treatment plant construction is classified as groundwater and now serves as a back-up source.

The spring is located on a steep rocky slope above the lake and marina.  Water is collected via 2 zones of perforated pipe to a spring box with an overflow.  From the spring box water is collected into a 6 inch ductile iron line that is transitioned to a 2‚Äù PVC line prior to the treatment pumphouse.  The spring was redeveloped in 2005 and in 2015 to increase production and address potential surface water influence deficiencies.  However, as stated previously the spring was declared GWUDISW by the State of Montana DEQ in 2016.

The well is located ~800 feet from the spring by the large upper parking lot.  The 203‚Äô well was drilled in 1968 and was historically used to supplement the spring during start-up and in low water flow years when the spring did not produce adequately.  The well had been removed as a source but was re-connected in an emergency project in the spring of 2015 and served as the sole water source from 2016-2019.  In 2017, the park requested the NPS Water Resources Division (WRD) to get the well pump tested and inspected.  Results of this assessment indicated that the well could most likely serve as an adequate source for the demands of this water system.  Two triggered raw water samples taken June 4, 2015 tested positive for Total Coliform and E. coli, resulting in the system being placed on a 4-log virus removal compliance monitoring category.  The well water is chlorinated but not filtered with the surface water treatment filters.  

2020 New Treatment System:  Spring water is delivered to the treatment building and immediately pressure boosted with a 1.5 hp booster pump, pressure gauge, pressure relief valve, and a series of three Harmsco cartridge filters from 5 micron to absolute 1 micron filter pore size.   Filtration is followed by chlorination and delivery to the underground storage tank.  Automated monitors for finished water include turbidity, pH, a chlorine.  The well water line enters the treatment building separately from the spring water, is chlorinated but by-passes the filtration bank.  

Storage and Distribution:  
Treated water is stored in a 50,000 gallon buried concrete reservoir directly outside the spring collection area.  The system serves a marina and a fish cleaning station.  The marina has a small food service operation, irrigated lawns, and a boat dock operation.  There is a black water pump out and holding tank as well as a hose for potable water tank filling located on the dock.  A point-of-entry (POE) sample tap was added to the distribution as part of the 2015 emergency project.  However, the sample tap in the comfort station plumbing chase is being utilized as the POE sample site, and a Hach model sc200 free chlorine, pH, and temperature monitoring meter was installed in the plumbing chase with the 2020 system upgrade project.

water_system_name: UTIL Crooked Creek Ranger Station Water System
description:
Source: Water is supplied to the contact station by a 103 foot  deep non-public well located directly adjacent to the ranger station parking lot.  Water quality is reported to be very poor and has historically been utilized as a non-potable well.

Treatment:In 2019, park rangers added a treatment system provided by Kinetico.  The system components in order are a 50 micron pre-filter, a water softener, and UV.  For drinking water there is an under sink RO unit.  The treated water is only available to employees in the employee restroom and breakroom sink.  The public is provided bottled drinking water.

Source:This well was activated as a non-potable water source in 2019 when the treatment system was installed.  Very little is known about the construction and water quality of this well although it is known to be of poor quality and not suitable for drinking.  

Distribution:This non-public system serves NPS employees for day use only while on duty.  There are currently no seasonal or permanent residents living at the station.  The public is provided bottled water and the public restroom sink is signed as non-potable.

description:
This seasonal residence house is supplied water by a 1500 gallon HDPE potable water storage cistern.  The cistern is buried immediately outside the residence.  Water for the cistern is hauled from the Lovell municipal water system.  Piping is 1‚Äù HDPE. There is a foot valve in the cistern and pressure is provided by a ¬Ω h.p. shallow well jet pump located in the residence basement and is followed by a Gould‚Äôs V100 pressure tank.  There is a pre-filter and an Aquasana Whole House Filter installed downstream of the pressure tank.  Treated municipal water is hauled in a potable water hauling tank that is owned and operated by the park.  
There is a similar cistern located at the Resource Maintenance Shop located on the same property.  The cistern serves an employee break room and restroom in the shop and is located on the same property as the Flicker House.  This facility also has a roof catchment system that collects water in a separate cistern and is used for irrigation and herbicide mixing.

NOTE:
Neither of the two cisterns serving the residence and the shop are operated as potable drinking water systems.  No bacti-testing is done and no chlorine residual is maintained for the potable water hauling tank.  Bottled drinking water is provided for any employees living or working at these facilities.

fmss_system_name: OH - Ochopee Community Water Treatment Plant System
description: The Park Headquarters Water System, also known as the Everglades Shores Water System and Ochopee Water System, consists of two wells, 75 gallon hydropneumatic tank, polyphosphate injection, 1-mile transmission main, chlorine injection system, gravity tower aeration, two 6,000 gallon above ground water storage tanks, three distribution pumps, and distribution system.  Dual 3 HP submersible well pumps alternate and are controlled by a float control and solenoid valve located at the treatment plant storage tanks.  Raw water is treated with polyphosphate (Aquagold 170) to inhibit corrosion.  Additional water treatment consists of forced aeration (air stripping) for removal of hydrogen sulfide and disinfection by sodium hydrochloride.  A 22 gallon hydropneumatic tank equalizes pressure between the storage tanks and distribution pumps.  The system is operated year round and is a Community Water System (FL5110087).  This system serves the following Park facilities: Park Headquarters, maintenance building, auto shop, eight park quarters, 12 VIP camper sites, ranger station, Big Cypress Swamp Welcome Center, and a RV dump station located on Dona Drive.   Additionally non-NPS users are connected to this system, including:  Wooten's Airboat Rides, Miccosukee Indian Reservation, two private residences, and Blue Crab Cafe (Joanie's Restaurant).

description: The Headquarter Water System is a groundwater system consisting of a pumphouse with two wells, 3 mile transmission line to a treatment plant, two treated water storage tanks, and distribution system. Dual 3 HP submersible well pumps alternate and are controlled by a float control and solenoid valve located at the treatment plant storage tanks. Raw water is drawn from Well #1 or Well #2, chemically treated with polyphosphate and routed to the treatment plant via buried 4 inch PVC transmission line. Treatment consists of polyphosphate injection (prior to transmission) to inhibit calcium scale, aeration (airstripping) for removal of hydrogen sulfide, corrosion control, and disinfection by chlorine injection.  The storage tanks each have 6,000 gallon capacity and are used for aeration and contact time. A 22 gallon hydropnuematic tank equalizes pressure between the storage tanks and distribution pumps. Treated water is distributed with two 5 HP variable frequency drive pumps. This system serves 22 service connections and approximately 100 park staff daily.  Specifically, this system serves the Park Headquarters, maintenance building, auto shop, eight park housing units, 12 VIP camper sites, and two RV service stations located on Dona Drive.  An additional distribution line to Tamiami Trail serves the Park Welcome Center, Wooten's Airboat Rides, Miccosukee Indian Reservation, a private residence, Blue Crab Cafe and the SCA house.

description: The Weeks Property Water System is a groundwater well system. This system is rarely used and a potential an option for abandonment.

This system consists of a 250 foot drilled well installed in June 1965, a submersible pump rated at 10 gallons per minute, and continuous chlorination.  Treated water then flows into an underground 50,000 gallon concrete water storage tank.  The treated water is then pumped via a variable frequency drive for distribution to the Visitor Center, staff residential area, and maintenance shop.  A separate, high capacity fire pump can also pump treated water from the underground water storage tank in the case of an emergency.

description: The Adams Key Water System consists of potable and non-potable water systems that operate independently of one another.  However, cross-over piping exists to provide water from the non-potable system into the potable system if demand exceeds supply.  With cross-over piping open, salt water is processed through a reverse osmosis system before entering a 1,000 gallon above ground concrete holding tank.  From the holding tank, the RO product water is pumped into the potable water storage tanks and serves the water faucets at the two staff quarters.  No potable water is available to visitors at Adams Key.

description: The Adams Key Water System consists of potable and non-potable water systems that operate independently of one another. description: The Boca Chita Key Water System consists of dual source water intakes, a small-scale reverse osmosis (RO) system, two 2,500 above ground water storage tanks, HP distribution pump, and distribution system with a 120 gallon hydropneumatic tank in the comfort station.  The primary water source is a rainwater collection system that utilizes the roof of the Barn, which also houses the RO system and storage tanks.  In the event that rainwater is not available, water is pumped from Biscayne Bay, treated through the RO system, and enters the storage tanks.  The RO system is designed for low flow production and functions as a backup water supply.  The RO brine is released to marine waters near the Barn location.  This system is a non-potable system and does not include chlorination.  This system serves the Boca Chita Key Comfort Station, which only has toilets and no sinks.  Water is used for flushing toilets and by maintenance staff for cleaning.  No potable water is available to visitors at Boca Chita Key.

description:
This system that serves the drinking fountain and hand wash station is currently classified as a public non-community transient system.  Water is pumped from a well located in a pumphouse where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution before it enters a 250-gallon hydropneumatic tank.  An 80 gallon Mix Master chlorine contact tank was installed in 2017 to provide adequate chlorine contact time.  The water is then delivered to the contact station and drinking fountain via a 1-inch PVC water line.

The drilled well has the following characteristics: depth 340 ft, casing diameter 4 inches, sanitary seal depth 318 ft, yield 25 gpm, installed 1998, casing depth 318 ft, static water level 34.6 ft.

fmss_system_name: Water System at Visitor Center & Maintenance
description:
The City of Kountze provides water to this area through approximately seven miles of six-inch water line.  Facilities served include the headquarters and maintenance building, and the visitor center.  

A separate water service line provides water to the fire suppression system for these buildings.  The fire suppression water service line is equipped with a reduced pressure zone (RPZ) backflow protection device.  The fire suppression system consists of an at-grade 75,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank and a booster station.  The water tank inlet is equipped with a float valve, which shuts off the water into the tank when the tank is full.  

Backflow devices include the double check valve for the fire suppression system at the visitor center (no longer needed), double check valve for the fire suppression system for the maintenance complex (no longer needed), the reduced pressure zone (RPZ) valve on the City water service before the fire suppression water tank, and two reduced pressure zone (RPZ) valves for the boiler system.

description: The Peaks of Otter Water System consists of two systems that have connection piping.  The two systems are normally operated as two independent systems with the connection valve closed.  However, either of the systems is capable of providing water to all facilities located on both distribution networks.  The first water system (aka Lodge System) consists of a well with submersible  pump, two buried concrete water tanks, chlorine injection system, and distribution system.  The water is pumped from the well to the first tank (Maintenance Tank), which is 2,500 gallons, and then flows by gravity to the second tank (Lodge Tank), which is a dual chamber 150,000 gallon tank.  The Maintenance Tank serves the staff quarters and maintenance area.  The Lodge Tank uses one 75,000 gallon chamber as the primary water supply and the other chamber as an emergency backup supply.  This water system serves the Peaks of Otter area, which includes a maintenance area, ranger office, one staff quarters, Peaks of Otter Lodge, and Peaks of Otter Restaurant.

description: The main water system is fed from two wells.  The wells have been classified as a groundwater source.  After being pumped from the wells, the water is transferred through a steel pipe to an absolute 1-micron bag filter system.  After filtration, liquid chlorine is injected and the water is transferred to one of two storage tanks (320,000 gallon and 1,000,000 gallon).  From the storage tanks water is distributed to the Park facilities.
source_notes: Well No. 2. The water table is lower in the summer months in response to pumping, higher evapotranspiration rates, and less recharge from precipitation. The aquifer is generally fully recharged in the winter and spring in response to precipitation and snowmelt.

park_name: Brown v. Board Of Education National Historic Site
description:
The Operations Section at the Topeka Water Treatment Facility uses various chemical and mechanical processes to transform raw water from the Kansas River into clean and safe drinking water. The first of these treatment processes is the elevation of raw river water into the treatment basins using water intake structures. The Topeka Water Treatment Facility has two intake structures that contain six pumps each. All water intake pumps have rated daily capacities of five to ten million gallons. A durable and heavyweight, small mesh traveling screen is positioned immediate the intake pumps to prevent entry of debris that may either damage mechanical equipment or impair chemical treatment processes.

The newest plant, the East-South Plant (1999), was constructed in parallel and identical design as the East-North Plant (1981). Many infrastructure improvements were added in 1999 to 2001. At the Topeka Water Treatment Facility, there are many shapes and sizes of basins and each is designed to provide controlled contact and settling time for each specific chemical or mechanical process. Depending on their location in the treatment scheme, individual basin capacities range from thousands of gallons to nearly three million gallons. The basins in each of the three treatment plants at the Topeka Water Treatment Facility are connected in series, with each adjoining basin slightly lower in elevation than the previous. This allows the water to continuously flow by gravity from the beginning of the treatment process to the end. The average detention time for an entire process cycle though each of the three treatment plants is nearly 24 hours. The first chemical used in the water treatment process at the Topeka Water Treatment Facility is a positively charged synthetic polymer. It is flash-mixed into the raw river water before it flows into the first sedimentation basin. The polymer effectively attracts and enmeshes negatively charged turbidity particles in raw river water and causes them to settle. A large blow-down located at the center of the polymer sedimentation basin is activated at timed intervals to dispel settled and collected solids. This process reduces the amount of suspended material or turbidity significantly.

The partially processed water then flows by gravity to the disinfection contact basin. Chlorine is added at the disinfection contact basin to destroy any bacteria or viruses that may be present. Ammonia is subsequently added at a specified entry point after chlorine addition to form the secondary distribution disinfectant, chloramine. A series of baffles in the disinfection contact basin provide effective contact and reaction time to ensure that the destruction of pathogens is complete. The controlled application of chlorine and ammonia in the absence of sunlight reduces the level of disinfection by-products below regulatory requirements. These small, covered and baffled treatment units are also designed to be economically and easily retrofit as regulations progress and alternative disinfectants such as chlorine dioxide and ozone are required.

The disinfected water then flows into the coagulation, flocculation and softening basins where lime and alum are added. Lime reacts with various dissolved minerals to form primarily two insoluble precipitates: calcium carbonate and magnesium hydroxide. Additionally, the softening process enmeshes, coagulates and settles smaller remaining particles and dissolved organic substances.

Alum is added to further aid and enhance coagulation. In the two east treatment plants, previously reacted solids in specialized solid contact basins provide improved softening precipitation and efficiency of chemical application.

Clarification basins have similar collection weirs, sweeps and blow-downs. The water is then diverted to large primary clarification basins that allow additional detention time for gravity to settle out remaining precipitates and suspended materials. Large sweeps slowly rotate along the entire bottom of these basins to collect and move settled precipitates and turbidity to a central collection area. Large blow-downs located at the center of the primary clarification basins are activated at timed intervals to dispel settled and collected solids.
After the primary clarification basin, carbon dioxide is bubbled in to stabilize and lower the pH of softened water. Controlled application of soda ash and phosphate is also added at the primary recarbonation basin to increase the alkalinity level and provide corrosion control. The water then flows into a second large clarification basin. This allows additional detention time for remaining suspensions and precipitates to settle. Center sweeps and blow-downs collect and remove additional settled solids.

After water exits the secondary clarifying basin, fluoride is added to supplement levels that occur naturally. Large rectangular filtration beds are located in the enclosed area behind the doors. Cleansing of the water through specialized filtration units completes the treatment process at the Topeka Water Treatment Facility. The small amounts of suspended fine particles that remain after secondary clarification are nearly all removed by processing the water through automated filtration units. The Topeka Water Treatment Facility has nearly 10,000 square feet of mixed layer filtration units containing approximately four feet of filtrating materials. Regular cleaning of the filter beds is accomplished by repeatedly forcing clean water up through filtration beds. Processed filtered water is stored in clearwell reservoirs located beneath the filters. To ensure the filtration process is optimized, turbidity meters continuously monitor finished water quality.

The suction shafts on these pumps are as long as twenty-seven feet. The Topeka Water Treatment Facility has nearly nine million gallons of underground reservoir storage and seven high service pumps with rated capacities from five to fifteen million gallons a day. These pumps transfer water from underground clearwells into four large transmission lines. These large arterial water lines branch out into a network of more than 800 miles of water mains that transport clean and safe drinking water every day.

description:
Buffalo Point: The campground, cabins/concession area, maintenance area, contact/visitor center and residence are served by the same water source.  The source consists of a deep water well.  The water distribution system for the campground consists of a 4-inch PVC fill line from the pump house to a new 30,000-gallon above ground fiberglass water storage reservoir located above the campground.  The reservoir provides gravity pressure to the water distribution system serving all the loops of the campground.  Each loop of the campground has a comfort station and each campsite a water hookup with a vacuum breaker on every hydrant.

Additionally, the campground reservoir water is delivered by booster pump to a 100,000-gallon bolted steel reservoir located at the top of a hill. This reservoir provides water storage and gravity pressure for the distribution system serving the rest of the facilities.  The reservoir provides potable water to rental cabins, restaurant, ranger station, maintenance area, trailer dump station and residences.

description: Canyon de Chelly (CACH) National Monument operates separate water systems for potable water needs inside the Park and the Park fts fire suppression systems. otable water for use inside the Park is supplied from a 120-foot drilled well located near the southwest corner of the parking lot for the Thunderbird lodge, with a backup potable water connection provided by the NTUA. A well with a submersible pump (normal operations 35 gallons/minute) supplies water to the distribution system. From the well, water travels through a depth filter to remove iron and then a carbon filter in series. Following filtration, a sodium hypochlorite solution is injected into the water. Through a liquid level control, potable water is pumped to a 100,000-gallon buried concrete potable water storage reservoir, comprised of two, 50,000-gallon compartments that are operated in series. This gravity reservoir is located on top of a hill, east of the Park fts maintenance facility. The south potable water reservoir was built during the 1980s and was identified as the settling reservoir. The north potable water reservoir was identified as the reservoir supplying the distribution system. From the two potable water storage reservoirs, potable water is distributed to service connections within the Park including the maintenance shop, the visitor center, park headquarters, the campground, and park housing.
point_of_use: maintenance shop, the visitor center, park headquarters, the campground, and park housing

description: Potable water is supplied to the south side of the Park through an 8-inch cast iron water main from the city of Coolidge, supplied by Arizona Water, a municipal water source. This water supply passes through a meter, located in a valve vault immediately southeast of the maintenance facility wall, and then passes through a 6-inch Wilkins 950 XL reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device prior to entering the Park property. An additional Wilkins 950 XL reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device is installed on the meter bypass, located adjacent to the 6-inch Wilkins 950 XL reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device. Potable water from Arizona Water supplies the Park Visitor Center, maintenance complex, VIP RV host site connections, and the garden area. description:
This system serves a variety NPS structures, including 19 housing units, visitor center, campground, maintenance area, and ranger building/fire cache structure.  Water is supplied by the Cave Springs Well (WS004), which produces approximately 26 gallons per minute.  The well is 78 feet deep with a static water level of 11 feet (1990).  The well casing is 6" diameter PVC and is screened from 35 feet to the bottom of the well with 6" diameter PVC screen.

The sourcewater has the following water quality issues: 
-	Total dissolved solids (TDS) = 505 mg/L (Date of test: 1990). > Secondary maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 500 mg/L
-	Total dissolved solids (TDS) = 1170 mg/L (Date of test: 2002). > Secondary MCL of 500 mg/L
-	Hardness = 235 mg/L (Date of test: 1990). Very hard water.
-	Iron = 0.38 mg/L (Date of test: 1990). > Secondary MCL of 0.3 mg/L

From the well, the water is pumped to the water treatment building where potassium permanganate is injected for oxidation of the iron and two multi-media filters (Carbon, Multi-Media, and Greensand) are used to remove the iron.  The water is then chlorinated and stored in three fiberglass 20,000 gallon underground storage tanks located behind the treatment building.  The water is then pressurized using three 3 HP booster pumps and five WX -350 pressure tanks (119 gallons each) for distribution to the park.  Two 10 HP fire pumps are used for high flows.  A separate booster pump in the pumphouse is used to provide pressure to two 119 gallon pressure tanks at each campground comfort station (comfort station A & B) through a pressure sustaining valve and is controlled by pressure switches located in the pumphouse.  Backwash water from the filters is discharged into a lined evaporation pond.  The backwash water is pumped through a soaker hose located about mid-depth on the lagoon liner to promote evaporation of the backwash water.
point_of_use: 19 housing units, visitor center, campground, maintenance area, and ranger building/fire cache structure
source_notes:
Well No 7. The Needles area of Canyonlands National Park is the only area of the Park having a public water supply source.  The primary source of water for Well No. 7 is a 20-foot
 sequence of coarse sand and gravel from 31-51 feet.
aquifer_name: Alluvium and the upper part of the Cedar Mesa Sandstone Member of the Culter Formation
source_description_links: 1. PWS Report, 2. Public Sourcing - Martin, 2001 (https://irma.nps.gov/DataStore/DownloadFile/460448), Key aq report: https://irma.nps.gov/DataStore/DownloadFile/429526

description: This is a hauled water system.  The park purchases and hauls water from the city of Moab.  Potable water is supplied to the eight apartments, maintenance building, and the visitor center.  Water is stored in two 20,000 gallon underground fiberglass tanks then flows into a wet well located inside the maintenance building.  A submersible pump in the wet well is manually run to fill a 1,000 gallon plastic water tank.  Water from the 1,000 gallon plastic water tank is pumped into a 559 gallon hydropneumatic pressure tank, then filtered through a small cartridge filter and chlorinated prior to distribution.  A fire pump is also available in the pumphouse.  A hand pump is located on the older 20,000 gallon tank to get water in case of an emergency.

description: There are two water systems at Capitol Reef National Park.  The first system is an irrigation that is fed off of a diversion of the Fremont River.  This system is completely separated from the potable water system.  The other system is a potable water system fed by a solar powered well pump.  The well is approximately 80 feet deep and has a 6" casing insided of a 12" casing with the void between filled with grout.  Within the well are two pumps; the solar powered pump can provide 2-50 GPM depending upon available sunlight; the other pump is powered by a generator and can be used in emergency situations.  After leaving the well the water flows to the treatment building and into three platic 300 gallon tanks operated in parallel.  The water is then transferred to the RO skid where it is pressurized with a pump and flows through a water softener.  The water then flows through one of two 5 micron cartidge filters and carbon filters operated in parralel, then through a VFD pump into the RO filters.  After exiting the RO skid the water has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected into the water and flows into the clear well.  The clear well has a float control and when needed will pump water to the buried 100,000 gallon concrete storage tank.  From the storage tank water will flow into the Park's distribution system.

description: The UVU field station is supplied by a drilled well that has been deemed GWUDI due to a short seal on the well.  Water is received from the well to the treatment building where it flows through a sand separator into a 1000 gallon settling tank (also necessary to provide consistent flow to the filtration units).  It is then pumped through an iron-neutralizing tank with oxygen injected into the water.  From that tank if flows to a sediment collection tank to 3 cartridge filters operated in parallel through a check valve.  After the check valve, the water flows to a black polyethylene reaction tank, then through two nanofilters operated in parallel.  After the nanofilters, it is treated with a GE Homespring microfiltration unit, chlorinated and then the water flows through a check valve to a distribution pump and into four 119 gallon pressure tanks.
treatment_system_type: Sand separation, Iron neutralization, Oxygen injection, Filtration, Chlorination

The water system for Carlsbad Cavern is classified as a Public Non-Community, Non-Transient Water System (PWSID 3549408).  A single source provides water to two distinct components.  The Cave Component serves the Visitor Center, Cavern Supply Restaurant and Gift Shop, the Big Room snack bar and public restrooms, water treatment building, administrative offices, maintenance area, comfort station, and staff residential housing.  The much smaller Rattlesnake Springs Component is served with a lateral from the old pumphouse near the well head.

Water for both system components is pumped from a groundwater well located in the Rattlesnake Springs area to a 250,000-gallon water storage tank.  Water is treated with sodium hypochlorite at the well head. The 250,000-gallon tank provides contact time prior to delivery to the distribution system. Water is pumped from the 250,000-gallon water storage tank by a booster station into the water distribution system. A 315,000-gallon glass-fused-to-steel water tank, which floats on the distribution system, provides storage for this component.  A water softener and polyphosPHSte injection system installed at the treatment building has been taken off line due to problems created in the distribution system.  

Water supplied to the facilities in the Cavern Big Room passes through two (2) pressure reducing valves in parallel in the Cavern pump room that reduces pressure from 430 psi to 80 psi.  The utility lines from the surface to the Caverns are located in conduits in a vertical shaft in front of the Visitor Center.  The spaces between the conduits are filled with grout.  A short tunnel connects the bottom of the shaft to the Cavern pump room. Connections to surface utilities are located in a below-grade vault at the top of the shaft.  The vault is designed to drain to the surface.  A portable pump is available to remove water in the event of a line break.  There is an alarm system installed to signal pump failure.  A float valve has been installed in a small tank at the base of the vertical shaft so that when water is detected, a signal will be sent to the solenoid valve to stop the flow.  This prevents undetected flooding in the event of a line break when no one is on duty. There are 6 testable backflow devices in the park as follows:  VC double check valve for fire suppression system, VC RPZ for boiler, VC Cave wet well washdown RPZ, VC Cave wastewater pump packing RPZ, Booster Station double check valve for fire suppression system, and an RPZ for the irrigation system.
point_of_use: visitor center, cavern supply restaurant and gift shop, big room snack bar, public restrooms, water treatment building, administrative offices, maintenance area, comfort station, staff housing.
source_notes: Well pulls from a spring which is sourced presumably in the Slaughter Canyon Alluvium based on Martin (2011)

description:
This Public Non-Transient Non-Community System serves the Visitor Center, maintenance area, and residential area.  A second, non-potable system provides water for irrigation and flushing of toilets.  No potable water is available at the campground.

The water supply for the park is obtained from a 3,100 foot deep flowing artesian well located in the maintenance area.  The well reportedly produces 175 gpm at a residual pressure of 20 psi, and maintains a pressure of 120 psi when closed.  At high flows, the well produces particulate material which limits the practical production.  The water from the well is 95 degrees Fahrenheit and is of poor quality, with total dissolved solids in excess of 1,800 mg/L and sulfate of 1000 mg/L.

A reverse osmosis (RO) treatment unit, located in the pump house is used to treat all the potable water in the park.  Pretreatment of the water includes a greensand filter for manganese, iron, and hydrogen sulfide removal, mixed media particulate removal, softening, and cartridge filtration.   The pretreatment units were originally installed by Culligan in 1978.  The current reverse osmosis unit is made by Osmonics and was installed in 1999.  The quality of the treated water is monitored by in-line instrumentation to measure total dissolved solids and pH.  The target water quality of the plant is a pH between 6.8 to 7.2 and total dissolved solids of 250 to 300 mg/L.

The RO system is operated manually, which blends the treated RO permeate water with raw water (treated only with the greensand filter and depth filter) to ensure that the blended water is not corrosive.  The raw water feed rate to the blend tank is controlled by a gate valve that is throttled and a solenoid valve that automatically opens when the RO system is producing water.  Water from the blend tank is then chlorinated and pumped to the 50,000 gallon buried concrete water storage tank on the mesa.  The booster pump runs based on floats located in the blend tank.  The RO system is run until the potable water storage tank is full.  The water level in the tank is determined by floats in the tank that are hard wired into the control panel.

The distribution system consists of 4-inch and 6-inch pipe installed in 1973.  The 50,000 gallon concrete storage tank is lined with hypalon to prevent leakage.  Only a portion of the tank is lined, and therefore the entire volume is not utilized.

The non-potable water system is supplied by water from the well in the pumphouse prior to treatment.  The non-potable water system only supplies the toilets in the visitor center and the fire hydrants.  The non-potable water storage tank is manually filled by opening a ¬æ‚Äù gate valve in the treatment building.  The non-potable water storage tank is a 57,000 gallon glass-fused-to-steel tank.  The piping, most of which was reportedly installed prior to 1960, is made up of asbestos cement, PVC, and galvanized steel pipe.  The piping is thought to be in generally poor condition and is in need of replacement.
point_of_use: Visitor Center, maintenance area, and residential area

description: THIS WATER SYSTEM HAS NOT BEEN ACTIVE SINCE 2020. This system is classified by the state as a Minor B system. The Murray County Rural Water Company provides treated water and storage capacity for the Guy Sandy Area. The park does not treat or provide storage for this water. The water system operated seasonally from April to November. The Murray County Rural Water Company provides disinfection through chlorination of its water and maintains a chlorine residual of 0.4 mg/l to 0.6 mg/l in all parts of the distribution system. The Guy Sandy area is connected to the County water system via one service line.

Black Sulphur Spring is located near Rock Creek. Water flows into a concrete clearwell where it is chlorinated prior to being delivered to the single water hydrant next to the small pavilion. This spring has not been in service since 2009.

The Oklahoma Health Department has classified all springs in the park as Minor Class B (Non-Public Water Systems). This non-public classification requires that each system be tested for bacteriological quality a minimum of twice a year. DO 83 requires all non-public systems to be tested for bacteriological quality once per month.

The Buckhorn area consists of a campground with four loops, a RV dump station, a ranger station, boat launching facilities, picnic areas, a swimming beach, and five comfort stations.  The Point Area consists of a campground, boat launching facilities, a ranger station, two comfort stations, and an RV Dump Station.  A submersible pump located about 17 feet below the lake surface, pumps the water to a water treatment plant located on a hill north of the Buckhorn Area.  The water treatment plant is a rapid sand filter housed in a prefabricated steel building.  

The rapid sand filter consists of two above-grade steel tanks for chemical mixing, coagulation, sedimentation, anthracite filtration, and a clearwell.  A polymer is added to aid in coagulation/filtration and reduce the precursors for disinfection byproducts.  An ultrasonic level sensor located on the settling tank turns the submersible pump on and off based on the water level in the settling tank to run the water treatment plant in automatic mode.  The chlorinator and polymer chemical feed pumps are automatically turned on when the submersible pump is running.  The plant has the capacity to treat 114,000 gallons per day (gpd).  Winter production ranges from 2,000 to 5,000 gpd.  Summer production averages 10,000 to 15,000 gpd with a peak of over 25,000 gpd on July 4.

The finished water is chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite and flows by gravity to fill the 2 water storage tanks that are adjacent to the plant to a maximum height of 11.5 feet.  A transfer pump is also available in the water treatment plant to fill the water storage tanks to their overflow elevation.  One of the tanks is a 32,000 gallon glass-fused-to-steel tank and the other is a 25,000 gallon welded steel tank.  

The park also has a backup water source, as it is connected to the Murray County water system (OK2005012).  The systems are separated by a normally closed gate valve with a water meter so that the County can charge the park for any water used.  The water is supplied through a 3/4  inch diameter water meter, so flow is greatly restricted and the County water system is not capable of providing adequate water for the park during the peak water demand season (summertime).
point_of_use: campground with four loops, a RV dump station, a ranger station, boat launching facilities, picnic areas, a swimming beach, and five comfort stations

description: This non-community transient water system serves all the developed Park areas (Bonita campground, park headquarters, park housing, and visitor center) with the exception of Quarters 29. This system is served by a 116-foot drilled well, located inside the campground. This well was drilled during 1962. This drilled well delivers water at a rate of 40 gallons-per-minute. This well is cased with a 6-inch steel casing. The bottom 60 feet of this well is screed with a slotted PVC pipe, and the remainder of the hole is cased to the surface. A sanitary seal between the borehole and the conductor casing extends down 70 feet below grade. Gravel pack covers the whole length of the screen section. Water enters the treatment house through a 2-inch water line, is chlorinated using a sodium hypochlorite solution, and is sent for storage inside three, buried concrete potable water storage reservoirs. These three concrete potable water storage reservoirs have capacities of 10,000 gallons, 20,000 gallons, and 50,000 gallons and are connected in series (20,000 to 10,000 to 50,000). These potable water storage reservoirs are located on a hill above the Park fts developed area. Potable water is sent by gravity from these reservoirs to the Park fts distribution system.

It consists of water distribution piping and two 50,000-gallon redwood water storage tanks. The park hauls water to the island as needed from Ventura. The water is pumped from the boat's haul tank through a 2 ¬Ω inch PVC transmission line that is anchored to the side of the rock cliff face and into the distribution system. The water tanks float on the distribution system so that there is no dedicated fill line to the tanks. The distribution system piping consists of 2 ¬Ω inch PVC pipe. The tanks are housed in a building. Sodium hypochlorite solution is manually added to the water tanks for disinfection. A pump circulates the water in the tanks to prevent stagnation. One tank at a time feeds the system to prevent draining both tanks in case of a leak or break. Bottled water is provided to the residents for drinking.
point_of_use: 3-bedroom ranger residence, a maintenance residence, and the bunkhouse/visitor center
