water_system_name: U MSC Del Norte Water System
fmss_system_name: U MSC Del Norte Water System
description: This year-round USN water system serves the Navy site and Del Norte Ranch. It consists of a groundwater well located in the alluvial fill of the wash that empties into Prisoners Harbor, a 2700- gallon polypropylene holding tank by the well, three booster pumps, controls, a back up generator, a solar panel array mounted on a portable trailer to operate the well and booster pumps, an above ground 2 ¬Ω-inch diameter galvanized iron water line, a 50,000-gallon galvanized bolted steel water storage tank for the Del Norte Ranch, a 20,000-gallon elevated water storage tank for the Navy site, and disinfection equipment. A float switch in the holding tank controls the well pump. When the water level in the tank reaches pump-on the well pump pumps water to the holding tank. The water in the holding tank is periodically batch-chlorinated for disinfection. Three manually operated diaphragm pumps push water through the water transmission line to the Del Norte Ranch water tank and the Navy site tank. Several expansion joints are located along the line to allow for thermal expansion. The water is disinfected prior to discharging into the Navy site tank. A manually operated hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite solution into the Navy site water tank inlet line. No additional chlorination is provided at the Del Norte connection. The system can be manually operated from a remote location.
point_of_use: Navy site and Del Norte Ranch

description: This water system serves the ranger residence , bunkhouse and campground. Water is hauled from the mainland in stainless steel totes and pumped into an underground configuration of PVC piping creating a 10,000 gallon storage area.

Facilities on San Miguel Island consist of a single structure housing a ranger station, two 1-bedroom apartments, and a bunkhouse, and a visitor campground.  Water is provided only to the ranger station/residence.  The water system consists of a shallow well, a 500-gallon polyethylene water tank with submersible booster pump, a dedicated 1 ¬º inch pvc dedicated fill line to an 8,000-gallon and a 6,000-gallon buried fiberglass water storage tank, and 1 ¬º inch pvc distribution piping.  The submersible well pump is set to energize on a timer, depending on the water level in the intermediate 500-gallon tank.  When the timer calls for water, if the float switch in the 500-gallon tank is in the on (down) position, the well pump delivers water to the 500-gallon tank until either the timer or float switch de-energizes it.  When the water in the 500-gallon tank reaches booster "pump on" level, the float switch energizes the booster pump which delivers water to the fiberglass water storage tanks via a dedicated fill line.  A water meter records the volume of water delivered from the well to the water storage tanks.  The fiberglass tanks are valved such that the 8,000-gallon tank supplies sinks and the 6,000-gallon tank supplies the toilets.  Each tank has separate overflow and drain lines.  Water flows out of each tank by separate outlets to the ranger station.  Valving at the station controls from which tank water is drawn.  Water is drawn from one tank at a time.  The two outlet lines join downstream from the valves before passing through a second water meter and entering the building.

This water system serves a park staff residential area with two one-bedroom duplexes, two 2-bedroom duplexes, maintenance building, the powerhouse restrooms, the Ranch House, a private residence, the bunkhouse and visitor campgrounds. It consists of two wells, a bag filtration system comprised of a roughing filter, pre-filter, and Giardia filter, two 57,000-gallon bolted steel water storage tanks, booster pump, three 8,000-gallon buried fiberglass tanks (NPS Housing Area), and distribution system. A timer controls the main (East) well. The West well is a backup well that is seldom used. The submersible pump in the main well pumps water through the pumphouse where it passes through the filtration system and is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution. The treated water discharges into the two 57,000-gallon water tanks for storage and contact time. Chlorine is periodically added to these tanks to ensure the minimum 0.2 ppm is maintained in the distribution system. Water from these tanks is delivered to three pressure zones. Water flows by gravity to the powerhouse and ranch complex. A pressurization system consisting of a small booster pump and hydropneumatic tank serves the maintenance building. A manually operated booster pump in the main well pumphouse pumps water to the three fiberglass tanks above the staff housing area through a dedicated 2-inch PVC water transmission main. These tanks are not equipped with overflow or drain lines. Water flows by gravity to the staff housing.
point_of_use: staff residential area with two one-bedroom duplexes, two 2-bedroom duplexes, maintenance building, the powerhouse restrooms, the Ranch House, a private residence, the bunkhouse and visitor campgrounds

park_name: Little Rock Central High School National Historic Site
description:
Central Arkansas Water receives its supply from two surface water sources, Lake Maumelle in Pulaski County and Lake Winona in Saline County. Both lakes can supply water to Jackson Reservoir, a regulating reservoir located within the Little Rock city limits at Reservoir Park. Water is delivered by pipeline to the Jack H. Wilson Water Treatment Plant and Ozark Point Water Treatment Plant. Both treatment plants are located within the city limits of Little Rock.

Central Arkansas Water utilizes a conventional water treatment process at each of our two water treatment facilities. The process includes flash mixing, coagulation/flocculation, sedimentation, filtration, and disinfection.

description: This transient, non-community water system serves the entire Park including the maintenance shop, Park headquarters, the Visitor Center, Park housing, and the campground. Water is pumped from a well drilled in 2008. Water from the well enters into a treatment building, located to the East of the well. Water passes through a meter and undergoes disinfection with a sodium hypochlorite solution. Treated water leaves the treatment building and is pumped to a 50,000-gallon above ground welded steel potable storage water reservoir, located above the hill, through a 2-inch PVC line. Treated water is distributed from the potable water storage reservoir throughout the Park utilizing a 6-inch transite pipe. During 2001, a microscopic particulate analysis (MPA) was conducted and identified a low probability of this system being under the influence of surface water.
point_of_use: maintenance shop, Park headquarters, the Visitor Center, Park housing, and the campground

description: This seasonal non-community system is composed of a surface water intake from Lost Creek that flows by gravity to the water treatment building.  Filtration is accomplished with a 50-micron cartridge pre-filter, a 5-micron cartridge intermediate filter, and a strainrite Giardia/Crypto barrier bag filter system.  After continuous disinfection with sodium hypochlorite, the treated water flows to a poly storage reservoir.  It is distributed by gravity to the comfort station, and several faucets throughout the campground.  Approximately 3900 linear feet of 2" pvc piping was installed in 2012 from the storage reservoir to the campground.

This system consists of a pumped intake from Annie Spring that flows, after continuous disinfection, to two painted steel water storage tanks (100,000 gallons each) for distribution to the concessionaire operated cabins, campground, store, concessionaire employee dormitory, and staff RV park, and restaurant. The water is pumped from the well into a small treatment building where it is disinfected and then pumped to the two Mazama tanks where it is then sent to both the Mazama distribution and the Annie Springs HQ tank and to the Headquarters and Rim distribution systems.

The water is pumped to a 50,000 gallon clear well in the pumphouse, then lifted to two 12,000 gallon concrete storage tank using two booster pumps. The water is treated with sodium hypochlorite at the pumphouse prior to entering the clear well. The system serves a marina, fish cleaning station, comfort station, ranger station, and campground.

description: The water system is supplied by a solar powered drilled well. Water from the well is pumped to a small composite pressure tank through a 5 micron cartridge filter to remove sediment, has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected into the water, then flows to a 80 gallon composite chlorine contact tank.  Water then flows to a spigot in the picnic area and a spigot in the campground. The water system appears to have been a public water system (CO0326006) prior to 2005, but was reclassified as a non-public water system.

This system consists of a drilled well that that is disinfected with liquid sodium hypochlorite. The water then flows to both the distribution system and the 20,000 gallon welded steel water storage tank (Note: the tank floats on the system). The older well located inside the pumphouse reportedly had capacity issues during drought years.  In January 2016 a new well was drilled to help with the capacity issues. The old well was taken off line. This water system appears to have been a public water system (CO0326001) prior to 2011, but was reclassified as a non-public water system.  The water system serves a comfort station and a fish cleaning station.

This system is classified as a Public Non-Community Transient water system (CO032010) that serves a campground with 2 comfort stations, visitor center/ranger station, and a RV dump station. The RV dump station has been offline since 2020 due to a water leak.  This system consists of a drilled well, water treatment system, a dedicated fill line to two 10,000 gallon concrete storage tanks, and a distribution system. The well is reported to be 77 feet deep with a screen from 35 feet to 75 feet. A microscopic particulate analysis (MPA) test was completed in 2007 and did not detect any constituents found in surface water (so it is not groundwater under the influence of surface water).  The water is pumped from the well and has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected prior to the AdEdge oxidation-filtration system (APU26-1252CO-2-285) that was installed in 2021 to remove manganese.  The system utilizes AD26L media that is held in 2 composite vessels in parallel designed for a flow rate of 5 gpm.  The raw water manganese concentration is 0.68 mg/L.  The system is designed to remove the manganese below the secondary maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 0.05 mg/L.
point_of_use: campground with 2 comfort stations, visitor center/ranger station, and a RV dump station

Water is pumped into the pumphouse where it is treated with sodium hypochlorite and pressurized by a 86 gallon pressure tank for delivery to the distribution system. description: The water system is supplied by 2 active wells, Well #3 (from Bay of Chickens) and Well #4. Well #2 is no longer connected to the water system, but is available as a monitoring well. Two (2) Microscopic Particulate Analysis (MPA) tests were completed for each Well #4 and Well #3 (Bay of Chickens) in 2020.  The MPA results all scored 0 indicating low risk per the EPA Consensus Method. Water from the wells is pumped to the pumphouse, where it is chlorinated prior to flowing into a 200,000 gallon, insulated, above ground, water storage tank through a dedicated fill line. Water flows by gravity from the water storage tank to a campground, visitor center, administrative area, marina, RV dump station, fish cleaning station, and some residences.

park_name: Dayton Aviation Heritage National Historical Park
description:
The City of Dayton has two water treatment plants which treat water that is pumped from wells. Wells pump extremely hard ground water to treatment plants for softening. Hard water has natural minerals which can clog pipes and water heaters over time.

At the water treatment plants lime is reacted with minerals in water to reduce hardness. Then, carbon dioxide, fluoride and chlorine are added. Rapid sand filtration is the final step in the treatment process. After filtration, water is pumped to the distribution system. In 2012, Dayton treated and pumped 24.6 billion gallons to over 400,000 area citizens.
treatment_system_type: lime; carbon dioxide; fluoride; chlorination; filtration
climate_change_water_resources: https://static1.squarespace.com/static/602c33437336ed7a5ac5b3e6/t/62d5af4852f5bb7fd5abc700/1658239260260/OH-MunicipalCostsOfClimateChange.pdf

park_name: Dayton Aviation Heritage National Historical Park
description:
The source of WPAFB's drinking water is the Great Miami Buried Valley Aquifer. This aquifer is a large underground area of water-bearing sand and gravel deposits.

WPAFB has a current, unconditional license to operate two community public water systems supplied by this groundwater aquifer. This natural aquifer provides WPAFB with approximately 1 billion gallons of water each year.
There are six wells that serve Area A and four wells that serve Area B. Each well system has its own individual treatment process. Treatment includes air stripping to remove volatile organic chemicals (VOCs), chlorine disinfection to eliminate bacteria, and fluoridation which promotes strong teeth. Housing residents receive water which has also been softened.

description:
The source is collected in a spring box (project replaced the spring box and transmission line in fall 2015) or as ADEC indicates - a man made device for extracting raw water from river or lake or other "surface water" source.  The water flows through a system of valves from the source into a 2" HDPE pipe down a steep incline into the 2 1/2" galvanized pipe and into the treatment building.  The water is filtered with the Strain rite giardia (model #HPM99-CC-2-SR) and crypto (model # HPM99-CCX-2-SR) filters in the stainless steel housing (model # AQ2-2).  Automatic turbidity meter collects a sample, the water is metered and then continuously chlorinated.

System serves 2 residential seasonal staff and 9 non-transient staff from 5/15 to 9/17 and an additional 860 transient visitors from 6/1 to 9/15 each summer.  According to ADEC - the system has 2 unmetered service connections.  AK DEC completes Sanitary Survey on this system every 5 years, last survey was done 7/11/18, next will be in 2023.  System is required to complete one Coliform sample per month in operation according to Sample Siting Plan.  An annual nitrate is required during the hottest operational month.  Daily turbidity after filters when plant is operational and daily entry point chlorine residual is required.  System is required to submit a system startup form to DEC prior to serving water to the public -Complete start up procedures BEFORE serving water to the public & submit the form to DEC. Electronic form is located on DEC website: http://dec.alaska.gov/eh/dw/rtcr/ (due date noted is flexible based on start-up date)
 
Last Sanitary Survey corrective actions needed:  
1.)  The drain line for the hot water heat exchanger in Mechanical Room 121 does not have an appropriate air gap.
2.) The water line immediately before the strain rite final filter, in the utility building, has a drain line that does not have an adequate air gap.
3.)The 6.25% chlorine solution is made up with untreated water during start up procedure instead of potable water.
4.) There is a hole in the screen of the raw water transmission line's air release, high point, closest to the spring.
point_of_use: staff residence, day staff, and visitors

description:
Source: The source for this system is a deep well (1341 ft) that is classified as ground water. Water quality from the well is very stable. There is very little seasonal of weather related fluctuation with this aquifer. The well pump is set at 150‚Äô and very little drawdown is noted with pumping cycles. The well head is housed in a secured, small cinder-block structure located directly adjacent to the chlorine treatment building.

Treatment: Water treatment consists of a sodium hypochlorite liquid chlorination injection system that is applied in a below grade pit housed in a cinder-block construction treatment building directly adjacent to the well house. Chlorine mixing and contact time is provided in an 18' X 100‚Äô long section of pipe directly following the injection point.

Storage and Distribution: Storage consists of 2 separate underground concrete storage reservoirs known as the "upper" and "lower" tanks. The lower tank is 50,000 gallons and serves the campground, administration, maintenance and residential areas. The upper tank is a 22,000 gallon tank that serves the visitor center and ranger station. Treated water is pumped from the well house into distribution with the surplus continuing to the lower tank. The upper tank is filled via a booster pump located at the lower tank.  Chlorination is boosted at the lower tank prior to pumping to the upper tank
point_of_use: campground, administration, mainenance, and residential building

description:
This year-round system serves a 50-house park residential area, maintenance facility, elementary school, and swimming pool.  It consists of a warm water spring, a weir box, a treatment building containing a 1 micron absolute pre-filter, a reverse osmosis filtration (RO), a hypochlorinator for injection of sodium hypochlorite solution, a 350,000-gallon in ground concrete water tank, and distribution system.

Spring water flows by gravity to the weir box.  Piping and pressure reducing valves in the weir box direct the flow to either the water treatment building or to the irrigation system, depending on upstream pressure.  At the treatment building water first flows through the pre-filters followed by the RO filter.  Following the 5 micron and then 1 micron absolute filters, a portion of the water bypasses the RO filter and is used to blend with the RO treated water to reduce the corrosiveness of the filtrate.  The filtrate is chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite solution before it discharges into the water tank.  Filter concentrate and backwash water discharge to the sewer system.  The treated water then enters the distribution system to the NPS housing.
point_of_use: 50-house park residential area, maintenance facility, elementary school, and swimming pool

description: This year-round system serves a comfort station with a drinking fountain and a hose bibb, and hose bibbs in a small campground.  It consists of water from a spring that flows by gravity through about five miles of 1¬º-inch water line to a 20,000-gallon underground concrete water storage reservoir.  The water flows through a tablet hypochlorinator that uses calcium hypochlorite before entering the tank.  Water is then fed by gravity from the tank to the facilities.

description:
This year-round system serves the privately owned Furnace Creek Ranch and the Furnace Creek Inn, three NPS campgrounds (Furnace Creek, Texas Springs and Sunset) with 2,000 campsites, the NPS administrative complex, visitor center, and the Timbisha Indian Reservation.  It consists three wells, a 2-million gallon buried concrete water storage reservoir, a treatment building containing filtration and disinfection equipment, and a distribution system.  

When water in the 2-MG tanks reaches pump on level, a pump in one of the wells is called and pumps water at a rate of 325 gpm to the treatment building.  One additional well may be called depending on the water level in the water storage tank.  In the treatment building the water passes through two 1-micron filters then through reverse osmosis membrane filters.  Sodium hypochlorite solution is then injected before entering the 2-MG tank.  The 2-MG water tank provides water by gravity through a 10-inch water line to the privately owned Furnace Creek Ranch and Furnace Creek Inn,  the comfort stations and hose bibbs in the campgrounds, other park facilities, and the Timbisha Indian Reservation.  A SCADA control system allows the operator to monitor and control water treatment plant functions either at the plant or from a remote location.
point_of_use: Furnace Creek Ranch and the Furnace Creek Inn, three NPS campgrounds (Furnace Creek, Texas Springs and Sunset) with 2,000 campsites, the NPS administrative complex, visitor center, and the Timbisha Indian Reservation

description:
This year-round system serves the NPS maintenance yard, ranger station, six mobile homes, seven RV sites, and four apartments.  It consists of a spring collection box, chlorination building, and a two-compartment 100,000-gallon buried concrete water storage tank.  

Water flows by gravity from the spring box through a 6-inch AC pipe to the treatment building where the raw water first passes through a 5 micron and 1 micron filters followed by a Superior Vacufeed which injects sodium hypochlorite solution for disinfection.  The treated water then flows by gravity to the reservoirs, which provide storage and chlorine contact time.  A float valve controls flow into the tank.  It then flows into the distribution system.
point_of_use: NPS maintenance yard, ranger station, six mobile homes, seven RV sites, and four apartments

description:
This year-round system serves a 34-site campground, two comfort stations, several hose bibbs, and an RV dump station.  It consists of a spring box, clearwell, treatment building containing a solar operated submersible pump in the clearwell, 5 micron and 1 micron absolute filters, a vacuum chemical feed system for chlorine injection, a 20,000-gallon above-grade welded steel water storage tank, auto controls for system operation, and a distribution system with two comfort stations and a drinking water fountain.

Spring water flows by gravity to the clearwell under the floor of the water treatment building.  When called by the float switch in the water storage tank, the submersible solar pump is energized.  The water is pumped through the 5 micron filter followed by the one micron filter.  Chlorine is then fed by the vacuum feed system before being pumped to the water storage tank via a dedicated fill line.  Water then flows by gravity out to the distribution system.
point_of_use: 34-site campground, two comfort stations, several hose bibbs, and an RV dump station
source_location_refs: Google Earth coordinates for Mesquite Spring campground

description:
This year-round system provides water to NPS staff housing, the concession operated restaurant and grocery store, an 82 unit motel, a 50 unit trailer site, and the hose bibbs in the 200-site RV and tent campground.  It includes an 8-inch diameter 220-foot drilled well with submersible pump, a treatment plant with 5 micron and 1 micron cartridge filters, reverse osmosis filtration (RO) and sodium hypochlorite disinfection, a 20,000-gallon buried concrete water storage tank, a 65,000-gallon buried concrete water storage reservoir, and a 1,200-gallon hydropneumatic pressure tank.  

When called by the float switch in the water storage tank, the well pumps deliver water through a 5-micron prefilter and then through the RO unit.  The RO plant is manually operated.  The RO filters are backwashed manually about once every week and reject water from the plant is discharged to the sewer system.  Treated water is chlorinated, blended with 10 percent raw well water that has passed through the 5-micron prefilter and 1-micron filter, and then flows to the 20,000-gallon water tank for storage and chlorine contact time.  A booster pump delivers water from the 20,000-gallon tank to the 65,000-gallon tank during periods of high demand.  Water flows from the 65,000-gallon tank through the distribution system to all facilities.  A pressurization system consisting of a 5 hp booster pump and a 1,000-gallon hydropneumatic tank is used to increase pressure to staff housing.  A 50-hp fire booster pump is also online to provide high flows in the event of a fire.  The distribution system consists of 2 to 6-inch PVC and galvanized iron piping.
point_of_use: NPS staff housing, the concession operated restaurant and grocery store, an 82 unit motel, a 50 unit trailer site, and the hose bibbs in the 200-site RV and tent campground
source_location_refs: Google Earth coordinates for Stovepipe Wells campground
source_description_links: Well information and Key aq. Report: https://irma.nps.gov/DataStore/DownloadFile/433176

description:
This year-round system serves Scotty's Castle, 11 residences, and a comfort station.  It consists of a spring collection building, a treatment building, a 60,000-gallon above-grade concrete water storage tank and a 40,000-gallon above-grade concrete water storage tank, and a distribution system.  

Spring water flows by gravity from the spring collection building to the water treatment building where a hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite solution for disinfection.  The treated water flows into the storage tanks, which provide both storage and chlorine contact volume.  Flow into the tanks is controlled by altitude valves.  Water then flows by gravity out to the distribution system.  

-  Fluoride levels in the water (2.1 mg/l) exceed the State MCL (2.0 mg/l).  A waiver from the State requirement to treat the water for fluoride levels greater than the MCL (2.0 mg/l) has been requested.
point_of_use: Scotty's Castle, 11 residences, and a comfort station

Treatment consisted of chemical feed equipment (Stenner Model 85MHP40) for disinfection with sodium hypochlorite activated by a flow switch and contact tanks (five 120 gallon tanks and two 264 gallon tanks). This system served Smithfield Beach restrooms with drinking fountains and foot wash spigots. This was a seasonal system. Beach is located on PA side of Delaware River, river is on state lines of PA and NJ.

description: The Deerlodge water system is a Transient Non-Community Water System providing water to a ranger residence and two hose bibs near the campground.  Water is obtained from a solar powered drilled well, the water is then filtered through a 1 micron strainrite bag filter and disinfected with liquid sodium hypochlorite.  Water is then pumped to two 1,000 gallon tanks operated in series.  From the tanks water is distributed to two hose bibbs and on ranger residence with an additional pressure pump.
source_location_refs: Township Range Section Converter from Drill Log Location.

description: The Split Mountain Campground is served by a Transient Non-Community Water System.  The system serves a comfort station and a boater human waste dump station.  Water comes from a drilled well fitted with a pitless adapter.  The water  is chlorinated with liquid sodium hypochlorite and flows from the well through a 2 inch PVC water line to the 12,000 gallon water tank.  From the water tank water flows back through a 4 inch PVC line to the well house and then is split going to the comfort stations.  One of the comfort stations has not been used for several years.  The leg that serves the boater waste dumps station is pressurized by the well to a hydropneumatic tank before chemical injection and pumps to a tank with an air gap.  The line also has an RPZ for backflow.
point_of_use: Campground with a comfort station, boater human waste dump station

description: The Echo Park Water System is a seasonal Transient Non-Community Water System serving a campground, ranger residences, and river rafting trips.  Water is collected from a drilled well that is solar powered and has liquid sodium hypochlorite added to disinfect the water.  Water then flows to two 500 gallon storage tanks operated in parallel and then can flow back to the distribution system.  The chemical injection pump is controlled by a flow-switch.

description: The Quarry water system is a distribution system that is supplied by the Jensen Water Improvement District (PWSID UTAH24010).  The system serves residences, a maintenance area, administrative offices,  a visitor center and limited distribution to the Quarry building (the Quarry building is temporarily closed due to being structurally unsound). description: The Fort Jefferson Water System provides both potable and non-potable water to Fort Jefferson on Garden Key. There are two compartments in the storage tank that store non-potable water with storage capacities of 19,000 and 40,000 respectfully.  The non-potable storage is supplied by a rainwater catchment system.  After storage, the rainwater is filtered and chlorinated in the same fashion as the potable water.  The storage tanks are plumbed to allow water from the potable water compartment to be pumped to the non-potable compartments, but the reverse is not possible.  The non-potable water serves employee toilets and washing faucets as well as a wash-down faucet at the boat dock.

description: The Fort Jefferson Water System provides both potable and non-potable water to Fort Jefferson on Garden Key.  The potable water system consists of a 2-inch PVC ocean intake, dual 1.5 hp pressure pumps, cartridge filters, reverse osmosis (RO) system, buried 37,000 gallon storage tank, four 80-gallon hydropneumatic tanks (two prior to RO and two after storage), and a distribution system.  The ocean intake is located in the Fort's moat and protected by the sea wall. The raw water is pumped through a series of 20 and 5-micron cartridge filters, and two 80-gallon hydropneumatic tanks prior to arriving at the RO system.  The RO system is a package plant that includes 20 and 5-micron cartridge filters, a charcoal filter, and the RO membrane filters that have a flowrate of approximately 1.5 to 2.5 gallons per minute.  The RO brine waste is discharged to the original Fort Jefferson drainfield, which is not part of the Fort Jefferson Septic System.  The finish water is pumped to the buried storage tank where a floating tablet chlorinator is used for disinfection.  From the storage tank, water is pumped through two 80-gallon hydropneumatic tanks and into the distribution system.  The 37,000 gallon potable water storage tank makes up one compartment of a three compartment tank.
point_of_use: Potable water faucets at Fort Jefferson

description: The Loggerhead Key Water System consists of an ocean intake, pumphouse with 60-gallon hydropneumatic tank, cartridge filters, reverse osmosis (RO) system, UV disinfection, chlorine injection system, two 500-gallon water storage tanks, and a distribution system.  In 2017, erosion damage and concerns led to the ocean intake being modified to use a flexible pipe on the beach and the intake pump being moved from the Boathouse to the new pumphouse.  The raw water is pumped through a 60-gallon hydropneumatic tank and 20 and 5-micron cartridge filters prior to arriving at the RO system.  The RO system is a package plant that includes UV disinfection and has a maximum flowrate of approximately 0.5 gallons per minute.  The finish water is pumped passes through the chlorine injection point and into the plastic storage tanks, which are located in the treatment buildings.  The chlorine injection system uses a 45 gallon dosing tank, dosing pump, and liquid sodium hypochlorite. The distribution system includes a 3/4 hp distribution pump and 60-gallon hydropneumatic tank.   This system serves the 1926 House and Kitchen House.
treatment_system_type: Filtration, Reverse Osmosis, UV Disinfection, Chlorination

description: The Flamingo Water System consists of two 40-ft deep wells with dual 40 hp submersible pumps, a water treatment plant with chlorine disinfection, 550 gallon transfer tank, two 10,000 hydropneumatic tanks, a 248,000 gallon steel bolted water storage tank, and a distribution system.  The treatment plant is required as the groundwater is naturally saline. The treatment system is capable of producing finished water at 275 GPM and consists of cartridge filtration followed by reverse osmosis (RO) membrane filters, a degassifier (hydrogen sulfide removal), calcite system, and sodium hydroxide, sodium hypochlorite, and corrosion inhibitor injection systems.  On average the treatment plant processes between 20,000 and 30,000 gallons of finished water daily.  Approximately 35% of raw water becomes finished water, while the remainder is RO brine that is disposed of at the Flamingo Wastewater System.  A continuous chlorine analyzer monitors residuals in water sent to the storage tank and a SCADA system is used to monitor and operate treatment system components.  The treatment plant was built in 2005, the steel storage tank in 2004, and the RO units were replaced in 2013.  Several minor system components were replaced in 2017 after Hurricane Irma damaged the water system.  This system serves the Flamingo area, which includes the Flamingo Marina, store, employee housing, maintenance yard, Visitor Center, and campground.
point_of_use: Flamingo area, which includes the Flamingo Marina, store, employee housing, maintenance yard, Visitor Center, and campground
treatment_system_type: Filtration, Reverse Osmosis, Hydrogen Sulfide Removal, Calcite System, Chlorination

description: The Pine Island Water System consists of two wells, steel 75,000 storage tank, a water treatment plant, 1,000 gallon hydropneumatic tank, and distribution system.  The 4-log removal treatment plant includes cartridge filtration with 20, 5, and 1-micron filters, corrosion control through the injection of AquaGold 170, and a chlorine injection system.  The chlorine injection system uses a dosing tank, pump, and an NSF Standard 60 Certified liquid sodium hypochlorite.  The chlorine is injected after the steel storage tank to prevent the formation of disinfection byproducts and before the hydropneumatic tank to ensure adequate contact time prior to water entering the distribution system. description: This system serves the Visitor Center, the Learning Center, and the public restrooms outside the Learning Center. The well supplying the Visitor Center was completed during January 2013. The drilling report was available and identified it was a bedrock well (Mauch Chunk Formation aquifer) with a grout seal and six-inch steel casing to 280 feet, with a total depth of 614 feet. The report identified a production capacity of 2,880 gpd or 2 gpm. The well is located outdoors, with a sanitary well cap and submersible pump (5 gpm Goulds pump per the design plans). Treatment consists of sodium hypochlorite addition for disinfection, Aqua Mag blended phosphate corrosion inhibitor, and sequestrant for iron and manganese. Water is pumped from the well, then to treatment, and stored inside a 5,000-gallon buried storage tank located on a hilltop for gravity fed distribution. The plans identify the tank is a horizontal concrete cylindrical tank approximately 8 feet in diameter and 13 feet, 4 inches in length. A two-inch line connects the well to the tank and a four-inch line connects the tank to the distribution system. A filter and two booster pumps were installed in 2020 at the Visitor Center building and the Learning Center building.
point_of_use: Visitor Center, Learning Center, and public restrooms outside the Learning Center
treatment_system_type: Chlorination, Phosphate Corrosion Inhibitor, Iron and Manganese Sequestration

description: This public transient non-community water system serves the entire Park, including the two park residences, the maintenance area, and the visitor center. Water is pumped from a 220-foot drilled well to a treatment building where it is disinfected with a liquid sodium hypochlorite solution. Treated water is pumped off of a timer to limit water production to nighttime (though this can be manually bypassed). Additionally, there is also a throttling valve inside the treatment building to control the rate at which water is pumped and minimize drawdown. Treated water is then pumped to an 870-gallon chlorine contact tank to achieve the calculated chlorine contact time. Treated water is either sent to distribution throughout the Park or pumped uphill to a 10,000-gallon above ground welded steel potable water storage reservoir. There is no return line installed on this water distribution system.
point_of_use: two park residences, the maintenance area, and the visitor center

description: The Fossil Butte National Monument water system serves a visitor center, maintenance area, bunkhouse, and seasonal picnic area.  Water is obtained from a spring and runs through a 3" transmission line to a 500 gallon storage tank at the picnic area.  At the picnic area the water flows thorugh a 1 micron bag filter is injected with liquid sodium hypochlorite and enters a detention tank and then is dispensed from two hose bibs (with threads scored to prevent connecting hoses).  From the 500 gallon tank water can be diverted to the 20,000 gallon tank which serves the rest of the facilities.  From the 20,000 gallon tank water flows to the visitor center where it flows through a 0.2 micron katadyn ceramic filter, has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected, enters a detention tank and then is dispensed in the facility.  Water also flows from the 20,000 gallon tank to the maintenance area where the water flows through a 1 micron bag filter, has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected, enters a detention tank and then flows to the distribution system in the maintenance and housing area.  Prior to being treated in the maintenance area water flows to a fire suppression system in the maintenance area and treated water (protected by a double check backflow preventer) is connected to a fire suppression system in the housing area
point_of_use: visitor center, maintenance area, bunkhouse, seasonal picnic area

description:
The irrigation water system consists of a well, pumphouse, and water distribution system. There are several non-potable watering points located around the park to water trees and vegetation. There are two major zones of irrigation hydrants.  One zone serves the officers row area and the other serve the Historic cottonwood Grove with 12 separate hydrants.

There are several components of old potable water systems that are no longer in use, but are available for irrigation purposes.  Two wells near Building HB-1 used to supply the park with potable water before the park connected to the City fts water system. The well that was located inside the pumphouse (the pumphouse has been removed) was drilled in 1964 and a second well just outside where the pumphouse was located was drilled at a later date. A 50,000 gallon welded steel water tank constructed in 1964 is located about ¬Ω mile away from these wells. These facilities are physically disconnected from the water system.

Two wells and a pumphouse near the Church Camp used to supply the Church Camp with potable water. One well is located inside the pumphouse and another just outside the pumphouse. The Church Camp is only used for storage and the water facilities are no longer in use.

description: The Fort Raleigh Water System consists of two wells, two chlorination systems, six 50 gallon hydropneumatic tanks, 100,000 gallon above ground concrete raw water storage tank, two booster pumps, 3,000 gallon hydropneumatic tank, and distribution system.  Additionally, after the storage tank, two fire pumps are installed to provide backup pressure in case fire flow is needed.  The primary chlorination system is located prior to the storage tank and the other is used as a chlorine booster system prior to the 3,000 gallon hydropneumatic tank.  The chlorine dosing tanks have a 35 gallon capacity.  The six 50 gallon hydropneumatic tanks are typically offline and only used as backup when the storage tank is taken offline.  The system is operated year round and is a Non-Transient Non-Community Water System (NC0428415).  This system serves all facilities within the Fort Raleigh National Historic Site, which includes the Park's Headquarters, Visitor Center, maintenance area, five staff quarters, Visitor Center Comfort Station, two VIP RV pads, The Elizabethan Gardens, and Waterside Theatre, which has two comfort stations, three food concessions and various support buildings.

Three sites are served by the same municipality. The park has 3 separate water meters for each system served. The three systems are as listed:

The center has 2 restrooms; the men's restroom (1 toilet, 1 urinal) and women's restroom ( 3 stalls). Both restrooms have a shower. Outside of the building is equipped with a hose bibb. This location has a fire suppression system with backflow prevention device that is inspected annually and is maintained up to date with the inspection.
3.	The fort visitor contact station is equipment with men's restroom (3 sinks, 3 stalls, 2 urinals) and women's restroom (2 sinks, 3 stalls) and a water fountain and bottle refill station. This location has a fire suppression system with backflow prevention device that is inspected annually and is maintained up to date with the inspection.

description: Fort Sumter and Fort Moultrie National Historical Park receives potable water from the Charleston Water System. The City of Charleston obtains its water primarily from the Bushy Park Reservoir and the Edisto River, as a secondary source. The raw water is treated with powdered activated carbon for taste and odor control, then polymer, alum, and lime are added for pH adjustment and to aid in coagulation/flocculation. Sedimentation occurs before chlorine dioxide and filtration. Fluoride, ortho-phosphate, chloramine, and lime are again added before being sent to the storage tanks for distribution. This municipal water system serves Fort Sumter, Liberty Square, Huger, and supplies water to the passenger vessels that leave from Liberty Square.

description:
This water system serves all park facilities including the visitor center, staff offices and public restrooms, and the residential/maintenance area.  Water is pumped from a well in the residential area through a pumphouse where it is disinfected with a sodium hypochlorite solution.  The disinfected water is pumped to a glass-fused-to-steel bolted 56,000-gallon water storage tank via a dedicated fill line.  Water flows by gravity to all park facilities from the water storage tank via a branched distribution system.  The system consists of a dedicated 3-inch fill line from the well pumphouse to the water storage tank, and a 6-inch AC water distribution main runs from the tank to the park facilities.  A bypass line allows water to be directed to the distribution system, bypassing the storage tank.

There are 3 fire suppression systems in the park that are connected to the potable water system.  The fire suppression system for the visitor center is a wet system.  The fire suppression systems for the maintenance building and the curatorial building are dry systems that have external fire department connections.  The fire suppression system for the curatorial building and the visitor center are equipped with backflow protection devices.
point_of_use: visitor center, staff offices and public restrooms, and the residential/maintenance area

park_name: Fort Union Trading Post National Historic Site
description: Fort Union trading is connected to Dry Prairie Rural Water System.  The Park routinely tests chlorine residuals and takes a bacteriological sample.  The Parks community well system has been disconnected.

Fort Vancouver receives 100% of its drinking water from the City of Vancouver.  The Drinking water enters Fort Vancouver at five connections, one located at East Reserve Street and East 6th Street, two located at the reconstructed Fort, one located at the west end of East 5th street, and another connection located at the intersection of Fort Vancouver Way and Evergreen Boulevard.  The water enters via a 16 inch main line, flows through a double check back flow prevention device, a master meter, and then is reduced to an 8-inch main and 6-inch fire flow line.  The distribution system is comprised of a mixture of steel, lead, PVC, and asbestos concrete pipes.

water_system_name: SAHO CDW Sandy Hook Water System
description: The well was drilled in 1970 with a total depth of 900 ft (well permit #2900048826). It was located indoors and had a vertical turbine pump installation (Peerless Pump, model 10MA-2STG, 400 gpm). Treatment consisted of iron removal and disinfection. Water was pumped from the well to the aeration house where it was sprayed through aerators to oxidize and precipitate iron. Water then flowed by gravity to an underground 144,000 gallon process/contact tank where lime was added for pH control. Water was then chlorinated and pumped through sand filters. Finished water was pumped to a 350,000 gallon above ground storage tank. There were 138 residential users on the system. The system served Coast Guard buildings, NOAA buildings, New Jersey Marine Sciences Consortium, Marine Academy (MAST) High School, Sandy Hook Day Care, six beach centers with showers, bathrooms and water fountains, park offices and housing units.
point_of_use: Coast Guard buildings, NOAA buildings, New Jersey Marine Sciences Consortium, Marine Academy (MAST) High School, Sandy Hook Day Care, six beach centers with showers, bathrooms and water fountains, park offices and housing units

park_name: George Rogers Clark National Historical Park
description:
The City of Vincennes purchased what is now the municipal water utility in 1935.  Water for the City is provided by a wellfield located on the West Side of the City just south of the Wabash River.  With the largest well out of service, the capacity of the wellfield is approximately 8,000 GPM or 11.52 MGD.  Chlorine, phosphate and fluoride are added at the wellfield.  The wells pump water through an 18-inch water main into the existing distribution system.

According to information gathered in 2002, there are three water storage tanks providing a total of approximately 5,000,000 gallons of storage within the Vincennes water system.  Two of these tanks are ground storage tanks and it is estimated that only the top third of these tanks is usable storage.  Therefore, the total usable storage for the City is actually more like 2,340,000 gallons.  The Vincennes University tank is a 2,000,000 gallon elevated water storage tank with an overflow of approximately 594.5 feet above sea level.  The tank is located on the V.U. campus and was constructed in the 1950s.  The Hillcrest Tank is a 2,000,000 gallon standpipe with an overflow elevation of approximately 596.9 feet above sea level.  The usable storage of this tank is thought to be about 670,000 gallons.  This tank was constructed in 1978.  The Fox Ridge Tank is also a 2 million gallon standpipe with 670,000 of usable storage.   The tank was constructed in 1987 and provides storage to the high pressure zone of the City's water system

description: This system consists of a outdoor well with a submersible pump and a sanitary well cap. The well casing is raised above ground just over two feet with a concrete apron. There is a well log available. The well was completed February 1, 2012. It was screened in unconsolidated soil (sand and clay). It is 410 ft deep, cased to 385 ft, and grouted to 100 ft. The well is activated by an in-line pressure switch. Water storage tank levels are controlled by pressure switches. Water is called for when the tank level dropped to a preset level (94.5 ft), this activates the reverse osmosis (RO) unit (GE E4 Osmonics) and booster pump. Water is pumped from the well, passes through a meter, sand filter, two pressure tanks, water softener, silica filter, and a RO unit after which Aqua Mag blended phosphate solution is injected followed by sodium hypochlorite solution (chemical feed pumps were Stenner Model 45MHP22). The system serves the entire park: visitor center, colonial kitchen, spinning and weaving shop, craft shop, log house conference center, picnic area restrooms, maintenance building, water treatment plant and Quarters #1 and #2. The discharge from the reverse osmosis (RO) unit and the silica filter backwash on the park's water system requires a discharge permit. The NPDES permit number is VA0089982 and it is current through 9/30/2024. The permit sets discharge limits for pH and dissolved oxygen and monitoring parameters for flow and total dissolved solids. The permitted outfall is Popes Creek, and the discharge pipe is located near the water tower.

description:
The park water system provides water to all park facilities including Visitor Center, the residential area, RV dump station, the maintenance area, wilderness trail crew quarters, Lower Scorpion campground, Upper Scorpion campground, and drinking fountain at the visitor contact station at the Monument trailhead.

Water is pumped from a well at Woody's Corral through a water treatment building where it is disinfected with a sodium dichloro-s-triazinetrione solution.  The well is 100 feet deep with a 6-inch diameter casing and perforations from 25 to 80 feet.  From the treatment building the water is pumped through a 200 gallon chlorine contact tank to a 55,000-gallon bolted glass-fused-to-steel tank, via a dedicated fill line (except for the water main serving the corrals, campgrounds, and contact station).  An older 50,000 gallon welded steel tank has been taken offline, but can be manually filled for fire-fighting purposes.  The 50,000 gallon tank is equipped with an air gap on the tank fill line for backflow protection and is physically disconnected from the distribution system.  System controls are provided by a radio telemetry system.  A pressure transducer in the 55,000 gallon tank senses water pressure from the tank and energizes the well pump at low pressure (low water level) and de-energizes the pump at high pressure (high water level).  Water is delivered from the water storage tank to the park facilities via a distribution system consisting of 6-inch DI pipe.  An older well, the Middle Fork well, is physically disconnected from the water system, appears to be in a floodplain, and is unavailable in its current condition.
point_of_use: Visitor Center, the residential area, RV dump station, the maintenance area, wilderness trail crew quarters, Lower Scorpion campground, Upper Scorpion campground, and drinking fountain at the visitor contact station at the Monument trailhead.

description: The water system serves the Swiftcurrent restaurant, Campstore, cabins, concessionaire housing, campground, picnic area, ranger station and housing.  Drinking water is provided by a well classified as groundwater with a 15 hp submersible pump located next to campground entrance.   The well is 70 feet deep with 8-inch casing extending to 47 feet and 6 inch well screen below that.  The annular space is grouted with cement to 17 feet.  Well water is pumped directly to a chlorination facility where is disinfected with liquid sodium hypochlorite and then pumped to the 125,000 gallon buried concrete water storage tank.   Three surge tanks in the chlorination building to help control water hammer.  Water level in the tank is controlled by a solar operated pressure transducer that initiates well pumping cycles.
point_of_use: Swiftcurrent restaurant, Campstore, cabins, concessionaire housing, campground, picnic area, ranger station and housing

description:
The water system serves headquarters, NPS HQ Residential area, wastewater treatment facility, NPS & Concessions Horse Corrals, shops and housing, Apgar Village, Apgar Campground and the town of West Glacier.  Drinking water is provided by the Rubideau spring located approximately 2 miles west of Headquarters and is classified as groundwater. MPA analysis supports the groundwater designation. The water collection system at the spring was rehabilitated in 2004-2005. Water from the spring is collected by a buried infiltration gallery and covered collection box. During seasonally high groundwater in the spring area, surface water flows over the top of the spring box structure. The spring box is equipped with pressure relief valves in the top of the concrete structure.

Water from the spring flows by gravity under McDonald Creek to a chlorination house where it is chlorinated with liquid sodium hypochlorite and then flows by gravity to two 100,000 gallon buried concrete reservoirs. Water tank levels are controlled by valves with telemetry elevation controls.  The system also has a supplementary re-chlorination facility that is designed to monitor chlorine residuals in the storage tanks and supplement by pumping water in a closed loop between the re-chlorination facility and the reservoir tanks. The re-chlorination capacity has not been used for several years.  There is no dedicated post-treatment fill line to the water storage reservoirs. All service connections are prior to storage tanks.

water_system_name: WLD Granite Chalet Utilities Water System
description:
Source: 
A surface water system utilizing an infiltration gallery on a stream located approximately 300 yards north of the chalet.  Water is collected behind a small concrete retention structure via 4" slotted pipe buried in a gravel bed and covered by filter fabric.  Raw water is delivered approximately 150 yards to the water treatment station via gravity flow through a 2 inch HDPE line that was installed new in 2015.  There is a separate drain line for the collection basin that daylights a few yards downgrade from the basin.  

Treatment:
Treatment consists of a course sediment filter (5 micron nominal) followed by an absolute 1 micron filter.  Filters are located on a small riser of galvanized line and are not enclosed in a structure.  Water is treated in batches by attaching a food grade hose to a post-filtration hydrant and filling a 100 gallon tank and adding a premeasured volume of 12% sodium hypochlorite to reach 0.2-1.0 mg/l free chlorine.  There are two 100 gallon storage tanks on site allowing one tank to be accessed and used by the public while the other tank is being treated and allowed to reach the the required contact time prior to use.  A new 100 gallon batch is produced each day during the operational season.  The unused portion of the previous day's batch is discarded each day prior to treating the new batch.

Important treatment conditions required by the MT DEQ Permit are as follows:
-  Ensure that no chlorinated water from discarded batches reaches surface water.  Use a dechlorinating agent if necessary.
- Temperature, pH, turbidity, and free chlorine residual must be recorded daily and reported using the appropriate forms provided by MT DEQ.
- Treatment must achieve a minimum of 92 minutes of contact time.
- Chlorination treatment must be to a level that maintains a minimum of 0.2 mg/l of free chlorine AFTER the contact time has been reached.
- Locking control valves must be used and in a locked-out position on the tank that is not currently in use for supplying potable water to the public.
 - Pre-filtration and post-filtration sample taps must have the handles removed, locked-out or otherwise inaccessible  whenever NPS operators are not present in order to prevent the public from taking untreated water from these taps.
source_notes: Source of water is an infiltration gallery. For our purposes this is classified as shallow groundwater with the type being the original source, and the aquifer is the top formation for this area.

water_system_name: HBD Many Glacier Utilities Water System
description:
The water system serves the Many Glacier Hotel, concessionaire housing, and concessionaire horse corrals.  Drinking water is provided by two wells classified as groundwater.  Well #1 is located approximately 125‚Äô north of Swiftcurrent Lake and is 61 feet deep with 6-inch diameter casing and slotted well screen located 54 to 59 feet below grade.  The annular space is grouted to 17 feet below ground surface.  Well # 2 is located approximately 75 feet north of Swift Current Lake and has a 10-inch diameter casing.  Addition construction details are not available.  Well water is pumped directly to a chlorination facility where it is chlorinated with liquid sodium hypochlorite and then pumped to the water storage tanks.  Tank 1 is a 350,000 gallon buried concrete water tank and Tank 2 is a 106,000 gallon concrete reservoir with a liner. Tank 1 serves fire suppression needs and can fill Tank 2. Tank 2 serves all potable water distribution. 

An emergency fire flow system can serve the area by pumping water directly from Swiftcurrent Lake.
point_of_use: Many Glacier Hotel, concessionaire housing, and concessionaire horse corrals

water_system_name: WLD Sperry Chalet Utiltiies Water System
description: The water system serves the Sperry Chalet and kitchen.  Drinking water is provided by an infiltration gallery in a spring/creek that is supplied by glacial melt about 300 yards south of the chalet. The dam and pond at the infiltration gallery is lined with a hypalon polypropylene liner and the intake perforated pipe is embedded in gravel/sand that is covered with filter fabric. An older spring/infiltration gallery is also still connected to the system. The older spring is also supplied by glacial melt and is used as back-up source if the primary spring does not produce sufficient volume. Water from the spring flows to the treatment facility by gravity flow and is treated with cartridge filters (5 micron and 1 micron absolute) followed by chlorination.  The treated water flows into an 18,000 gallon steel water storage tank located next to the treatment building.

Source: The source for this system is a surface water intake on the creek located near the residence.  The concrete retention structure has a covered intake with control valves located at the source.  Water flows by gravity to the treatment building.  The water quality of this source is strongly influenced by glacial flour in the creek.

Treatment: Problems caused by chlorination and manganese precipitation and fouling of filters have resulted in this system being utilized for non-potable uses only in the residence.  The current treatment system consists of filtration with no disinfection.  All sinks are signed as non-potable and bottled water is provided for the residents.

Distribution: This non-public system serves one seasonal residence.  Pressure is provided by two pressure tanks located in the treatment building.

water_system_name: HBD Belly River Utilities Water System
description:
This water system is a remotely located backcountry non-public system using surface water.  It is approximately a 5 mile hike from the trailhead to the ranger station.  The system is serviced by a monthly trip from a maintenance operator. 

SOURCE:  
The water source is a small stream with a concrete intake structure constructed in the stream approximately 1000 feet east of the ranger station.  The intake structure contains 3 four-inch PVC perforated collection pipes overlain with 2 feet of gravel and sand. 

TREATMENT:
Water flows by gravity from the collection intake to a treatment facility where it is filtered with 3 stage cartridge filtration.  A 50 micron large pore cartridge filter, followed by chlorine injection, and then a 2-stage cartridge filtration bank of nominal 1 and absolute 1 micron filters. 

STORAGE: 
Storage is in a 220 gallon galvanized steel pressure tank.

DISTRIBUTION:
This system serves a ranger station, ranger residence, trail cabins, and an outside hose bib.  As a non-public system, the water is not made available to the public.

description:
The water system serves the campground and picnic area, ranger residence, and a RV dump station.  Drinking water is provided by a remotely located spring in rough terrain east of the campground.  Historically, the source was classified as groundwater under the influence of surface water.  A major rehabilitation of the collection box and raw water transmission main was completed in 2013.  Following two MPA tests in 2014, the source was re-designated as ground water.  

The spring box is constructed of galvanized steel with two 4 inch PVC schedule 40 well screen laterals.  The laterals are bedded in pea gravel and sand.  The entire spring collection area is covered with an impervious liner buried 6 inches to 1 ft subsurface.  The annular space around the circular spring box is sealed with bentonite.  There is a 6 inch drain and overflow line and the 4 inch raw water collection main with valves inside the spring box.  The 4 inch collection main flows by gravity through rough terrain to the treatment building near the campground.  High points in the collection main are fitted with air relief valves and low points have ball valves to facilitate draining.  The water is treated with a 5 micron and 1 micron absolute cartridge filters followed by chlorination and distribution. Liquid chlorination is provided via a flow driven injection pump. Chlorine contact time is provided by a section of pipe 24 inches in diameter and 140 feet long located immediately after chlorination and prior to the first service tap.
point_of_use: campground and picnic area, ranger residence, and a RV dump station

water_system_name: WLD Lake McDonald Utilities Summer Water System
description: The water system serves Lake McDonald Lodge/restaurant, Jammer Joes Restaurant, cabins, NPS and Concessions housing, the Lake McDonald Campstore, and Sprague Creek Campground.  Drinking water is provided by an 8-inch diameter steel well.  The well is 80 feet deep with a 10‚Äô section of slotted screen from 67 to 77 feet.   Well log records indicate it is grouted to a depth of 16.5 feet.  A 5 hp submersible pump delivers water to a 5,000 gallon clearwell located under the pumphouse, where it is chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite and then pumped into distribution or to the storage tanks via a 15 hp vertical turbine pump.  Storage is provided by a 100,000 gallon welded steel water storage tank and a 100,000 gallon glass-fused-to-steel bolted water storage tanks. Water flows into distribution by gravity flow.

water_system_name: HBD Logan Pass Utilities Water System
description: The water system serves a Visitor Center, office, drinking fountains, one frost free hydrant, and comfort station.  Drinking water obtained from a sand infiltration gallery located approximately 1/2 mile west of the Visitor Center in the bed of Oberlin Creek. Water is collected in a concrete collection box and flows by gravity ~200 yards to the treatment building.  The water is filtered by two sets of parallel cartridge filters of 50 and 1 micron filters. There is no electrical power available at the treatment building, so disinfection is accomplished by a Dosatron flow driven chlorine injection pump. Due to the lack of electricity, there is no opportunity for continuous monitoring equipment at the treatment building. Turbidity, pH, and chlorine residuals are all monitored with grab samples and hand held metering equipment. The treated water is stored in a 10,000 gallon welded steel water storage tank.
point_of_use: Visitor Center, office, drinking fountains, one frost free hydrant, and comfort station

description:
SOURCE:
A 39' groundwater well with six inch casing and submersible pump (11 gpm).  Static water level is 20 feet.  The drawdown of the well fluctuates significantly seasonally and is reported to be as low as 35 feet during the late season.  The well is located between the government housing area and the horse corral.  The well is slightly downgrade from the corrals and ~200-400 yards from the corrals.  The area is heavily vegetated and the well has a good history of negative bacterial testing results and nitrate testing.

Chlorinated by liquid hypochlorination injection pump located in a small building right next to the wellhead.  A 1 ¬Ω" HDPE dedicated fill line from the well to the tank and new control lines were installed during the 2002 season.

description:
SOURCE: An infiltration gallery consisting of a shallow dug well located approximately 10 feet from the edge of Bowman Creek.  The source is classified as Ground Water Under the Direct Influence of Surface Water.   The infiltration well is is a 36 inch diameter Corrugated Metal Pipe (CMP) set inside a 48 inch diameter CMP.  The void between the CMPs is filled with cement grout.  Two lids protect the top of the source from direct access.  Water is collected through perforations in the lower section of the CMP.  

A submersible pump in the well is controlled by water level floats in the storage tank.  A new 15 gpm Franklin submersible pump was installed in 2015.  Due to problems with recharge rate in the well, a Franklin Pumptec solid state pump protection system was installed at the same time.

TREATMENT: Water is pumped from the infiltration gallery to a treatment room where it is chlorinated and filtered with 5 micron and absolute 1 micron filters. Treated water is pumped to a storage tank via a dedicated fill line.   

STORAGE: Storage is an unbaffled 10,000 gallon buried fiberglass tank. Water from storage tank is distributed to service connections by gravity flow. 

DISTRIBUTION: The system serves a Ranger Station, comfort station, drinking fountain, and several seasonal residences.  The park has a special status from the primacy agency that allows the water system to be public (Transient, Non-Community) in the summer and non-public in the winter.
point_of_use: Ranger Station, comfort station, drinking fountain, and several seasonal residences

description:
Antelope Point Marina is operated and maintained by the concessioner Antelope Holdings, LLC.  The potable water at Antelope Point is pumped from a well, then to a treatment facility (treats for Arsenic, adjusts pH, and chlorinates), and 50,000 gallon storage tank located on Navajo Nation tribal land.  Most of the distribution system and water usage is located in GLCA boundaries.

In a vault underneath the ramp near the upper office area, a system is in place that boosts water pressure to the bathrooms near the offices and that treats water to the fountains near the office area. An RP assembly (Apollo 1 inch 00230 I Model 40205T2) forms a separate booster pump line to supply 60 psi to the upper bathrooms.  A separate line goes through an air gap into a holding tank that supplies water to the upper fountains.  The fountain water is recirculated through 2 Hayward (Elizabeth, NJ) High Rate Sand Filters and then through a series of 4 UV lamps (Sterilight UV Disinfection System ‚Äì R-Can Environmental, Guelph Ontario, Can.) as a disinfection step.  

Beyond the vault area the water is distributed throughout the Marina dock facilities.  

The Marina Store and Restaurant use a Hydronics System which pulls cold water from the lake and circulates it through a heat exchanger to cool a 30 to 40% glycol solution.  This cooled solution is then circulated to another heat exchange system that assists the refrigeration and A/C systems, thus reducing energy costs.  There is one potential direct cross-connection at the water/glycol tank where a potable water supply line is connected.  This connection is protected by a 1 inch Febco (825Y, J013700) RP assembly.  The supply main into the store/restaurant area is protected by a 2 inch Febco (825Y, J003780) RP assembly.  At this assembly there is a bypass line with a 1.5 inch Watts (009002, A21115) RP assembly protecting it.

Glen Canyon National Recreation Area is not responsible for operation and maintenance of this water system.
source_location_refs: Google Earth coordinates near Antelope Point Marina

description: This community water system serves the entire Bullfrog area of the Park including the marina, campground, Park housing, and the maintenance shop. Three wells serve the Bullfrog area of the Park and are identified as Well # 1 (inside the maintenance yard), Well # 2 (inside the treatment house on the hill), and Well # 3 (across from the Visitor Center). Each well is approximately 800 feet deep. Well # 1 was well drilled during 1965. The wellhead for well # 3 is located inside an enclosed structure near the split of the road to the boat ramp. Well # 3 was drilled during the early 2000s. Water from each well is pumped utilizing three Paco submersible pumps model # 4101007402. Water from each source meets inside the pumphouse and merges into one water main. Water is pumped into a small chlorination room, located outside of the pumphouse, where it is injected with chlorine gas for disinfection utilizing Wallace and Tiernan venturi-driven valves. Potable water is pumped to two, 500,000-gallon above-ground welded-steel potable water storage reservoirs for storage. Each 500,000-gallon potable water storage reservoir is operated in parallel. A telemetry system monitors the tank levels and operational status of the wells. Potable water is sent from these two reservoirs for distribution throughout the Bullfrog area of the Park.
point_of_use: Bullfrog marina, campground, Park housing, and the maintenance shop

description:
Drinking water from the Dangling Rope Water System is obtained, treated, pumped, and stored through the following equipment configuration:  (1) Two groundwater wells (only one well is used with another as backup) [1.5ft X 1.5ft concrete surface seals] with submersible pumps transfer water to the Dangling Rope Pumphouse, (2) into pumphouse through a 32 gauge mesh sediment filter --> (3) to an LMI Milton Roy (Model AA771-450B1) chlorinator using liquid chlorine‚ (3) out to storage tank [55,000 gallons], (4) back into pumphouse through a flowmeter, (5) to dual Grundfos [3hp, 30gpm, model CR-5] variable frequency drive pumps, (6) through a Neptune [2 inch] flowmeter, (7) and out to the distribution system.

Past the flowmeter the water is also plumbed into a parallel fire system pressurized by dual 40hp Marathon brand fire pumps. As of 2021, this system is inactive due a windstorm causing significant damage to the marina.

Drinking water from the Dangling Rope Water System is obtained, treated, pumped, and stored through the following equipment configuration:  (1) Two groundwater wells (only one well is used with another as backup) [1.5ft X 1.5ft concrete surface seals] with submersible pumps transfer water to the Dangling Rope Pumphouse, (2) into pumphouse through a 32 gauge mesh sediment filter --> (3) to an LMI Milton Roy (Model AA771-450B1) chlorinator using liquid chlorine‚ (3) out to storage tank [55,000 gallons], (4) back into pumphouse through a flowmeter, (5) to dual Grundfos [3hp, 30gpm, model CR-5] variable frequency drive pumps, (6) through a Neptune [2 inch] flowmeter, (7) and out to the distribution system.

Past the flowmeter the water is also plumbed into a parallel fire system pressurized by dual 40hp Marathon brand fire pumps. As of 2021, this system is inactive due a windstorm causing significant damage to the marina.

description: This transient, non-community water system serves the Hite area of Glen Canyon National Recreation Area   including the campground, staff housing, gas station and store, and maintenance facility. A 400-foot drilled well, classified as ground water under the direct influence due to the location adjacent to the Colorado River), serves this water system. Water is pumped through a submersible pump to an outdoor concrete settling basin reservoir, installed during 2011. Water exits this settling basin and is pumped into a  Pall Aria Water Treatment System, installed during 2020. After entering this system, water undergoes prefiltration through a 400-micron prefilter and a 0.1-micron membrane. Following filtration, water travels into a 4,500-gallon bright tank. Following the bright tank, water undergoes filtration through a 5-micron filter prior to reverse osmosis treatment through a GE Osmonics Muni Series Reverse Osmosis (RO) unit. Caustic soda ash is added to the RO unit to adjust the pH. Water undergoes through two stages of membrane filtration prior to treatment injection utilizing a liquid sodium hypochlorite solution. Treated water undergoes chlorine detention inside a 4,000-gallon clearwell. Once the detention time is met, potable water travels through a 3-inch water line for storage inside two, 25,000-gallon buried fiberglass potable water storage reservoirs. Potable water is sent from these two, 25,000-gallon potable water storage reservoirs for distribution throughout this area of the Park. Additionally, two, 50,000-gallon buried fiberglass potable water storage reservoirs are located in the Park housing area to provide for additional storage.
point_of_use: Hite area campground, staff housing, gas station and store, and maintenance facility

description: This transient, non-community water system serves the Lees Ferry area of the Park ‚Äì including the Park housing units, the comfort station at the boat dock, the campground, and the RV dump station. Water for this system is supplied through two intake pipes directly from intakes in the Colorado River. These two, buried intake pipes are operated by dual, 15-horsepower Baldor and Uniclosed pumps that can pump up to 50 GPM. Water drawn from the Colorado River is treated with aluminum sulfate (AlSO4) and a polymer through flash injection. This raw water travels through a mechanical flocculator, then to a tube settler, and finally a multimedia filter composed of anthracite, fine sand, course sand, and gravel. Following filtration, sodium hypochlorite solution is injected into the raw water. Potable water is treated through in an in-line ultra-violet (UV) system. Following ultra-violet treatment, potable water is stored inside a 2,5000 clear well for mixing and chlorine contact. Potable water is sent to an elevated, 150,000-gallon welded steel potable water storage reservoir for holding prior to distribution throughout the Lees Ferry area of the Park.
point_of_use: Lees Ferry housing units, comfort station at boat dock, campground, RV dump station.

description: The source for this transient, non-community water system is a 280-foot deep drilled well. Water from the well is pumped into the water treatment building through a submersible pump. Water from this well is high in sediment concentration, so it passes through a strainer prior to undergoing filtration through a 5-micron cellulose cartridge filter. Following filtration, sodium hypochlorite solution is injected through an 85 MD17 Stenner pump. After treatment, potable water is stored inside a 1,500-gallon above ground potable water storage reservoir to achieve the appropriate chlorine contact time. Potable water travels from the reservoir back into the water treatment building and is pressurized using 2 Hydropro model # V250D pressure tanks and booster pumps. Following pressurization, potable water is sent for distribution, where the line splits in two directions. A Watts 919-QT reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device is attached to the water line serving the truck filling station. The other water line sends potable water for distribution throughout the Lone Rock area of the Park.
This system is used by the maintenance shop and the mill and consists of a drilled well that flows to a PF-86 Proflo Pressure tank to a water softener and then to the distribution system.
source_notes: Multiple maintenance shops so unclear where this is located.

description: This community water system serves the Wahweap Area of the Park   including the lodge, restaurant, Park housing, maintenance facility, campground, and boat docks. Historically, the source of this water for this water system was from two drilled wells, identified as Well # 3 and Well # 4. Well # 4 is a 360-foot-deep drilled well that is currently dry. Well # 3 is a 400-foot-deep well that is online and responsible for providing water for this water system; however, testing has revealed a high arsenic content in water procured from this drilled well. Water from well # 3 is pumped through a Franklin Electric submersible pump, model # 2391088504. Water enters the water treatment building were it is treated with gaseous chlorine. Chlorine gas is injected into the water utilizing Water Solutions using pump model CL # 1021089 at a pressure over 180 PSI. Following pH adjustment with chlorine dioxide gas, arsenic is removed from the water utilizing an AdEdge Water Technologies filtration system consisting of E33 media rated for up to 425 gallons-per-minute of flow. After potable water undergoes arsenic treatment through this filtration system, it is sent for storage inside three, 500,000-gallon above ground welded-steel potable water storage reservoirs prior to distribution. Liquid level inside these reservoirs is telemetry controlled. Pumps at Wells #3 and #4 are automatically started and stopped based on water levels in the potable water storage reservoirs.
point_of_use: lodge, restaurant, Park housing, maintenance facility, campground, and boat docks

description: This system consists of a 50-foot deep, 6-inch diameter drilled well, two 5000 gallon concrete storage tanks, and water distribution piping.  Water is pumped from the well by a submersible pump via a dedicated fill line to the water tank.  A metering pump is energized with the well pump to inject sodium hypochlorite into the fill line to the tank.  Free chlorine residuals are monitored and recorded daily.

The comfort station is equipped with toilets, waterless urinals, showers, and a drinking fountain.  It is connected to the Presidio water system via a 6-inch cast iron main.  A 2-inch service line provides potable water to the facility.  A 4-inch connection provides water for a fire sprinkler system.  Each line is equipped with its own reduced pressure backflow preventer.
point_of_use: comfort station with showers, sprinkler system

description:
Two separate water systems supply the Island's facilities. One system provides potable water hauled from the City of San Francisco, and a salt water system provides non-potable water for toilet flushing.  Potable water is provided to the lower comfort station, the upper comfort station, breakroom sinks in the park administrative offices, and the restroom by the visitor entrance to the cell block.  Potable water received from the City of San Francisco is transferred to fresh water tanks on a small barge for transport to the island. Upon arrival on Alcatraz it is pumped from the barge tanks into two 500-gallon plastic holding tanks housed in the lower comfort station. Water is then supplied to the lower comfort station where it provides water for handwashing and drinking water fountains. A pressurization system consisting of two booster pumps and two pressure tanks increases pressure at the lower comfort station. A booster pump lifts the potable water to two pressure tanks located at the upper comfort station. Water is also pumped to a small holding tank at the visitor entrance, and a small booster pump pressurizes the water for use at the comfort station sink and toilet.

Pumps deliver salt water drawn from the bay to pressure tanks from which water is used for flushing toilets in both lift stations. The freshwater and saltwater distribution systems are separate.
point_of_use: two comfort stations, administrative offices, restroom by visitor entrance to cell block.
source_notes: San Francisco gets its water from the hetch hetchy regional water system
source_location_refs: Google Earth coordinates for City of San Francisco Water Department
