description: This CWS water system serves all the facilities at Fort Baker, Fort Cronkhite and Fort Barry.  It consists of a service connection to the MMWD water system, 1 2-compartment 400,000 gallon concrete water storage tank at Fort Baker, a 250,000 gallon water storage tank that feeds Fort Barry, a 2 compartment 400,000 gallon concrete tank at Fort Cronkhite, a water distribution system and booster pumps.  Booster pumps periodically feed the treated water to the Fort Barry and Fort Cronkhite water tanks.  Controls are connected to the SCADA system that monitors and controls water system.

description: This system receives water from the Marin Municipal Water District (MMWD).  The water enters the park through a backflow prevention device and flows through a 1-inch galvanized iron pipe to a 40,000-gallon steel tank.  Flow into the tank is controlled by an altitude valve.  It gravity feeds into the distribution system serving the visitor center, ranger office, maintenance yard, and two comfort stations.  Chlorine residuals are taken once per month when the bacteriological sample is taken.  Since the water provided by MMWD is disinfected, no supplemental chlorination is performed.

description: The water system consists of a 435 foot drilled well that feeds a softener.  After being softened the water is injected with liquid sodium hypochlorite. description: This seasonal transient non-community (TNC) water system serves a 34-site campground.  It consists of a spring source, disinfection with sodium hypochlorite solution, two 1800-gallon polyethylene water storage tanks, and a distribution system.  Water flows by gravity from the spring through 2-inch galvanized iron pipe to the treatment building where it is chlorinated and stored in the water storage tanks.  In 2020 a bag filtration system was installed that filters for cryptosporidium.  The water then feeds the campground by gravity through 2-inch PVC distribution piping.  The system has been determined by the State not to be under the influence of surface water.

description:
This system serves the headquarters facilities, including the maintenance compound, employee housing, the visitor center, and picnic area.  It currently consists of two springs (#2, and #3), two 50,000-gallon welded steel water storage tanks, a Rosedale bag filtration system, a hypochlorinator system for disinfection, and distribution piping.  Water from spring #3, which produces about 19 gallons per minute (gpm), flows by gravity to a treatment building where it is filtered and disinfected.  Filtration is accomplished through three banks of three filters, each progressively finer to effectively remove particles, including Giardia and Cryptosporidium.  Sodium hypochlorite is added for disinfection before being discharged into the water storage tanks.  Treated water flows first into the south water storage tank which provides chlorine contact, then into the north tank for storage before flowing by gravity into the distribution system.  Spring #3 has historically met water demand.  Spring #4 has never been put online.

There are nine backflow preventers installed at various locations.  A reduced pressure backflow preventer (RP) is on the waterline leading to the Headquarters sewage treatment lagoons.  This unit is removed before the onset of freezing weather.  A hose bibb equipped with an RP provides water for washing down the sewage grinder stations area.
point_of_use: headquarters facilities, including the maintenance compound, employee housing, the visitor center, and picnic area

description: This seasonal water system serves the Lehman Creek campground and consists of a groundwater spring source, a treatment building containing bag filtration system, sodium hypochlorite injection system, and two 1800-gallon water storage tanks.  Water flows by gravity through a 2-inch galvanized iron pipe from the spring to the treatment building where it passes through the bag filtration system.  The bag filtration system consists of a roughing filter, pre-filter and Giardia filter in series.  The water is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution before entering the water storage tanks.  The tanks are operated in series.  A float valve on the inlet line to the first tank controls the water level in the tanks.  Water is then provided to the campground water distribution system.

description: This TNC water system serves the Wheeler Peak campground and includes a spring source, a treatment building containing a Rosedale bag filtration system, hypochlorinator, and two 1800-gallon polyethylene water storage tanks, and a distribution system.  The springbox collects water from a 4-inch collection pipe.  Water flows by gravity through a 1¬Ω-inch galvanized iron pipe from the spring to the treatment building where it passes through the bag filtration system.  The bag filtration system contains a roughing filter, pre-filter, and Giardia filter in series.  The water is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution before entering the water storage tanks.  The tanks are operated in series.  A float valve on the inlet line to the first tank controls the water level in the tanks.  Water then is provided to the campground water distribution system.    A blowoff located in the line from the springbox to the treatment building relieves trapped air in the line and reduces water pressure.
source_location_refs: Google Earth for Wheeler Peak Campground

description: This Public, Community Water System provides potable water to all the facilities on the North and South rims of Grand Canyon National Park, as well as the PHSntom Ranch developed area in the Inner Canyon. The source water for the park is a natural spring (Roaring Springs), located in a cave in the Muav limestone formation on the North Rim of the canyon at an elevation of 5,200 feet. ROARING SPRINGS PUMPHOUSE:A collection box inside the cave collects water from the spring source. Two stilling basins inside the cavern remove solids, prior to sending the spring water through a series of snaking tube settlers, utilized to remove sediment from the spring water, prior to treatment inside the Roaring Springs pumphouse. Following removal of sediment, spring water is treated utilizing compressed, gaseous chlorine. Gaseous chlorine is injected into the spring water through HydroInstruments Series 900 regulators injected a pressure of 160 PSI. The SCADA system controls the injection through a vacuum valve, which must be at a higher pressure than static water pressure delivered from Roaring Springs. The chlorination system at the Roaring Springs Pumphouse consists of 8, 150-pound gas cylinders with Hydro Series 900 valves. The system automatically shifts between cylinders when empty, providing approximately four months of continuous chlorination. Following chlorination, the potable water main splits into two mains: one main is pressurized utilizing a series of three pumps to deliver potable water to the developed area of the North Rim, and another main utilizes gravity to send potable water to the Inner Canyon, and eventually the South Rim. INNER CANYON: The transcanyon pipeline delivers water from the spring source at Roaring Springs to the North Rim, Inner Canyon, and South Rim. Following treatment at the Roaring Spring pumphouse, potable water travels through an 8-inch welded steel pipe to the Inner Canyon, an elevation drop of 2,700-feet. Once the transcanyon pipeline reaches the Inner Canyon, it tees off in two separate locations to provide potable water to inner canyon facilities. The northern-most line tees off to supply the PHSntom Ranch area of the Inner Canyon, including the lodge, restaurant, employee housing, and stables. Potable water travels through a pressure reducing valve to reduce the approximate 700 PSI of static pressure caused by gravity to a 100 PSI. Another tee from the transcanyon pipeline supplies the delta area of the Inner Canyon, including the ranger station, campground, wastewater treatment plant, and boat beach hydrant. This pressure valve also reduces the static pressure caused by gravity to deliver potable water at a pressure of 100 PSI to the delta area of the Park. HAVASUPAI GARDENS: Water is gravity-fed through the Transcanyon Pipeline to Havasupai Gardens, where it is then pumped upward to the South Rim. The pressure from traveling down the canyon from Roaring Springs to the Bright Angel campground is sufficient to lift this water up 1,320 feet. Once water from Roaring Springs reaches Havasupai Gardens, it flows into a sedimentation basin equipped with a discharge and a dichlorination system to reduce the pressure in the line. At Havasupai Garden ‚Äì 3, 500-horsepower pumps (utilizing two in tandem or one pump), pressurizes water to travel upward, approximately 3,200 feet, through the borehole to reach the South Rim tank farm (6 tanks with 14 million gallons of storage capacity). Potable water is distributed to the Ranger Station, water bottle filling stations, and staff restroom facilities at Havasupai Gardens. SOUTH RIM TANK FARM: After leaving the Havasupai Garden pump station, potable water is pressurized and sent up the South Rim of the Grand Canyon to the South Rim tank farm. Pressurized, potable water is delivered into 5, welded-steel above ground potable water storage reservoirs installed in parallel. These five reservoirs have a potable water storage capacity of 14 million gallons. Potable water is distributed evenly to each of these 5 reservoirs, causing the liquid level to stabilize among the 5 reservoirs. Each potable water storage reservoir was constructed during a different era, and these reservoirs differ in size. The potable water pipeline enters into the tank farm and distributes the potable water equally to each of the five tanks, allowing the water to level equally between these reservoirs. Potable Water Storage Reservoir A = 2 million gallons Potable Water Storage Reservoir B = 2 million gallons Potable Water Storage Reservoir E = 3 million gallons Potable Water Storage Reservoir D = 3 million gallons Potable Water Storage Reservoir F = 3 million gallons Prior to the potable water leaving these reservoirs for distribution, the potable water first enters into a booster station, located on the southwest edge of the South Rim tank farm. This booster station contains two rooms: one for equipment and one for chlorine drum storage. A turbidity meter is installed on the potable water line entering the room to determine if an additional chlorine boost is necessary (mainly during spring runoff. A Pulsatron liquid sodium hypochlorite solution injection system is installed downstream from the turbidity meter. Following the injection point, the potable water line splits. The left split sends potable water to Reservoir C, located to the east of the Mather Visitor Center. This reservoir is utilized to maintain pressure in the distribution system. Two, 15-horsepower Baldor motors are installed on the right split, which pressurizes the potable water for an approximately 25-mile journey through a water main to the Desert View area of the Park. Following storage in the 14,000,000-gallon South Rim tank farm and potential chlorine injection at the booster station, potable water is sent for distribution throughout the Park. Potable water first travels to the above-ground, 1,000,000-gallon potable water storage reservoir C, a concrete reservoir located to the east of the South Rim's Mather Visitor Center. This reservoir is utilized to pressurize the potable water in the distribution system prior to traveling through the 23-mile long water main to the Desert View area of the Park. DESERT VIEW AREA: Once potable water is pressurized inside the 1,000,000-gallon above-ground, concrete potable water storage reservoir (C), it travels through an approximately 25-mile water main to reach the Desert View area of the South Rim of the Park. Following pressurized transport to the Desert View area of the Park, potable water enters a three-room chlorine booster station, located to the southwest of the developed area along an access road. Potable water enters into the East room of the booster station, is treated through a Stenner 85MPH40 chlorine injection pump, and pumped for distribution throughout the Desert View area of the Park utilizing three pumps (identified as Pump 1, Pump 2, and Pump 3). Additionally, two pumps inside the East room of the booster station (identified as Pump 4 and Pump 5) were utilized for pressurizing the fire hydrants only. Plastic, 35-gallon containers of liquid sodium hypochlorite solution were stored inside the central room of this booster station. Additionally, the West room of the booster station is utilized to house the SCADA system. WATER HAULING: There is no potable water pipeline serving the Yaki Point (South Kaibab Trailhead), the Hermits Rest, the East Entrance Station, and South Entrance Station areas of the South Rim of the Park. All potable water serving these areas of the Park is delivered through a 4,000-gallon water hauling truck. This potable water hauling truck is disinfected between uses. Yaki Point Hauled, potable water is filled into this water system utilizing a 2-inch metal pipe filling point. This filling point is locked and capped during period of non-use. A chlorine booster station is provided downstream from the filling point, allowing staff to add additional chlorine if the free chlorine residual in the hauled, potable water is less than 1.0 mg/L (PPM). Following injection, potable water is pressurized through two, Challenger Series Diaphragm tanks, model 355, 119 gallons. Potable water is distributed to the Yaki Point area of the Park (two Park housing buildings, potable water bottle filling station, and the animal trough) through two, Berkley S39520 1-horsepower pumps. Hermit's Rest All potable water for the Hermit's Rest Water System, located on the Park's South Rim, is hauled using one of the Park's water hauling vehicles and is filled into an above ground, 16,000-gallon bolted-steel potable water storage reservoir, installed during 2023. Hauled, potable water is filled into this water system utilizing a newly installed, 2-inch metal pipe filling point, located to the southeast of the reservoir. This filling point is locked and capped during periods of non-useA chlorine booster station is provided downstream from the filling point, allowing staff to add additional chlorine if the free chlorine residual in the hauled, potable water is less than 1.0 mg/L (PPM). Following injection, potable water is pressurized through two, diaphragm tanks. Potable water is distributed to the Hermit's Rest area of the Park (two Park housing buildings, Hermit's Rest Gift Shop and Snack Bar, potable water bottle filling station, and employee restrooms) through two pumps. South Entrance Station All potable water for the South Entrance Station Water System of the South Rim is hauled using one of the Park's water hauling vehicles, and is filled into an underground potable water storage reservoir. Hauled, potable water is filled into this water system utilizing a 2 ¬Ω-inch metal pipe filling point. Potable water for the south entrance station is stored inside a 2,000-gallon, buried, concrete water storage reservoir. East Entrance Station Staff stated all water for the East Entrance Station Water System, located on the South Rim, is hauled using one of the Park's water hauling vehicles and is filled into an underground potable water storage reservoir. Hauled, potable water is filled into this water system utilizing a 2 ¬Ω-inch metal pipe filling point. Potable water for the south entrance station is stored inside a 2,000-gallon, buried, concrete water storage reservoir. NORTH RIM: Following chlorination at the Roaring Springs Pumphouse, pumps force potable water through a six-inch aluminum water line to the developed area of the North Rim. Two, Pitot Tube pumps (Baldor 200hp, ‚ÄúSuper E‚Äù) force water up 3000 vertical feet of elevation and 12,500 lineal feet of steel pipe to the North Rim tank farm. Potable water is stored inside two, 2,000,000-gallon welded steel potable water storage reservoirs identified as the north reservoir and the south reservoir. During months when the Park is open for visitation, stored potable water flows into a chlorine booster station, located to the south of the tank farm. This booster station is equipped with chlorine pumps to increase the residual of potable water delivered to the developed area of the North Rim. From these storage reservoirs, potable water is fed to the North Rim Pumphouse where pressure is boosted from 30 PSI to 60 PSI using 3 VFD pumps (Baldor, 1.5 horsepower). There is also a 25 horsepower (US Motor) Fire Pump in service at this pumphouse as well to supply the fire suppression systems on the North Rim developed area. A chlorination room is located to the side of the pumphouse which is used in the winter months to boost chlorine levels in potable water storage reservoirs using liquid sodium hypochlorite. This chlorination system is not used in the summer months as water is freshly chlorinated at the Roaring Springs pumphouse. Following storage, potable water is sent for distribution throughout the North Rim area of the Park. This Public, Community Water System provides potable water to all the facilities on the North and South rims of Grand Canyon National Park, as well as the PHSntom Ranch developed area in the Inner Canyon. The source water for the park is a natural spring (Roaring Springs), located in a cave in the Muav limestone formation on the North Rim of the canyon at an elevation of 5,200 feet.
point_of_use: All facilities on north and south rims.

description: The Tuweep water system supplies the ranger station and is not available for visitor use. It is a rainwater collection system consisting of a galvanized sheet metal collector, two buried concrete storage tanks, three cartridge filters in series, and a 500 gallon tank for contact time. The operator must batch chlorinate the 500 gallon tank. The tank is not insulated and is subject to freezing in the winter. During the summer of 2010, the Tuweep Ranger decided to bring in bottled drinking water, and use the Tuweep ranger station water system for non-potable uses only and bypass the filters and CT tank.

water_system_name: South Rim Reclaimed Non-Potable Water System
description: Grand Canyon National Park has produced reclaim water since the 1920's. It was originally used as water for steam locomotives. The modern reclaim water system routes wastewater plant effluent through two coagulation/flocculation tanks, a clarifying/settling tank, and two mixed media sand/gravel filters. A gas chlorination system adds 2ppm chlorine to the water before it feeds into a 150,000-gallon clear well located under the reclaim building. Reclaim water is pumped to two reclaim tanks, one located near the school (150,000 gallon) and one located near CVIP (125,000 gallon). The reclaim water is used primarily for flushing toilets and irrigating grassy areas in the park. When the reclaim water demand is less than the plant effluent flow, the reclaim water is routed through two 500 gallon sodium metabisulfite dechlorination tanks, through an effluent flow meter, and to three lagoons out on Raw Well Road. These lagoons were originally built in the 1930's for wastewater treatment, but taken out of service after building the wastewater treatment plant. Water held in the lagoons is currently used for fire protection purposes. After the lagoons are full a valve located after the effluent flow meter can be turned to route water to an outfall that discharges into a small drywash. This water runs approximately 1 mile down the drywash before completely percolating into the ground.
source_location_refs: Coordniates for the Roaring Springs Pumphouse were used since water is reclaimed from that system.

description: The LeConte Water System consists three capped springs, 3,500 gallon raw water fiberglass holding tank fitted with two pumps, hydraulic ram, cartridge filtration, two 7,500 above-grade fiberglass storage tanks, manual chlorination process, and distribution system. The water from the holding tank can be transferred to the storage tanks via a hydraulic ram, solar pump, and backup gas powered pump. Prior to entering the first storage tank, water passes through 5-micron and 1-micron cartridge water filters. Water is batched pumped to the storage tanks and calcium hypochlorite is manually added in the first storage tank. This system is operated seasonally from March through November and is a Transient Non-Community Water System (TN0001268). This system serves LaConte Lodge facilities, which includes a restaurant, crew showers, restrooms with flush toilets, and three yard hydrants (two for guests and one at the NPS staff quarters.

description:
The 4-Lazy-F property was connected to the Moose/Beaver Creek water system.  Therefore, the old water system is INACTIVE.

The old water system consisted of a well located in a pit directly outside the lodge and a large bolted steel pressure tank in the basement that appears to penetrate the wall of the basement.  It is assumed that the lines serving the cabins are connected to the pressure tank in the ground outside the main lodge as there is no evidence of connections in the basement.  Water lines appear to be old galvanized steel lines.  There is some newer copper line that can be seen in the basement of the main lodge.

The caretaker cabin system has an outside well with a pressure tank and hot water heater that are both located in a small basement/crawl space area inside the house.

No longer in use.  This house was demolished.  The house was located off the Moose-Wilson road and used for seasonal housing.  The house was located on a bench above the Snake River.  The water source was a well classified as groundwater.
db_comments: No Evidence of this water source was found in NPS IRMA records

description: No longer used.  This was a NON-PUBLIC remotely located ranch that has recently been donated to the park.  There are several (at least 3) cabins that serve as seasonal lodging for NPS employees.  The source is a creek/spring collection system that gathers surface water from a small creek uphill from the cabins.  Water is initially collected into a small covered trough via small pipes that are shallow buried and inserted into small collection areas that are assisted by plastic dams in the creek.  Following the initial collection, water is piped into an old "spring house" type facility with several thousand gallons of holding capacity.  From the spring house, water flows by gravity into distribution to the cabins.  Pipes appear to be mainly old galvanized water pipes.

Climber's Ranch was connected to the Moose/Beaver Creek water system.  Therefore, the old Climber's Ranch Water System is now INACTIVE.

The old Climber's Ranch water system consisted of a well, pumphouse, and two small pressure tanks.
source_notes: Water is replenishd by a diversion ditch nearby.

description: Treatment: Liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected in a small chlorine treatment building located near the wellhead. description:
The water system has been completely replaced in late 2007 and 2008.  An old hand dug shallow well that was under the influence of surface water was replaced with a new well and storage reservoir.  

Source:  Groundwater well drilled in 2007.  Well information from a data sheet in the chlorine treatment room states well is 195 ft deep with an 8" casing from 0-60 ft and a 6" casing from 2-187 ft.  A .060 screen is located at 185-195 ft.  Well pump is 1 hp/ 35 gpm set at 160 ft.  

Treatment:  Well water is pumped to a treatment building that houses the chlorination equipment.  Chlorination is liquid sodium hypochlorite.  The well pump is controlled by floats in the reservoir and the system is connected to the parkwide SCADA monitoring system.  There is a continuous chlorine analyzer with digital readout in the treatment building.  

Storage:  After disinfection the treated water is pumped to two buried 50,000 gallon fiberglass storage tanks via a dedicated fill line.

Distribution:  An 8" fire main line supplies a fire pump and fire hydrants.  A 4" distribution main supplies domestic use.  Two jockey pumps and a pressure tank maintain pressure in the domestic distribution system.  The distribution main downstream of the pressure pumps is a 6" line.
This home located at the end of Bobcat Lane is used as a seasonal residence.  The water source is a well located at the southeast corner of the house.

description:
The system consists of the 2 wells, pumphouse, 500,000 gallon buried concrete water storage tank, and a water distribution system.  The system serves the Signal Mountain Lodge, Cabins, Campground and convenience store.  The campground is concessionaire operated.  NPS operators maintain operation of the water and wastewater systems.

The water system is supplied by two groundwater wells.  Signal Mountain Well #1 was drilled in 1991.  The total depth of the well is 260 feet with static water level approximately 235 feet below grade.  Signal Mountain Well #2 was drilled in 2011.  The total depth of well is 325 feet, with a static water level of 243 ft (in 2011) and 420 field perforations in the casing between 285 and 325 feet.

description: The Rudd House is located in Antelope Flats and consists of a main house with a smaller second building located oat end of Craighead Lane.  The house is used as a seasonal residence.  The water system consists of a drilled well in the basement of the house, submersible pump, and pressure tank.

Drinking water for this water system is supplied by two groundwater wells that are located directly adjacent to the treatment building.  Water is pumped directly from the wells to the treatment building constructed in 2018 where it is chlorinated with liquid sodium hypochlorite.  Sodium bicarbonate can be added for alkalinity control due to historic lead and copper problems on the system, but the water is only slightly corrosive and the old plumbing has been replaced in all of the residences, so the sodium bicarbonate is not currently used.  There is a back-up generator located next to the treatment building.  Following chlorination, the water is pumped to a new 300,000 gallon buried concrete water tank constructed in 2018.  The water system serves Moose and Beaver Creek.

The water source is from 3 different wells classified as groundwater.  The wells are located near the pumphouse and produce adequate volume to meet the water storage and demand needs of the two systems served.  The wells supply water to 2 different major locations (Colter Bay and Jackson Lake Lodge). 

The pumphouse was constructed in 2018.

Following chlorination, the treated water is delivered via dedicated fill lines to the Colter Bay and Jackson Lake Lodge 500,000 gallon buried concrete water tanks.  This system is system has two separate PSWID#'s assigned by EPA, one for Colter Bay (WY5680095) and one for Jackson Lake Lodge (WY5680103).  Well #2 is currently being pumped to Colter Bay and Wells #4 and #5 are being pumped to Jackson Lake Lodge.
point_of_use: Colter Bay and Jackson Lake Lodge

description: The Signal Mountain lagoon system has a well and pressure tank that are used for washdown water of wastewater components.  A single hose bibb is present on the blower building for operators to use.  The system is not chlorinated for monitored for bacteriological contamination or chemical constituents.

The AMK ranch is a University of Wyoming research center where they house researchers seasonally.  The water system serves the Lawrence House, Johnson Lodge, Berol Lodge, Sunroom Cabin, and Director's Cabin.  The NPS operates and maintains the water system, which was installed in 2019 to replace antiquated water facilities.  This public water system is supplied by 2 drilled wells, Well #6 and Well #7.  Both wells were drilled in 2019.   

Water from the wells is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite in a combined pumphouse/booster station building.  The chlorinated water flows into an above ground 35,000 gallon concrete water storage tank that is attached to the pumphouse/booster station building.  Water is then pressurized in the booster station with 2 EA 3 HP booster pumps and a 454 gallon pressure tank.  2 EA 15 HP fire pumps are also located in the booster station to provide fire flow.  The water distribution system consists of 6-inch diameter HDPE water main.  

The old wells (Berol Well and Boise Well) are disconnected from the potable water system, but still used for irrigation.  The Johnson Well was abandoned in 2019.
point_of_use: Lawrence house, Johnson Lodge, Berol Lodge, Sunroom Carbin, Director's Cabin

description: The park acquired the Moulton Cabins in 2019 from a private inholding.  The water system provides water to the main house, bathhouse, and a kitchenette.  The 8 cabins in this location have no water service, as they use the shared bathhouse and kitchenette.  The water system consists of a drilled well with 2 EA WellXtrol WX451C (158 gallons/each) bladder type pressure tanks located in the basement of the main house. The main house has an antifreeze type wet fire suppression system with a reduced pressure zone (RPZ) backflow prevention device that serves the main house, grand cabin, bunk house, and cabin 3.  The water system also has an irrigation system with a double check valve backflow protection device.

description: The Fort Pickens Water System consists of two water wells, two ground level water storage tanks, chlorine disinfection, distribution system, and two automatic flushing hydrants.  The two wells extend to an estimated depth of 150 ft.  Well No. 1 was original to the system (year built is unknown), while Well No. 2 was built in 2007 as part of a larger rehabilitation project needed after the system was damaged in Hurricane Ivan in 2004.  The 2007 project also included a new water plant, water tank, replacement of some distribution lines, the removal of a gas chlorination system, and installation of a liquid hypochlorite chlorination system.  Water sampling has shown that Well No. 2 has elevated levels of naturally occurring ammonia.  Additionally, both wells have elevated levels of natural organic matter (NOM).  The two wells are used in rotation.  The chlorination system is located within the water treatment plant and includes two 100 gallon chlorine tanks and dosing pumps.  The two ground level water storage tanks are located next to the wells and have 8,000 and 10,000 gallon capacities.  After the storage tanks, the water is pumped into the 6-inch distribution main that ends at the Ranger Station near the park entrance.  The system serves the facilities in the vicinity of Fort Pickens on the Florida side of the Park.  The facilities served include staff housing, the Fort Pickens Visitor Center, three comfort stations, day use picnic areas, Mine Store, Ranger Station, and two campgrounds.
point_of_use: Staff housing, the Fort Pickens Visitor Center, three comfort stations, day use picnic areas, Mine Store, Ranger Station, and two campgrounds

The West Ship Island Water System is a seasonal system operational between March and October, which consists of a single well, chlorine disinfection, three 119 gallon hydropneumatic tanks, two 340 gallon contact tanks, and distribution system.  The well production is metered and logged.  The chlorine disinfection system uses a 20 gallon dosing tank with pump and liquid sodium hypochlorite.  The contact tanks were installed to provide a minimum of 30 minutes of contact time of chlorination.  The water serves the Ship Island Snack Bar, Ranger Office, North and South Comfort Stations that include showers, Ranger Duplex, beach showers, various fire suppression tanks, and the Bunkhouse/First Aid facility.

description:
This water system is classified as a Public Non-Community Transient system (PWSID#: 0550015).  The facilities served by this system are a visitor contact station located at the mouth of the canyon which includes a public restroom and drinking fountain.

The source for this system is 78 foot deep well located about 500 feet north of the contact station.  The well has a pitless unit, concrete slab, and metal box used as a well cover.  Water is pumped at about 16 gpm from the well to the treatment room in the comfort station where it is passes through two cartridge filters, one 20 micron filter to remove particulates and a 0.5 micron carbon filter to remove taste and odor.  The water is then chlorinated by a hypochlorinator before being pumped into  two 300 gallon water storage tanks.  These tanks provide contact time for disinfection.  A small booster pump pressurizes two hydropneumatic tanks.  The water is distributed to the restroom facilities and drinking fountains.
point_of_use: contact station, public restroom, drinking fountain

description:
This system is classified as a non-public water system.  The facilities served by this system includes a large house (Ship-of-the-Desert) with a separate apartment at one end and a small adjacent house known as the servant fts quarters.

The source for this system is a 155-foot drilled well located approximately one-half mile northwest of the house at the mouth of Lamar Canyon.  Water pumped from the well, is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution and treated with a roughing filter and an activated charcoal filter.  

The water then flows by gravity to a 6,000-gallon galvanized steel reservoir located between the well and the residences.  The overflow from the potable water tank flows into two open stone tanks used for water storage in the event of fire fighting activities.  Water from the potable tank is then distributed to the house via 2-inch galvanized iron pipe.  There is a water softener and reverse osmosis treatment system installed at the facility that is not used.

description:
This water system is classified as a Public Community water system (PWSID #55014).  The system provides potable water to the Visitor Center and administrative offices, the maintenance area and residential area for park employees (consisting of 12 houses, four duplex units, two apartment units, and a recreation center), a campground and comfort station located at Pine Springs about one mile north of the Visitor Center, and the ranch house, horse corral, and storage buildings at Frijole Spring.  The system also provides water to the Texas Highway Department that has six houses, maintenance building, and an office located adjacent to the park maintenance area.

The water source for this system is the Glover Well Number 13, a 2,572-foot, 8-inch diameter drilled well located in the center of the parking area in front of the Pine Springs comfort station.  The pumping rate for the well is about 52 gpm.  The well is cased with 8-inch steel casing to 2,502 feet, and open hole to the bottom with the pump is set at approximately 2,480 feet. The well pump is operated manually to fill the reservoir.  A sensor in the reservoir automatically de-energizes the pump.  An emergency power generator is available to power the system in the event of a power outage.

The water is pumped from the well through a treatment room in the comfort station.  In the treatment room the water is first filtered through a multimedia filter (charcoal and sand/gravel) to remove iron bacteria and other particulates, as well as taste and odor caused by VOC fts from the raw well water.  The water is then injected with Aqua Mag, an iron sequestering chemical, and sodium hypochlorite for disinfection.  The filter is manually backwashed with the backwash water being discharged into the comfort station septic system.  

After treatment, the water then enters a 218,000-gallon, two-compartment, below-grade concrete reservoir approximately 1,000 feet up the Canyon.  Water flows out of the reservoir through a distribution system consisting largely of 8 and 6-inch PVC water main.  A booster pump located in the comfort station pressurizes water for use in the comfort station.  A pressure reducing valve (PRV) located by the highway reduces the water pressure to the maintenance area  and a second PRV reduces the pressure again to residences located to the east of the maintenance building.
point_of_use: visitor center, adminstrative building, maintenance area, 12 houses, 4 duplex units, two apartments, recreation center, Texas Highway Department (6 houses, maintenance building, office)

description: This system is classified as a Public Non-Community Transient water system (PWSID #550017).  The facilities served by this system are a campground, visitor contact station, comfort station, horse barn, a residence, and a duplex.  Water is pumped at a rate of 13 gpm from a 2,500 foot drilled well located north of the horse barn.  The water is pumped through a treatment building where it is chlorinated using sodium hypochlrorite, and then up the hill to a 54,565 gallon glass-fused-to-steel bolted water storage tank.  A pressure transducer located on the plumbing tree in the Pumphouse senses the water level in the tank an initiates well pumping cycles based on the water tank level setpoints on the control panel.  The well pump controls are currently set to start the pump when the tank level is at 15 feet and the pump is set to run for 360 minutes when started.
point_of_use: campground, visitor contact station, comfort station, horse barn, a residence, and a duplex

water_system_name: HALE-Historic Water System - Summit Area (Hosmer, HQ, Above HQ)
description: This year-round system serves the Headquarters building, government quarters, Hosmer Grove Campground, resource management, entrance station, and maintenance area. It consists of rainwater catchment, two 250,000-gallon above-grade welded steel raw water storage tanks, two slow sand filters, a proportional flow hypochlorinator, a 50,000-gallon buried concrete water storage tank for treated water, and distribution system piping consisting of 6-inch and 4-inch ductile iron pipe and PVC pipe. Water from the catchment system flows by gravity to the two raw water tanks, which in turn gravity feed the two slow sand filters. Float valves in the sand filters control flow to the filters. The water then flows by gravity to the underground concrete storage tank. The hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite solution before entering the concrete tank. A float valve in the concrete tank controls flow into the tank. The tank provides both storage and chlorine contact time before the water enters the distribution system.

Headquarters building, government quarters, Hosmer Grove
Campground, resource management, entrance station, and maintenance area

description: The water system serves the visitor center and park offices, comfort station, and maintenance area. It includes a 6-inch diameter 300-foot deep drilled well with two submersible pumps, photovoltaic pumping system, a peristaltic pump hypochlorinator, two 7500-gallon bolted steel storage tanks, one 70,000-gallon bolted steel storage tank, and distribution system piping and appurtenances. The well has two pumps. One pump is solar-powered and operates only when sunlight is available. A second generator-powered pump can be used as backup. A float switch in the large storage tank automatically operates the well pumps. A float valve in the other tank controls water level in that tank. The water storage tanks provide storage and chlorine contact time. Monthly bacteriological testing for total coliform is done.

description: The water serves all visitor and staff facilities at the Rim including the VC, Volcano House hotel and restaurant, administrative offices, maintenance area, resource management area, Thurston Lava Tube, Hawaii Volcanoes Observatory, USGS building, Jagger Museum, and  Namakani campground.  The system has facilities at the rainshed area and HVO.  The facilities at the rainshed consists of two hypalon lined catchment ponds, roof rainwater catchment, five raw water storage tanks (2x750,000-gallon, 3x500,000-gallon), water treatment facilities (chemical injection for pH adjustment, three slow sand filters, clearwell, disinfection equipment), two finished water tanks, and a distribution pressurization system used to increase pressure within the distribution piping as well as a fire pump to provide additional flow during high demand events.  Facilities at HVO include two water storage tanks (80,000-gallon, 20,000-gallon).  Rainwater collected in the catchment ponds and roof collectors is treated to raise the pH before a booster pump delivers the water to the raw water storage tanks.  The untreated water then flows by gravity to the treatment facilities where it passes through the slow sand filters and is collected in the clearwell.  The filtered water is then disinfected with sodium hypochlorite before being pumped to the finished water tanks.  Two booster pumps pressurize the water from the tanks into the distribution system piping where it flows to the Rim facilities, resources management, and Thurston Lava Tube.  Booster pumps within HVO pressurize the water for the buildings at HVO.  A small pump and hydropneumatic tank pressurize the water for use at Thurston Lava Tube comfort station.
point_of_use: visitor and staff facilities at the Rim including the VC, Volcano House hotel and restaurant, administrative offices, maintenance area, resource management area, Thurston Lava Tube, Hawaii Volcanoes Observatory, USGS building, Jagger Museum, and  Namakani campground

water_system_name: Kahuku Family & Dorm Residence Water Tank
description:
The Kahuku Ranch is located about 40 miles west of the main park entrance.  This non-public water system serves two residences and several out-buildings.  It consists of a connection to the County water system, a 1,000-gallon polyethylene water tank, a booster pump, a 2500-gallon polyethylene water tank, and 1¬Ω HDPE distribution piping.  Water from the county system gravity flows into the 1,000-gallon poly tank.  Due to periodic low pressures at the connection, this tank is needed to ensure an adequate water supply.  A pressure switch energizes the booster pump to pump the water from the 1,000-gallon poly tank to the 2500-gallon poly tank located on a hill above the ranch buildings via a dedicated fill line.  Water gravity flows to the ranch buildings.  A stock trough is also connected to the line from the steel tank to the residences.  The County water is chlorinated so no supplemental disinfection is needed at the ranch connection.

Accomplishments/Comments

description: The water system consists of a well, chlorine disinfection, and 50,000-gallon concrete storage tank. The older 14,000-gallon storage tank was not in use but is still located at the well site. The site was secured with a fend around the perimeter. The well head was located outdoors with a submersible pump. The well was equipped with a sanitary well cap and the casing was raised above 18 inches. The well had a sloped concrete apron. A pressure gauge was connected to the well casing and measured 0. After the water was pumped from the well, it enters the pump house and the pressure tank. The system has a chlorine injection point in the main line. The chlorine solution tank was 5 gallons. The system included chemical feed pumps and a back-up day tank. The sodium hypochlorite solution used was NSF 60. The system is required to perform 4-log disinfection by the PA Department of Environmental Protection. There was no chlorine contact tank but the water line length to the first user was over 1000ft. The State receives monthly total coliform results and monthly logs of the free chlorine residual for each day. Site ID for PA.GOV website: 607947 The water flowed from the well to the maintenance, to the VC, to the barn.

description: This cold water spring is considered ground water under the influence of surface water and therefore, must meet the new surface water treatment rules administered by the State of Arkansas and the Environmental Protection Agency.  The system utilize a combination of ozone and direct filtration for treatment.  The system disinfects using ultraviolet light.  The system dispenses water at a fountain located near the start and end of the mountain tower road.
source_location_refs: Google Earth Coordinates for Happy Hollow Fountain
aquifer_name: Arkansas Novaculite, Bigfork Chert, and Hot Springs Sandstone

description:
Hot water is collected from the 27 geothermal wells on Hot Springs Mountain and stored in four separate water storage reservoirs with total storage of 473,000 gallons.  This hot water is distributed to Park buildings, Bathhouses, hotels, and four jug foutains.  The state requires a grade two water operator. 

The thermal water system serves as the primary water source and is the heart of Hot SpringsNational Park. However, the severe deterioration of the plumbing system that directs the thermal water throughout the park is compromised due to age and environmental factors. This aged infrastructure can lead to leaks, stagnant water, and contamination points that negatively impact water quality and place public health at risk, whether for those consuming cooled thermal water or utilizing the bathhouse water features. With an average of 2.5 million visitors per year ranging from toddlers to the elderly, who come to experience the water, it is essential to replace the thermal water system infrastructure. Doing so can mitigate many risk factors that could lead to negative health outcomes. Therefore, the proposed projects for replacement and renovation are highly recommended and critical for protecting the health and safety of both the public visitors and NPS staff.

This aged infrastructure can lead to leaks, stagnant water, and contamination points that negatively impact water quality and place public health at risk, whether for those consuming cooled thermal water or utilizing the bathhouse water features. With an average of 2.5 million visitors per year ranging from toddlers to the elderly, who come to experience the water, it is essential to replace the thermal water system infrastructure. Doing so can mitigate many risk factors that could lead to negative health outcomes. Therefore, the proposed projects for replacement and renovation are highly recommended and critical for protecting the health and safety of both the public visitors and NPS staff.

description: This Public Transient Non-Community System serves the visitor center that has restrooms and a drinking fountain, a 31-site campground that has a single restroom and yard hydrant, and a residence area.  This public water system serves all of the facilities in the park.  The water system consists of a 1437 foot deep water well which produces water at approximately 10 gallons per minute.  The water is disinfected with liquid sodium hypochlorite at the well house before it is pumped to a 56,868 gallon ground level glass-fused-to-steel water storage tank.  Six point-of-use reverse osmosis filters are installed at the residence and the VC to remove arsenic for staff.  Arsenic levels fluctuate and have been above the maximum contaminant level (MCL) of 10 ppb.  However, the most recent 8/22/2017 results were only 9.85 ppb.  Arsenic levels above the MCL is not a violation since the water system is classified as a transient non-community water system.
point_of_use: 31-site campground with a single restroom, hydrant, and a residence area

park_name: Harry S Truman National Historic Site
description:
The city of Independence supplies water to about 250,000 people, including residents of Independence and 11 wholesale customers.  The water is supplied from 41 wells located at the Courtney Bend Water Treatment Plant.  The wells receive water from the Missouri River Alluvial Aquifer, classified as a ground water source.  The Well water is softened and disinfected at the treatment plant.

Independence, MO Water services NPS operated facilities at the park including the Truman Home, Visitor Center (firehouse), George Wallace, Frank Wallace, and the Noland homes.

description: The water system is connected to city water and has a backflow preventer that requires annual testing. Presumably, "city water" refers to the nearest municipal water supply in Michigan City. Michigan City receives is drinking water directly from Lake Michigan. It is treated through a conventional treatment process that includes Flocculation-Sedimentation (the mixing of Alum into the water to create a "Floc" which allows large particulate matter to settle out of the water) and Filtration to remove fine particulate matter and micro-organisms. Chemical additions including Chlorine (for bacteriological removal), Alum (to remove large particulate matter), Fluoride (to prevent decay), and Chloramines (mix of chlorine and ammonia which allows for longer disinfectant levels in the distribution system and removes chlorine odor from the water). Polyphosphate is added to inhibit internal corrosion of water mains and water services.
source_notes: Michigan City, IN gets its water from Lake Michigan

water_system_name: Ranger III and Park HQ in Town
description: The Ranger III water system consists of a water storage tank, pressure tank, and booster pumps.  The water is taken at the park HQ in Houghton, MI.  The water there comes from the CITY OF HOUGHTON water system.  The City did not have any violations in 2012.

The microfiltration water system replaced the older Katydyn ceramic filter system.  The old system took water directly from Lake Superior through 10 micron bag filters that were periodically back-washed during the treatment process and then through a set of ceramic filters coated with diatomaceous earth.  This older system had extensive maintenance demands and struggled at times with the new EPA/State of Michigan surface water treatment rules.  The current microfiltration plant utilizes a barrier design consisting of 0.2 micron filter modules.  The system automatically backwashes itself and the maintenance demands are minimal.  The microfiltration system filtered water requires post chlorination only and that water is pumped to a chlorine contact tank near the pump house.  From there the water is pumped to a standpipe and again pumped to a elevated storage reservoir.  Potable water is distributed by gravity to the NPS headquarters area that consists of housing, apartments, dorms, administrative offices, recreational facilities and maintenance facilities.  

The operator of the treatment process is certified by the state and does an excellent job of ensuring the provision of safe drinking water to the staff located at Mott Island.  Treated water turbidity is 0.03 NTU or less.
point_of_use: Headquarters area that consists of housing, apartments, dorms, administrative offices, recreational facilities and maintenance facilities

description:
The water system at Rock Harbor has a conventional water treatment plant to treat water directly from Lake Superior.  The treatment process includes pre-chlorination and a chemical addition of alum that is rapid mixed to disperse the alum.  The water is allowed to flocculate.  The water is then filtered; post chlorinated and pumped to two ground-level bolted steel reservoirs.  A booster pump delivers the water to a new bolted steel standpipe located on a high ridge above the water treatment plant.  The water is then distributed by gravity to the following concession operations: 10 duplex housekeeping units, 58 lodge units located in four buildings, dormitory, dining room and snack bar, store, guest house, marina, public laundry, restrooms and showers.  Also water service is provided to the following NPS facilities: district ranger house, ranger office, seasonal employee house, maintenance office/break area, campground and utility service area.
  
Fire protection for the area is provided in a separate system that utilizes Lake Superior water.  There is no connection between the potable water system and the fire protection system.

The water treatment plant is located in the utility service area.  The water treatment plant houses a certified water test lab.  The lab performs routine water tests for the plant including bacteriological analysis.  This lab conducts all the water quality testing for the park's public and non-public water systems.  

The water treatment plant is well operated producing water with a turbidity of 0.1 NTU.  The chlorine levels are checked daily, the chlorine concentration is adequate to achieve proper CT values and all required reports are being sent to the state.  The laboratory certification from Michigan expires in 2012.
point_of_use: 10 duplex housekeeping units, 58 lodge units located in four buildings, dormitory, dining room and snack bar, store, guest house, marina, public laundry, restrooms and showers

park_name: James A. Garfield National Historic Site
description: Water for the Lake Shore Division   Lake County comes from Lake Erie. For the purpose of source water assessments, in Ohio, all surface waters are considered to be susceptible to contamination. By their nature, surface waters are accessible and can be readily contaminated by chemicals and pathogens with relatively short travel times from source to intake. Although Aqua's surface water intake is located offshore in Lake Erie, the proximity of several onshore sources increases the susceptibility of the source water to contamination. The Lake Shore Division   Lake County system's drinking water source protection area is susceptible to contamination from municipal wastewater treatment discharges, runoff from residential, agricultural and urban areas, oil and gas production and transportation and accidents, releases and spills from vehicular traffic as well as from commercial shipping operations and recreational boating. Aqua treats the water to meet drinking water quality standards, but no single treatment technique can address all potential contaminants. The potential for water quality impacts can be further decreased by implementing measures to protect Lake Erie. More detailed information is provided in Aqua's Lake Shore Division   Lake County water system Drinking Water Assessment report, which can be obtained by calling Aqua at 877.987.2782.

description: The City of St. Louis Water Division maintains two water treatment plants that draw water from the area's two main rivers. water_system_name: Visitor Center / Unger House Municipal Water System
description:
The water for the visitor center and Unger House is supplied by the municipal system (Highland Sewer and Water Authority). The municipal water service line supplies the Unger House first and then the visitor center. The water entering the visitor center fills a 150-gallon water tank with an automatic fill control float. The water is then pumped and pressurized and serves the drinking fountain, bathroom handwash sink, and mop sink in the janitor's closet. 

Prior to the entering the visitor center, the service line also splits off and fills two, 15,000-gallon fiberglass storge tanks on top of the hill, which are used for the fire suppression system in both the visitor center and Unger House and the toilets and boiler system in the visitor center (this configuration is new since 2021: prior to that the 15,000-gallon tanks were used for potable water storage).
point_of_use: Unger House, Visitor Center, fand the drinking fountain, bathroom handwash skink, and mop sink in the janitor's closet. It also serves the fire suppression system in the Visitor Center and Unger House

description:
This non-public system serves the maintenance building facilities including an employee restroom, a break room, shower, and several hose bibbs.  The system includes a 45-foot deep, 6-inch diameter drilled well with submersible pump and 20-gallon hydropneumatic tank housed in the pumphouse (for hose bibs only).  Inside the maintenance building there is the following equipment: a water softener, carbon filtration, an additional pressure tank (for potable water in the building), a holding tank.  There system currently is not treated and posted "Non-Potable" at all points of distribution.

Water is pumped from the well by the submersible pump through the treatment building before entering the maintenance building.  The storage tank provides storage volume and chlorine contact time, if needed.  The submersible pump is controlled by a pressure switch located adjacent to the pressure tanks.  When system pressure reaches 30 psi, the well pump energizes, pumping water at 7 gallons per minute.  The pump de-energizes when system pressure reaches 50 psi.
point_of_use: maintenance facility with restroom, break room, shower, and hose bibs.
source_location_refs: Coordinates for the Maintenance Facilty used according to Martin (2009)

description: This year-round system serves a maintenance shop, visitor center, seasonal quarters, ranger quarters, fire dorm, and a campground with three comfort stations.  It consists of a pumphouse containing two booster pumps and a hypochlorinator, a 74,000-gallon fiberglass-lined, bolted steel water storage tank, and distribution system.  The water system is connected to the High Desert Water District municipal system.  Water flows into the pumphouse where the booster pumps pressurize the distribution system.  The pumping rate for each pump is about 60 gpm.  Sodium hypochlorite solution can be added before it leaves the pumphouse to boost chlorine residual of the incoming water.  It flows into the distribution system and to the water storage tank.  There is no dedicated fill line to the water storage tank.  The booster pumps are manually operated to fill the water tank.  The automatic timer control is not used.  At pump-off level, a signal is sent from the water tank to the well pump to de-energize.  The distribution system consists of 4-inch AC and 6-inch PVC water main.  There is an RV dump station at the campground.  The campground sites are served with frost-free hydrants located at various points throughout the campground.
point_of_use: maintenance shop, visitor center, seasonal quarters, ranger quarters, fire dorm, and a campground with three comfort stations

description: This year-round Transient Non-Community (TNC) system serves the NPS maintenance yard, visitor center, seasonal quarters (3 houses and 2 duplexes), and a campground with four comfort stations.  It consists of a drilled well with a solar-powered submersible pump, a pumphouse containing a hypochlorinator and back-up generator, a 100,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank, and a distribution system.  The well, installed in November 1999, is 405 feet deep, has a 6-5/8 inch diameter PVC casing and a sanitary seal to 100 feet, and yields 26 gpm.  The pump is a 5 hp submersible solar-powered pump set at 375 feet below ground surface with pumping rate of 26 gpm at the system dynamic head.  The SCADA control system, installed in 2001, is located in the maintenance building.  It was designed to monitor and control pumping conditions, water storage tank water level, etc. from a central location.  It currently is not operational.  The system is operated  manually.  When the water level reaches 8 feet, the operator will energize the well pump, which also energizes the hypochlorinator to inject sodium hypochlorite solution.  The water is then pumped through about three miles of 4-inch AC pipe to the main distribution system.  This transmission line provides adequate contact time for chlorine disinfection.  A 6-inch pvc water line was installed from the visitor center to the water storage tank.  The well pump operates under solar power when available, and uses the back-up propane fueled generator when necessary.  The distribution system consists of 3-inch AC and 6-inch PVC water main.  There is an RV dump station at the campground.
point_of_use: maintenance yard, visitor center, seasonal quarters (3 houses and 2 duplexes, campground with four comfort stations.

description: Kennesaw Mountain National Battlefield Cheatham Hill receives water from Marietta Power and Water Company. This serves one water fountain.

This system serves: Calumet (also serves Village of Calumet, Calumet
Township, Village of Hubbell, Village of Laurium). 

Michigan American Water is supplied by ground water from four wells. The company has been utilizing these wells since 1968. The water from this well supply is of excellent quality requiring only minimal treatment. The State performed an assessment of our source water in 2003 to determine the susceptibility or the relative potential of contamination. The susceptibility rating is on a seven-tiered scale from "very-low" to "very-high" based primarily on geologic sensitivity, water chemistry and contaminant sources. The susceptibility of our source is moderate.  This system covers all of the Calumet facilities.

description: This public transient non-community seasonal system uses ground water from 2 hard rock wells located next to the treatment building to provide water to three campgrounds, a lodge, employee housing and the maintenance area. Water is stored in an 85,000-gallon and a 150,000-gallon steel tanks.

description: A public community system that is capable of receiving ground water from three sources (Artesian Well and 400ft Well). Artesian well has both a suction pipe (1 1/2" at 35') and a half horsepower pump hung at 20'. When not artesianing, well is pumped. Pump also primes the suction line when needed. The primary source is an artesian well. Sodium hypochloride is used for disinfection, and continuous monitoring is in place.  This system also utilizes a lime injection system for corrosion control due to elevated copper levels. This system collects 5 bacteriological samples per month of operation. Water is stored in a 1.2 million gallon concrete aboveground storage tank.

description: The Headquarters Water System consists of two wells, a 64,500 gallon water storage tank, chlorination system, recirculation pump, and PVC distribution system.  The system provides potable water to the park's headquarters, visitor center, maintenance buildings, learning center, and employee quarters.  Well No. 1 is approximately 240 feet deep (age not documented) and located within a treatment room in the Maintenance Building.  Well No. 2 is approximately 700 feet deep, installed in 2012, produces about 70 gal/min, and is located near Battleground Drive.  Well No. 2 pumps water through the Well No. 1 treatment room where chlorine injection with sodium hypochlorite occurs prior to water storage.  An additional treatment building is located at the storage tank site, which includes an automatic recirculation pump and chlorine injection system.  The recirculation pump and chlorination system are designed to ensure proper water quality within the storage tank prior to entering the distribution network.  An in-line chlorine analyzer and data logger are also installed.  All system pumps are controlled with set points and include an emergency dial-out alarm in case of failure.  The water storage tank was built in 2012
point_of_use: Park's headquarters, visitor center, maintenance buildings, learning center, and employee quarters

description: This water system serves the visitor center (constructed 2003), two campground comfort stations, VIP trailer area, park administrative buildings, maintenance compound, and staff housing areas (six houses, one duplex, eight apartments).  It consists of a 823 foot drilled well (constructed in 2007), an 80 gpm submersible pump, continuous chlorination, and a 100,000-gallon underground concrete water storage reservoir. A 25 horsepower submersible well pump pumps water through the pumphouse where disinfection with liquid sodium hypochlorite occurs.  The treated water flows to the water storage tank via a 4-inch ductile iron dedicated fill line.   The treated water flows by gravity via 8 and 6-inch ductile iron pipes to the park facilities. This system collects 2 bacteriological samples per month of operation.

description:
This water system serves a 300-site trailer village, Hemenway Harbor, Lake Mead Marina (755 slips), Las Vegas Marina at Hemenway Harbor(635 slips), an 89-site campground, a picnic area with two comfort stations, ranger station, administrative offices, Lake Mead Lodge motel with administrative offices, 110-passenger tour vessel with on-board restaurant, marina snack bar and administrative offices, staff housing, 15 VIP sites, RV dump stations, dry boat storage, native plant nursery, FHA and construction contractor trailers/offices, interagency boat dock, fish cleaning stations, fire station, rescue quarters, and maintenance facility. It consists of a connection to the Boulder City lateral from the Southern Nevada water system, a 300K gallon concrete water storage tank, and distribution piping. Water flows into the water storage tank through a service connection to the Southern Nevada water system. The connection is equipped with a reduced pressure backflow preventer that is maintained by the City. An altitude valve that opens and closes to allow a four-foot drawdown in the tank controls water level in the tank. 
The water service connection to the Las Vegas Boat Harbor at Hemenway consists of a 3-inch pvc line that runs below grade to the top of the boat ramp where it transitions to a covered concrete channel that runs the length of the ramp. At lake level the pvc line transitions to a hose that joins the line to the water line that runs under the dock to the marina. The Desert Princess dock is connected to the Boulder City water system.

description: The water system serves a 3-loop RV campground and dump station, five comfort stations, marina snack bar, administrative offices, yacht club, trailer park, NPS staff and concessions employee housing, maintenance building, fire station, and a fish cleaning station. It consists of a raw water intake in Lake Mead, two intake pumps and water transmission line, a 500,000-gallon concrete raw water storage tank, a water treatment plant, a 100,000-gallon welded steel and a 300,000-gallon welded steel finished water storage tank, and a distribution system consisting of 6-inch and 8-inch PVC pipe. Raw water is pumped from the lake by the intake pump located on a floating barge to the raw water storage tank. A pressure transducer in the raw water tank controls this pump. Water flows by gravity to the treatment plant. Water treatment consists of filtration and disinfection and a disinfection byproduct {TTHM) removal system. Turbidity is continually monitored in the raw water and both turbidity and chlorine residual are continually monitored in the product water. The raw water is first pumped through a microfiltration system where particles down to 0.2 microns are removed. The filtered water discharges to a clearwell. It is then pumped through a 5-micron pre-filter followed by a nanofiltration system. The nanofilter is currently not in operation. The filtration system produces 181 gpm of treated water. The filtered water is disinfected using chlorine before being discharged to the final clearwell. This clearwell provides contact time for disinfection. Backwash water can either be discharged to a drainage ditch outside the treatment building (as permitted by NDEP), or it can be diverted to the sanitary sewer. The treated water is then pumped to the finished water storage tanks via a dedicated fill line before entering the distribution system.

description: The water system serves an RV campground, trailer village, marina store, trailer park, two campgrounds each with two comfort stations, ranger station, administrative offices, NPS employee housing, and NPS maintenance shop. It consists of a raw water intake in Lake Mead, intake pumps and water transmission line, a raw water storage tank, a water treatment plant, a 500,000-gallon above-grade concrete treated water storage tank, and a 2-compartment 145,000-gallon concrete water storage tank (only compartment 2 being used). Two intake pumps located on a floating barge pump water from the lake to the raw water storage tank. This tank has three separate compartments. One compartment is used for irrigation storage. Water in the other two compartments is raw water. A pressure transducer in the raw water tank controls the intake pump. Water flows by gravity to the treatment plant. Water treatment consists of filtration and disinfection. The raw water is first pumped through a microfiltration system, which removes particles down to 0.2 microns. The filtered water discharges to a clearwell. It is then pumped through a 5-micron pre-filter followed by a nanofiltration system. The nanofilter is currently not in operation. The filtration system produces 180 gpm of treated water. The filtered water is disinfected using chlorine before being discharged to the final clearwell, which provides contact time for disinfection. The treated water is then pumped to the water storage tank via a dedicated fill line. The backwash water is discharged to a lined pit outside the water treatment plant. The pit removes any solids and dissipates energy before the clarified water is overflows to the ground (as permitted by NDEP). The distribution system consists of two pressure zones. A booster pump (2) delivers water from the 500,000-gallon treated water storage tank to the 145,000-gallon water storage tank above the 120-site RV park and provides water to the RV park.

description: The water system serves the marina (824 slips), marina store, motel, restaurant, a fish cleaning station, ranger station, maintenance yard, NPS and concessions staff housing, boat repair shop and storage yard, trailer village and campground. It consists of a raw water intake in Lake Mojave, two intake pumps and water transmission line, a water treatment plant, a 500,000-gallon above-grade welded steel water storage tank (A-tank out of service) and a 500,000-gallon above-grade concrete water tank, and distribution system. The intake pumps located on a floating barge pump draw water from the lake and pump it to the treatment plant. The plant contains four clearwells located below the plant floor, a microfiltration system, a nanofiltration system, chlorine disinfection system. Before treatment, Acid Magic is added to lower the pH {~8.3 to 7.3) due to the mussels and a caustic is added after to raise the pH. One clearwell is used for irrigation storage, and one provides raw water to the microfiltration unit. The other compartments are treated for potable use. Water treatment consists of filtration and disinfection. The raw water is pumped through a microfiltration system that removes particles down to 0.2 microns. The filtered water discharges to a clearwell. The system is then designed to pump the water through a 1 micron pre-filter followed by a nanofiltration system. However, the nanofilter is currently not in operation since the microfilter treated water meets current MCLs. The filtration system produces about 450,000 gallons per day, or about 330 gpm, of treated water. The filtered water is disinfected chlorine solution before being discharged to the final clearwell, which provides contact time for disinfection. The treated water is then pumped to the water storage tanks via a dedicated fill line. The backwash water can be used for irrigation or discharged to the sanitary sewer. This discharge is permitted under NPDES. The distribution system consists of a 10-inch pvc pipe from the water treatment plant to the water storage tanks, and 4, 6, and 8-inch PVC pipe serving the remaining facilities. A radio telemetry (SCADA) system signals water level in the storage tanks to the treatment plant to fill the tanks. Turbidity is continually monitored in the raw water, and both turbidity and chlorine residual are continually monitored in the product water.
point_of_use: marina (824 slips), marina store, motel, restaurant, a fish cleaning station, ranger station, maintenance yard, NPS and concessions staff housing, boat repair shop and storage yard, trailer village and campground.

description: The water system serves a 42-space RV campground, marina store and restrooms, employee housing, trailer park and a fish cleaning station. It consists of a raw water intake in Lake Mead, two intake pumps and water transmission line, a concrete above grade raw water storage tank, a water treatment plant, a 150,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank and a 150,000-gallon concrete finished water storage tank, and a distribution system consisting of 6-inch and 8-inch PVC pipe. Raw water is pumped from the lake by 2 intake pumps located on a floating barge to the raw water storage tank. A pressure transducer in the raw water tank controls these pumps. Water flows by gravity to the treatment plant. Water treatment consists of filtration and disinfection. The raw water is first pumped through a microfiltration system where particles down to 0.2 microns are removed. The filtered water discharges to a clearwell. It is then pumped through a 5-micron pre-filter followed by a nanofiltration system. The nanofilters are not in operation. The filtration system produces 181 gpm of treated water. The filtered water is disinfected using calcium hypochlorite before being discharged to the final clearwell. This clearwell provides contact time for disinfection. Backwash water is discharged (as permitted by NDEP) to a drainage ditch outside the treatment building. The treated water is then pumped to a 150,000-gallon above grade concrete water storage tank and a 150,000-gallon above grade welded steel water storage tank via a dedicated fill line before entering the distribution system.

description: The water system serves the marina (95 slips existing, 695 authorized), marina store, restaurant and bar, overnight cabins, administrative offices, commercial ice making facility, a fish cleaning station, ranger station, maintenance yard, campground, RV dump station and concessioner and NPS staff housing. The water system consists of three active wells, arsenic removal, disinfection equipment, a 100,000-gallon above-grade welded steel water storage tank and a 500,000-gallon above-grade welded steel water storage tank, and distribution system. Well #3 is used as the backup source and pumps at a rate of about 35 gpm, which is diminishing due to dropping lake level. Well #4 is the primary source and consistently produces about 170 gpm. The discharge lines of all three wells join into a dedicated fill line to the water storage tanks to ensure adequate chlorine contact time. The well pumps are operated using a timer. All chlorintation is now conducted at the plant. A new well #6 was installed in 2019 near well #4.  All three wells are being used on a rotating basis.

description: The water system serves the marina, marina store, a fish cleaning station, ranger station, maintenance yard, NPS staff housing, concession staff housing, and the USFWS fish hatchery and housing. It consists of two wells, chlorine disinfection, reverse osmosis membranes for arsenic and nitrate removal, 2 x 100,000-gallon buried concrete water storage tanks, and a distribution system. New wellheads and fencing installed in 2019.

description: The Fritch Fortress distribution system is considered part of the Sanford Yake public water system by the state of Texas.  This system serves an RV dump station, comfort station, and the amphitheater.  The Fritch Fortress water system is connected to the City of Fritch Water System.  The point of connection is housed in a concrete building.  The plumbing tree includes a pressure reducer, which reduces the incoming pressure from 100 psi to 60 psi, and a hypochlorinator.  This system is used only during the peak season and is shut down the rest of the time.  The system is flushed and tested for bacteriological contamination before the season begins.  The City of Fritch Water Department chlorinates the water before it is delivered to Fritch Fortress.  Supplemental chlorination facilities are provided at the point of connection but are seldom used.  Adequate volume and pressure for the Fritch Fortress system is provided by the City of Fritch Water department, therefore an existing 100,000-gallon elevated water storage tank is not in service.  It has been completely disconnected from the water system.
point_of_use: RV dump station, comfort station, and the amphitheater

description:
This Public Non-Community Transient serves an RV dump station, Marina Store and comfort station, Marina boat pump-out station, Sanford Yake comfort station, and Cedar Canyon comfort station. Water is supplied from the Fritch Municipal Water Supply (TX1170004).  The municipal system disinfects the water before entering the Sanford Yake storage and distribution system.  Water is distributed from the water storage tank to the RV dump station, Sanford Yake comfort station, Cedar Canyon comfort station, Marina store and comfort station, and the maintenance/ranger complex.

The park has the capability of providing additional disinfection, but does not routinely do so.  Storage for the Sanford Yake distribution system consists of a 100,000-gallon elevated water storage tank.  Separate inlet and outlet pipes are provided to assure circulation throughout the tank and to provide adequate contact time for disinfection.

description: This year-round TNC class A system includes a primary component, and a secondary or backup component (two wells-#1 and #2). The primary system is a spring that flows by gravity to a lined 180,000-gallon concrete reservoir enclosed in an historic building. All water from the spring and both wells is chlorinated (finished) by the time it gets to the reservoir.  A flexible tent-like cover was installed over the top of the reservoir and the edges were sealed to prevent infestation (bats/rodents). Water is stored in 2 ‚Äì 22,500-gallon storage tanks and flows by gravity to (1) park administrative/maintenance facility, (2) visitor center, (3) several NPS seasonal and permanent quarters, (4) large campground with comfort stations and hose bibs scattered throughout, (5) day-use swimming beach area, (6) fish cleaning station. There are two drilled wells, submersible pumps, and continuous disinfection.  Water from well pumps #1 or #2 can go anywhere there is a demand at the time.  If the park is heavily irrigating and both pumps are on, water may flow to that area instead of going to the reservoir, making the contact time is in the lines.  If the irrigation is shut off and there is no water demand, then the water will flow to the reservoir and towers. The lines that go to the reservoir and the towers are all connected and double as fill lines to the reservoir and towers and distribution lines.

A high service pump lifts water from the chlorine contact tank to 3 hydropneumatic pressure tanks and then through the distribution system to the concessionaires houseboat dock, maintenance facility, store and deli, and to the Park fts trailer dump station. In 2020, the park added 1500 feet of 6-inch pipe to include the Keller Ferry campground the comfort station, four hose bibs throughout the campground, a seasonal NPS quarters, the concession operated boat docks/long-term moorage, and a fish cleaning station.
point_of_use: concessionaires houseboat dock, maintenance facility, store and deli, Park fts trailer dump station, Keller Ferry campground, comfort station, four hose bibs, seasonal NPS quarters, moorage concessions, fish cleaning station
