description: This year round non-public system is a surface source that provides water for two dorms for NPS personnel, kitchen, and fire cache.  It consists of an unprotected spring source, a concrete water storage tank, disinfection equipment, and distribution piping.  Water flows by gravity from the spring through the treatment building where it is chlorinated and into the concrete water storage tank.  Before the water is treated, a line branches off to feed three private residential water tanks of various sizes and construction.  The treated water then flows to the dorms where it is used for non-potable purposes.  Bottled water is provided for drinking.  A point of use RO filter is also provided at the kitchen sink for cooking and warewashing.
point_of_use: two dorms for NPS personnel, kitchen, and fire cache

description: This seasonal system serves a 3-bedroom ranger residence and consists of a 330-ft deep drilled well, a pumphouse containing disinfection equipment, a 1,200-gallon elevated water storage tank, and distribution piping.  A propane generator provides power to the well pump and chlorinator equipment.  The system is manually operated.   Water pumped from the well is first disinfected then discharges via a dedicated fill line into a 1,200-gallon elevated storage tank.  Water is then fed by gravity to the ranger station.

This system serves an 86-site campground and ranger station.  Facilities include a uptake structure that extends into Butte Lake, a submersible booster pump and dedicated 4-inch PVC transmission line to the water treatment plant, a package treatment plant, a 40,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank, and water distribution system with 4 and 6-inch cast iron piping.  The system is operated manually.  The submersible pump lifts water at 50 gpm from the lake through the transmission line to the treatment plant.  Alum and polymer are added to the raw water before entering the treatment unit.  The filter is backwashed about once per week.  Sodium hypochlorite is injected in the filtered water and is then pumped to the water storage tank.  Chlorine residuals are monitored continuously in the water treatment plant and grab samples are taken daily throughout the campground.  Both raw water and treated water turbidities are continuously monitored in the treatment building.

This seasonal system has a groundwater spring source that does not require filtration.  The system provides water to Drakesbad Lodge, swimming pool, and Warner Valley campground.  It consists of a spring source, treatment building with a photovoltaic powered hypochlorinator, an aboveground, and a 40,000-gallon steel water storage tank Water flows by gravity from the spring (yield about 160 gpm) to the treatment building where sodium hypochlorite solution is added.  Disinfected water discharges into the water tank for contact time and storage.  It is then fed from the tank to the distribution system by gravity.
point_of_use: Drakesbad Lodge, swimming pool, and Warner Valley campground

description: This seasonal system is a surface source that provides water for drinking fountains and hose bibbs in the campground.  Perforated pipes in the streambed collect the water that flows through roughing filters to remove some turbidity.  The water then flows to a treatment building where it flows through a bag filtration system consisting of a roughing filter, Giardia Filter, and Crypto filter, and is disinfected with liquid sodium hypochlorite, which is added by a proportional flow hypochlorinator.  The water then flows to a 10,000-gallon above ground steel storage tank.  The entire system operates on gravity.
point_of_use: rinking fountains and hose bibbs in the campground

description:
This year round system serves the campground, the concession facilities, the maintenance facility, summer employee housing, and the information center.  It consists of  stream  infiltration gallery, a welded steel above-ground 100,000-gallon storage tank (summer), a 10,000-gallon glass lined bolted steel tank (winter), summer treatment building containing two multi-media pressure filters and chlorination equipment, winter treatment building containing a Rosedale 3-bag filtration system and chlorination equipment, and distribution piping.  The infiltration is backwashed weekly.  The filters are replaced when the pressure differential reaches 10 psi, or after one week, whichever occurs first.

Summer Operation:  Surface water flows from the intake structure by gravity to the treatment building where a small pump is used to force the water through two pressure filters and into the storage tank.  Coagulant is added upstream of the filters to aid filtration.  The water is chlorinated and stored in the 100,000-gallon storage tank next to the treatment building.  A second pump is used to backwash the filters.  The backwash water flows to a pond next to the treatment building where it evaporates.  Treated water flows by gravity into the distribution system.

Winter Operation:  A small capacity bag filter system (8 gpm) provides water during the winter.  Surface water bypasses the main treatment building but feeds the main storage tank.  From the tank it flows by gravity to another treatment building where it flows through three bag filters (roughing, Giardia, and Cryptosporidium) in series and is chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite solution.  It is then stored in a 10,000-gallon bolted, glass-lined steel tank for distribution to the system.  The bag filters are periodically changed.  The rinse-to-waste water is discharged to an adjacent leachfield.
point_of_use: campground, the concession facilities, the maintenance facility, summer employee housing, and the information center

description: This year round public community (PC) system serves 42 administrative and residential structures.  It consists of an intake structure, a 1,000-gallon sedimentation vault, treatment building with membrane filtration system and hypochlorinator, a 100,000-gallon buried concrete water storage tank, and a distribution system.  The entire system operates by gravity.  Flow is initiated when the low water level electrode energizes the water tank inlet valve to open and is stopped when the high water level electrode in the water tank closes the inlet valve.  Water flows from the intake structure to the treatment building where it flows  through two the membrane filters.  The water is chlorinated before flowing to a 100,000-gallon underground concrete storage tank.  The storage tank is divided into two 50,000-gallon sections, one for domestic use and the other for fire protection.  Water flows into the fire protection side and then overflows to the water supply side of the tank.  Opening a gate valve when needed accesses the fire protection side of the tank.  An old distribution system delivers the water to the points of use.
point_of_use: 42 administrative and residential structures, fire protection

description: This year round system serves a 25-site campground, the Visitor Center, and entrance station.  The water system includes a source water infiltration gallery, a treatment building containing a mixed media pressure filter and treatment chemicals, a 40,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank, and distribution system piping.  The system is supplied from several streams and is operated year round.  The stream water collected by the infiltration gallery flows to the treatment building where it is first treated with caustic soda to raise pH and a coagulant, and then filtered to remove high levels of aluminum and manganese.  The maximum filter hydraulic capacity is 21 gpm.  After filtration, the water is chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite, delivered to the storage tank, and gravity fed to the system.  A radio telemetry system controls water storage tank liquid level.  The infiltration gallery is periodically (weekly) backwashed to remove silt and debris.  When backwashing can no longer effectively remove the silt, the drain rock in the gallery is replaced (about once every 10 years).
point_of_use: 25-site campground, the Visitor Center, and entrance station

description: This seasonal system provides water for the campground comfort station flush toilets.  It consists of a surface water source, a treatment building containing filtration and chlorination equipment, a 10,000-gallon welded steel aboveground storage tank and a 10,000-gallon fiberglass-lined bolted steel above ground tank, and distribution system piping. Water collected in the infiltration gallery flows by gravity through a settling basin to the treatment building where it is filtered through a Rosedale 3-bag filtration system (8 gpm) consisting of a roughing filter (5 micron), a Giardia filter (2 micron), and a Cryptosporidium (1 micron absolute) filter.  The filtered water is disinfected and is stored in the water storage tanks before entering the distribution system.  Flow into the water tanks is controlled by a solenoid actuated altitude valve.  The infiltration gallery is backwashed weekly to remove silt and debris.  The filters are replaced when the pressure differential reaches 10 psi, or after one week, whichever occurs first.  Piping allows the WTP and water tanks to be bypassed.  An air-gap is maintained during normal operation to eliminate a cross connection.
point_of_use: campground comfort station with flush toilets
source_location_refs: Google Earth Coordinates for Summit Lake Campground

description: This system serves the Warner Valley Ranger Station.  It consists of a surface water influenced spring source, two 1500-gallon polypropylene water tanks, a bag filtration system including a roughing filter, a prefilter, and a Giardia filter, and a solar-powered hypochlorinator.  The system operates entirely by gravity.  Water flows from the existing spring to the treatment building where it is filtered (3 gpm) and disinfected.  The treated water flows into the water tanks for storage and chlorine contact time and then into the distribution pipe to the Ranger Station.
The source of drinking water used by the city of Omaha and therefore LECL is ground water. This water is pumped from the Carrizo-Wilcox Aquifer located in the city of Omaha.

park_name: Lewis and Clark National Historical Park
description: The City of Warrenton's water is supplied by the Lewis and Clark River, Big South Fork and Little South Fork of the Lewis and Clark River and Camp C Creek. The State has performed a source assessment on the City's water supply. The 5 1/2 miles of raw water transmission pipes deliver water from our sources to either the 16 MG raw water reservoir or directly to the treatment plant. The raw water first goes through a pretreatment system and then through a membrane filtration system called continuous microfiltration.  After filtration, the water is disinfected with chlorine that is generated on site. The City of Warrenton has two treated water reservoirs with a total volume of 7 MG, which provide both optimum system pressure and water supply redundancy to all parts of our water delivery area.  Approximately, 94 miles of potable water pipe make up our treated water distribution and transmission systems. The water system supplies the City of Warrenton and portions of the county outside the City limits, primarily to the south of Warrenton.

Source:  The source for this year round public water system is a shallow well (constructed approximately 1979) supplied by a shallow infiltration gallery located in the flood plain and in close proximity to the Little Big Horn River.  The infiltration lines are buried in shallow alluvium adjacent the Bighorn River and composed of sand, gravel, and shale.  The well is 6 ft in diameter and approximately 26 ft deep and is located in a pump house next to the infiltration gallery.  Details regarding the collection laterals are sparse and historical reports indicate 4 laterals 150 ft. in length with burial depth around 17 ft.  A more recent lateral addition extends generally to the west in a T-shape consisting of over 540 ft. of 6 Inch bituminous coated perforated CMP piping.  Current operators believe that this newer T-shaped infiltration lateral is supplying the system water and that all old laterals have been physically disconnected from the well although this is unconfirmed.  Water is pumped from the well by two vertical turbine pumps.  The pumps are controlled by water level in the large storage tank.  
The source water is classified as groundwater not under the influence of surface water.  This classification is based on two Microscopic Particulate Analyses (MPA) completed in 1993.  The results of those MPA tests found a low-risk for surface water influence.  A 1995 letter from the Montana DEQ confirms the designation as groundwater.  Two additional MPA tests were completed in 2018 and both scored a 0-Low Risk for surface water influence.  The source water is high in iron and definite seasonal fluctuations in quality and quantity are noted by the operators.  During some years, the well pumps have to be shut down and operated manually in order to keep water in the well especially late in the summer/fall.

Treatment: Water is pre-chlorinated immediately with a 12.5% sodium hypochlorite solution for the purpose of iron oxidation and precipitation.  After chlorination, water is pumped via a 6" main uphill approximately ¬º mile to the administration area where it is passed through an iron removal filter system (parallel train of 16 Kinetico brand Macrolite 2000 filters) for removal of precipitated iron.  The filters are housed in the maintenance building.  The filters automatically backwash based on gallons treated and backwash water is collected in a manifold that discharges to an old on-site wastewater system.  After the iron removal filters, water either enters a "non-potable" system serving fire suppression and irrigation needs or else enters the potable or domestic side of the water system.  Water entering the domestic side of the system can enter either directly from the 6" main or from a 100,000 gallon reservoir that stores excess chlorinated but non-potable water.  In either case, all water entering the domestic side of the system is re-chlorinated prior to entering the 10,0000 gallon domestic water storage tank.

Storage:  
Water on the non-potable fire suppression and irrigation side of the system is stored in a 100,000 gallon buried concrete storage tank located near the visitor center.   After re-chlorination, domestic water is stored in a 10,000 gallon buried fiberglass tank located in the administration area.   

Distribution:  
The domestic side of the system serves an administrative office building/residential area, maintenance area, visitor center, and comfort station.  The non-potable side serves fire suppression needs and irrigation needs both in the cemetery and the administrative area.   Underground sprinkler systems are used for irrigation both in the administrative/residential area as well as the cemetery.    
There are four different underground sprinkler connections in the administrative/residential area.  Three are connected to the non-potable side of the system and one is connected to the domestic side.  The cemetery irrigation system is served by a separate 500 gallon storage tank and pump system that is connected to the non-potable side of the system.
point_of_use: administrative office building/residential area, maintenance area, visitor center, and comfort station, fire suppression, irrigation
source_notes: Source of water is an infiltration gallery. For our purposes this is classified as shallow groundwater with the type being the original source, and the aquifer is the top formation for this area.

description:
Lake Springfield is the surface water source of our drinking water.  It contains 17.6 billion gallons when full and covers about 4,200 acres. Its 265-square-mile watershed, including the Sugar and Lick Creek drainage areas, is composed primarily of agricultural land.  During times of low precipitation, water is pumped from the South Fork of the Sangamon River at its confluence with Horse Creek. A source water assessment for our supply has been completed by the IEPA. Information provided by this assessment indicates the vulnerability of our water supply to potential sources of contamination.  Causes of pollution to the lake include nutrients, siltation, suspended solids, and organic enrichment. Primary sources of pollution include agricultural runoff, land disposal (septic systems), and shoreline erosion. If you would like a copy of the assessment, call the Water Purification Plant at (217) 757-8630.

To convert this raw water supply to drinking water, lake water is pumped through CWLP‚Äôs Water Treatment Plant where chemical reactions are initiated to assist in the removal of algae, suspended solids, hardness and many chemical constituents. The clarification basins remove the bulk of these materials and the final filter beds remove very small particles. Fluoride is added to prevent tooth decay; chlorine to disinfect the finished water; and ammonia to stabilize the chlorine in the distribution system.
source_location_refs: Google Earth, CWLP Water Treatment Plant

description: This system is not currently in operation.  It was previously a Public Non-Community Transient (PWS #0860094) that serves the Tile house, which was used as a Rangers office. The system consists of a drilled well and hypochlorinator housed in a small enclosure, and two pressure tanks located in the tile water tower building. Water is pumped by a submersible well pump through the enclosure where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution. The water then enters two pressure tanks that provide pressure, storage, and chlorine contact time. There is a dug well about 25 feet west of the drilled well. This was the original well that served the house. Due to its proximity to the drilled well, it was a potential source of contamination. The well is filled with sand and a cover has been placed over the well.  WELL IS CURRENTLY ONLY USED FOR FILLING LIVESTOCK TROUGHS.
source_notes: According to IRMA Records, This well never existed.

park_name: Lyndon B. Johnson National Historical Park
description: Texas White House: This non-public system serves as an emergency backup to the Ranch Water System. Water is pumped from two wells through a treatment building on the north side of the house where it is disinfected using sodium hypochlorite solution. Water then enters a horizontal storage tank in the treatment building. This tank provides storage and chlorine contact time. A booster pump and bladder-type hydropneumatic tanks are used to maintain system pressure. This system is put on line only as emergency backup to the Main Ranch System. The source is two 6-inch diameter drilled wells (North Well and South Well) located 50 feet from each other and 100 feet north of the house. One well is equipped with a pitless adapter and underground discharge. The other has a sanitary well seal and exposed piping from the top of the well seal to the ground surface. Well slabs have been installed to meet the requirements of the TCEQ.

description: This Public Non-Community Transient water system (PWS#0860008) serves an office with a sink and a comfort station with drinking fountain. The system consists of a drilled well and hypochlorinator housed in a separate enclosure, a 500-gallon plastic water storage tank, and pressurization system located in a small building. Water is pumped from the well by a submersible pump, disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution, and discharged into the water storage tank. The tank provides storage and chlorine contact time. The water is pressurized with a booster pump and two bladder-type hydropneumatic tanks before entering the distribution piping. There is a separate irrigation system consisting of a well, pressure tank and distribution piping. The pressure tank is located in the same building as the tanks for the potable water. The 6-inch diameter, 200 foot deep drilled well is located approximately 450 feet northeast of the house. The well has a casing and sanitary seal depth of 50 feet, a yield of 7 gpm, and a static water level of 18 feet. The 2 horsepower pump is set at 137 feet.

park_name: Lyndon B. Johnson National Historical Park
description:
This area of the park is connected to the Johnson City community water system. This area consists of the Park Headquarters and visitor center, education center, Jones House, Cantwell House, Walker House, Boyhood Home comfort station, the Settlement Exhibit Center comfort station. The sources for the system are groundwater wells owned and operated by Johnson City located outside Park boundaries. The City also provides all necessary treatment (chlorination) and storage. There are approximately 11 wells identified within the Park boundary that have been sealed as required by the Texas Commission on Environmental Quality (TCEQ). There are three wells at the Boyhood Home site. The first is a shallow drilled well behind the house used for irrigation. A second drilled well located adjacent to the irrigation well was not in use and has been sealed. The third well is a dug well in front of the house. It has a rock casing extending above the ground that is enclosed with a wooden cover. The well is 4 feet 4 inches in diameter and is 41¬Ω feet deep. The well has been filled with sand to prevent the accumulation of water.  There are two interpretive wells at the Settlement. One is a dug well in front of the Sam Ealy Johnson Log Cabin and the other is a drilled well equipped with a windmill used to supply a tank and provide water to the Cooling House located on the north edge of the settlement. Neither of these wells is used as a source of drinking water. There are five backflow prevention assemblies within the park headquarters area. One reduced pressure zone assembly valve (RPZ) is used to protect the potable water from the water used for the chiller tower and another RPZ protects the potable water system from the boiler. A double check valve backflow assembly provides protection from backflow from the VC fire sprinkler system. There are also double check valves on the Education Center and Cantwell House fire systems. 

Johnson City gets its water from the Johnson County Special Utility District (JCSUD). JCSUD has two main water production sources. About 30 percent of total production comes from well water in Johnson County (Trinity and Paluxy Aquifers) and 70 percent is purchased lake water from Lake Granbury and the City of Mansfield. The City of Mansfield purchases lake water from the Tarrant Regional Water District (TRWD). TRWD pumps water primarily from Cedar Creek and Richland Chambers Reservoirs.

park_name: Lyndon B. Johnson National Historical Park
description:
This Public Non-Community Transient (PWS # 0860084) system serves the LBJ Ranch House, Ranch buildings, Klein fts shop, Malechek House, Bus Barn, Show Barn, and four Ranch VIP RV trailer sites. Water is used at the Show Barn for a restroom and cattle washing, and at the Bus Barn for two restrooms and drinking fountains. A drinking water fountain is provided for the public at the Show Barn. Water is pumped from the Martin well through a water treatment building where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution. From the treatment building the water is pumped to two 55,000 gallon glass-fused-to-steel bolted water storage tanks via a dedicated fill line. A float switch located in one of the tanks senses pump-on and pump-off water levels. This information is transmitted to the well pumphouse by a buried control cable, which then controls well pump operation. Water flows out of the steel tanks by gravity when demanded via a water distribution system consisting of 10-inch cast iron pipe. 

 Isolation valves on the bolted steel tanks allow one of the tanks to be removed from service while the other remains online. A gauge mounted on the outside of the bolted steel tank is used to determine water level in the tanks. A diesel operated fire pump remains in place but is no longer connected to the system.  Fire hydrants located throughout the distribution system provide fire protection to the Ranch buildings.  There is a pressure system installed in the same building as the fire pumps that provides pressure to the Malechek House, Showbarn, and VIP sites.  The pressure system consist of a 5 HP booster pump, 2 pressure tanks, and a pressure switch.  A separate pressure system for the Bus Barn consists of a small booster pump, pressure tank, and pressure switch is located in a small above ground enclosure near the Bus Barn.

The backup water sources for this system are the North and South Wells.  Both wells are piped into a pumphouse located near the Texas White House.  The South Well is currently offline.  A float located in a 525 gallon PE water storage tank initiates well pumping cycles.  The water is chlorinated before entering the tank using sodium hypochlorite and a chemical feed pump.  Water is then pressurized by a 1 HP booster pump and 2 pressure tanks that are controlled by a pressure switch.  The North and South Wells and pumphouse are used for irrigation and are physically disconnected from the potable water system in the pumphouse.  A PVC cap located behind the pressure tanks can be removed and a compression coupling can be connected in order to connect these wells into the potable water system if needed.

description: The water system serves the visitor center and consists of a 255-foot drilled well, hydropneumatic tank, hypochlorinator, fire pump, and distribution piping. A hydropneumatic tank provides for minimal storage and contact time before the treated water enters the distribution system piping.  Water for the visitor center fire suppression sprinkler system is stored in a cistern located below the pumphouse.  Untreated water from the well is discharged into the cistern.  An air gap separates the fire suppression water from the potable water.  Reduced pressure backflow preventers are connected to plumbing serving hazardous applications such as the swamp cooler and the boiler system.  The well is owned by Los Angeles Department of Water and Power (LADWP).  LADWP performs all chemical testing required by the state.  The park collects water samples for monthly bacteriological water quality testing as required by the state for a public water system.

description:
There is one water system that serves all of Mesa Verde National Park.  The source water is Mancos Creek, a mountain stream located above Mancos, CO.  A water intake was constructed above Jackson Lake in 2003 and modified in 2008.  The park is currently drawing all of its water from this location.  

The intake is made up of infiltration beds consisting of two banks of infiltrators beneath the river, diversion walls to slow water flow across the beds, sedimentation basin, and associated valving.  The system is designed to allow the operator to adjust the level of water over the beds.  This site is very remote with no unauthorized vehicular access, although the area is frequented by hikers and fisherman.  The park also has the capability of drawing from an alternate intake (Jackson Lake) if needed.

From the intake, the raw water gravity flows through a raw water transmission pipe a distance of about 17 miles to the surface water treatment plant located at the entrance to the park.  Water is stored in two 25,000 gallon raw water storage tanks near the entrance gate before flowing by gravity to the water treatment plant.  

The park installed a Pall Aria microfiltration system in the spring of 2006 capable of producing 150 gpm.  The new plant is automated to all aspects of the process, records the data for reporting requirements, and shuts the plant off if any parameters are exceeded.  The cleaning of the system is also fully automated when a preset quantity of water is processed or a preset pressure drop occurs across the membranes.  The backwash water is stored in two concrete basins before being discharged behind the plant; a NPDES permit is in place for the discharge.  Two 9,000 pound GAC filters were added in 2010 to remove organics that contribute to formation of disinfection byproducts.  The water is chlorinated using gas chlorination before distribution.

After treatment, three 60 HP high pressure (over 600 psi) booster pumps then pump the water to the top of the mesa to the 2 million gallon water storage tank (aka Navajo Hill Tank) near Far View.  From the Navajo Hill Water Tank, water flows by gravity to 3 water storage tanks (300,000 gallon storage tanks at Morefield, 300,000 gallon tank at Wetherill, and a 100,000 gallon tank at Chapin Mesa).  Water is hauled from the water system to a 2,700 gallon HDPE water storage tank at Cliff Palace where it is pressurized using solar powered booster pumps and a pressure tank to serve 2 drinking water fountains.
point_of_use: all of Mesa Verde National Park
source_location_refs: Coordinatates for the branching point of the creeks north of Jackson Gulch Reservoir used.

description: This transient, non-community water system serves the entire Montezuma Well area of the Park. This includes the maintenance shop, the two Mission-66 Park residence units, and a comfort station with a men fts restroom and a women fts restroom. The source for this water system is an approximately 160-foot deep drilled well, located inside a water treatment building. Water is pumped from this well through a submersible pump. Water enters into the pumphouse, passes through a meter and then the potable water line splits into two 1.5-inch metal distribution lines   one, 1.5-inch line serving the maintenance shop and one, 1.5-inch line serving the two Park residence units. Water delivered through each of these lines passes through a flow switch prior to treatment. Each of these lines is then injected with a liquid sodium hypochlorite solution. Following treatment, water from each line is pressurized through a 125 PSI Goulds HydroPro pressure tanks and then the water lines exit outside of the treatment building. To achieve appropriate chlorine contact, each 1.5-inch metal water line increases into 8-inch pipes   providing approximately 600 gallons of mixing capability per each line. These two separate water lines enter back into the pumphouse. One of these distribution lines provides potable water to the two Mission 66 housing units, while the other distribution line serves the maintenance shop.
point_of_use: maintenance shop, the two Mission-66 Park residence units, and a comfort station with a men fts restroom and a women fts restroom

description: This transient, non-community water system serves the Visitor Center, Visitor Center comfort station, one Park housing unit with 3 single bedroom apartments, an additional Park housing unit, and one Park office building. The source for this water system is an approximately 160-foot deep drilled well, located inside a well house in the Park housing area. Water is pumped from this well through a submersible pump. Water enters into the pumphouse, passes through a meter, and is then injected with a liquid sodium hypochlorite solution. Following injection, potable water passes through a 2-inch Watts 600 W06 reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device prior to distribution to the park housing units, park office space, visitor center, and visitor center through a 6-inch water main. Additionally, potable water is stored inside a 50,00-gallon welded-steel potable water storage reservoir, located uphill to the north of the Park visitor center. There is not a separate transmission line to the 50,000-gallon welded steel potable water storage reservoir; the distribution system serves as a transmission line to the reservoir.

This system consists of a primary well #2 and a backup (government) well #1, continuous photovoltaic powered chlorinator, submersible well pump, and a two-compartment 40,000-gallon concrete underground storage tank.  Well #2 is an 8-inch drilled well that is generator powered with solar backup.  It pumps at the rate of 5 gpm.  This well is currently used to provide potable water to the facilities.  Well #1currently feeds the stock tank and is considered non-potable.  It has a pitless adapter with an integral screened casing vent.  It is also connected to the potable water system and can be used as required to deliver water to the potable system.  The potable system provides water by gravity to the Interagency Fire Camp, a park residence, 35-site campground and RV dump station, information center, and a stock water tank.  Well #1 is used primarily to pump water to the stock water tank, but can be used as required to deliver water to the potable system.

The park residence, which is the historic Rocking L ranch house, has a separate chlorinator and galvanized steel storage tank that is filled by manually operating a gate valve near the well site.  Since the storage tank does not provide adequate water pressure, a booster pump and pressure tank are used.  A dedicated fill line is used to fill the water tank.  Treatment is provided by a proportional flow hypochlorinator that operates without power.

description: The water system serves the visitor center, comfort station, dorms, quad plex, and an RV site for three hookups.  It consists of a well with submersible pump, sodium hypochlorite disinfection equipment, a 12,000-gallon steel water storage tank, two booster pumps, and water distribution system.  When the water level in the storage tank drops below pump-on, the well pump is energized and pumps water from the well into the storage tank.  The raw well water is injected with sodium hypochlorite solution before discharging into the water storage tank for contact time.  When the pressure in the system upstream from the booster pumps falls below lead pump-on pressure, the lead booster pump is energized and charges the pressure tank to the pump-off pressure.  If pressure drops below lag pump-on, the lag pump will energize to supplement the flow.  The pressurization system is located in a vault below grade immediately downstream from the water tank.  Reduced pressure backflow preventers (RP) are or will be provided for the connection to the irrigation system, and make-up water for the heat pump.  The fire sprinkler system for the visitor center is connected to the Union Pacific (UP) water system.  This connection is equipped with an RP that protects the UP water system.
point_of_use: visitor center, comfort station, dorms, quad plex, and an RV site for three hookups
source_location_refs: NPS Buildings Layer - Kelso Depot Information Center

description: Kessler Springs is a former ranch obtained by the park.  The system currently provides water to quad plex.   A hybrid propane generator/PV system provides power to the well pump.  The system consists of a drilled well (drilled in 2020), two in-line water filters, a hypochlorinator for chlorine disinfection, 2 filters for sediment, 2 pressure tanks, and distribution piping consisting of a mixture of black PE, PVC, and galvanized iron most of which is believed to be 1 inch in diameter.

This seasonal system serves a 26-site campground.  It consists of a generator-powered drilled well, a treatment building containing chlorine disinfection equipment, two 4,000-gallon above-ground galvanized steel water storage tanks, and water distribution system.  A portable generator is brought to the well site when water is needed to fill the tanks, and is used to power the submersible well pump.  The water is pumped from the well through the treatment building for chlorine disinfection.  It then flows via a dedicated fill line to the two water tanks that provide storage and chlorine contact time.  Water flows by gravity to several frost-free yard hydrants in the campground.  The generator-powered submersible pump delivers water at 3 to 4 gpm.  It is run manually and pumps about 300 gallons before the dynamic water level in the well reaches the pump intake.

This year-round system serves a residence and several yard hydrants and livestock water troughs.  It consists of a drilled well, sodium hypochlorite disinfection equipment, a large bladder-type hydropneumatic tank, and 3-inch pvc distribution piping.  When the system pressure reaches pump-on, a pressure switch energizes the well pump.  Water is pumped from the well through an enclosure containing the disinfection equipment which injects sodium hypochlorite.  The treated water enters the distribution piping.  When the pressure reaches pump-off, the pressure switch de-energizes the well pump.  Contact time is provided within the piping before the first point of use.  Power is provided by a combination propane-powered generator/photovoltaic system.
point_of_use: residence and several yard hydrants and livestock water troughs

description: Improvements are planned for the water system.  The water facilities serve the residence.  The system consists of a 6-inch diameter, 100-foot deep drilled well with a submersible pump, disinfection equipment, hydropneumatic tank, two 4,000-gallon galvanized steel water storage tanks, and 2-inch pvc water line from the tanks to the residence.  The well, disinfection equipment, and hydropneumatic tank are located in a lockable fiberglass enclosure.  The submersible pump delivers water at 20 gpm from the well to the storage tanks via a dedicated fill line.  Sodium hypochlorite solution is injected into the water before discharging into the tank.  A propane-powered generator operates the well pump to fill the water storage tanks.  Water then flows by gravity to the residence.

This year-round system is operated by the California State University Desert Studies Center (DSC) under a contract with the park.  The park has entered into a cooperative agreement with DSC regarding operation of the water system.  DSC is responsible for the day-to-day operation of the system, including all required water quality monitoring.  The park is responsible for any major system facility improvements.  

There are two separate water systems.  One system provides potable water to hose bibbs, toilet and shower facilities in two residences and two dormitories, and communal kitchen.  The other system delivers untreated well water to maintain the water level in the fish pond.  A common drilled well serves as the source for both systems.  Two submersible pumps are installed in the well, one for each system.

The potable water system consists a submersible pump, three 2,500-gallon polyethylene water storage tanks, water treatment facilities consisting of a water softener, a 1-micron cartridge filter, reverse osmosis membrane filtration (RO), chlorination equipment, and distribution piping.  The untreated water fills the water storage tanks via a dedicated 3-inch PVC fill line.  The water tanks are plumbed to allow any one tank to be removed for maintainance without disruption of service.  Two separate lines feed the potable connections in the distribution system.  One line serves the kitchen and the other feeds the remaining potable connections.  Each has its own chlorinator to inject sodium hypochlorite.  An RO membrane filters the water feeding the kitchen, since the well water is highly mineralized and unpalatable.  Water filtered by the RO filter is stored in a separate 300-gallon polyethylene.  On demand, an in-line flow switch energizes the chlorinator which injects sodium hypochlorite for disinfection.  The filtered and chlorinated water flows by gravity to a faucet in the kitchen.  There is no commercial grid power available at Zzyzx, so power is provided by a combination of a wind charger, a solar array, and several generators.  The RO membrane is removed once per year and sent to manufacturer for inspection and maintenance.  The RO unit is also disinfected at this time.
point_of_use: Two residences, two dorms, communal kitchen

description: This system is under two names, however the official name for the system is Thompson House.  This system serves the Carbon River Ranger Station (CRRS) and the Thompson House. description:
The water system at Mount Rushmore consists of two wells that are considered groundwater under the influence of surface water. They provide water through the treatment plant to a buried 500,000-gallon concrete reservoir adjacent to the plant. Within the plant the water is disinfected and treated to adjust the pH and alkalinity. Booster pumps (new in 2012) then deliver the water to two buried concrete storage reservoirs located, at the same elevation, in different areas of the park. The older 75,000-gallon reservoir is located above the maintenance shop/work area and a newer 125,000-gallon reservoir is located on a high area above the administration building. The reservoirs provide system pressure by gravity for the entire park that includes: concession restaurant, gift shop, dormitory, administration buildings, employee housing, maintenance areas, studio, visitor orientation area, public restrooms, parking lot restroom and amphitheater/visitor center.

Treated water is stored in a 500,000 gallon reservoir adjacent to the MORU WTP where it is pumped to one of two reservoirs in the park water distribution system with capacities of 75,000 and 125,000 gallons. Raw water is provided by two wells. The capacity of the oldest well is 50 gallons per minute (gpm), and the new well (2011) is 40 gpm. Peak water usage occurs in the summer months and is estimated to be 70,000 gallons per day (gpd).

The treatment process:
Groundwater is pumped through a manual strainer which removes all particles greater than 250 microns in size that could damage the membrane system. Sodium hypochlorite (not in use because arsenic is As V versus As III, hence it is already oxidized) and ferric chloride are then fed in the raw water pipe upstream of the coagulation tank for arsenic removal. The coagulation tank contains a mixer and provides the necessary detention time for floc formation to occur. Chemical storage and feed systems for sodium hypochlorite and ferric chloride were added in the new treatment building.

The turbidity of the pretreated water is monitored as the membrane filtration feed pump moves the coagulation tank effluent to the membrane system. The membrane feed pump provides the necessary pressure to force the pretreated water through membrane units. The membrane filtration system removes all materials from the water which are 0.1 micron or greater in size, which includes turbidity, bacteria, Giardia and Cryptosporidium. The membrane filtration system is maintained by backwashing and chemical cleaning. Sulfuric acid, citric acid and sodium hypochlorite storage and feed systems, including a clean-in-place tank, provide the necessary chemicals for cleaning the membranes. Compressed air is used in the monitoring and cleaning of the membranes and a compressor and air receiver provide the air needed.

Filtrate from the membrane treatment system flows into an intermediate storage tank. A filtrate booster pump supplies the pressure necessary to pump the finished water to the onsite reservoir. As the filtrate leaves the intermediate storage tank, it is monitored for turbidity on its way to pH adjustment and disinfection. Carbon dioxide and sodium hydroxide are fed to the filtrate for alkalinity adjustment and pH control, respectively. Following pH adjustment, the filtrate is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite. The hypochlorite feed is controlled manually and monitored by an oxidation / reduction (ORP) sensor. After disinfection, the finished water flows to the existing onsite storage reservoir.

Wastes from the backwashing and membrane cleaning operations, as well as tank drains, are discharged to the backwash wet well. Neutralization following maintenance washes and Clean In Place washes is performed manually in the wet well before discharge to the sewer. The wet well contains a submersible pump which discharges through a 2 inch line to a manhole and 3 inch force main that eventually flows to the wastewater treatment plant.
point_of_use: All park facilities, including the concession restaurant, gift shop, dormitory, administration buildings, employee housing, maintenance areas, studio, visitor orientation area, public restrooms, parking lot restroom and amphitheater/visitor center

description: This Public Transient Non-Community System serves the visitor center that has three restrooms and 1 outside faucet, the ranger housing area that consists of 9 year-round residences, and administrative offices.  This system serves all of the facilities in the park.  The water system consists of 2 ground water wells, a pumphouse with liquid sodium hypochlorite system, a 50,000 gallon above ground water storage tank, and a booster system with two 3 HP booster pumps and two 264 gallon pressure tanks.  Well #3, constructed in 1979, is 750 feet deep with perforations from 270-280 feet and 539-689 feet and is equipped with a 3 HP submersible well pump that pumps approximately 3 gpm.  Well #3 is only run seasonally, since a portion of the water main from Well #3 to the pumphouse is above grade due to rock and is subject to freezing.  Well #2, constructed in 1964, is 700 feet deep with perforations from 270-280 feet and 539-689 feet. Aqua-Mag was injected as a sequestering agent to improve water quality, but has been offline since 2007.
point_of_use: Visitor center that has three restrooms and 1 outside faucet, the ranger housing area that consists of 9 year-round residences, and administrative offices.  This system serves all of the facilities in the park.

description: The Tupelo Complex Water System consists of two wells (primary and backup), two treatment buildings, distribution system, and a 50,000 gallon elevated storage tank. Both the primary and backup wells have chlorine injection systems and blended phosphate systems to minimize pipe corrosion. The primary well is located adjacent to the elevated storage tank, while the backup well is located adjacent to the maintenance shop. A 42 gallon hydropneumatic tank is in the backup well's treatment building. The wells are connected in a looped distribution system. The system was built around 1957. This system serves the Tupelo District Facilities, which includes the Visitor Center, Park Headquarters, maintenance shop, park offices (six occupied and 20 vacant), and a VIP pad.

description: This public, non-transient non-community water system provides all the water for this Park. This system serves the maintenance shop, the campground comfort station, employee housing, and the visitor center. Water is pumped from an 870-foot drilled well (identified as well # 4) with a submersible pump (Stenner Model 85MHP40), which is designed to pump 15 gallons per minute. Water is pumped into the pumphouse where it undergoes treatment with a sodium hypochlorite solution.  Water is sent to a 100,000-gallon, above ground welded steel storage reservoir for storage. This reservoir was built during 1973 at a diameter of 33 feet and a height of 15 feet. From the above ground welded steel storage reservoir, water is pumped to a booster station through two Balor-Reliance (model 85700817) booster pumps. Prior to distribution, water travels through a 200-gallon steel pressure tank (Wessels Company) at approximately 50-PSI prior to distribution.
point_of_use: maintenance shop, the campground comfort station, employee housing, and the visitor center

description: This groundwater system provided non-potable water to the site for pressure washing of two CXT Vaults.  No potable water is provided at this location.  The bored well was installed in 2000 with yield of 16 gallons per minute.  The well is 254 feet deep, cased to 187 feet, and screened to 245 feet.  Static water was found at 16 feet deep, and surface seal was concreted to 38 feet.
point_of_use: pressure washing of two CXT Vaults

description: This year-round non-public system consists of a 58.5 ft drilled well, submersible pump, 8" steel chlorine contact pipe, and a hydropneumatic pressure tank. The park has decided to manage this system as a non-potable system due to elevated levels of nitrates and taste.  The park is providing bottled water for staff.

description: This year-round TNC system consists of a 210' drilled well, submersible pump, continuous disinfection, and a green sand removal filter dosed with potassium permanganate. description: The Bowles water system is a non-public surface water system serving 1 residence in the Stehekin area of Lake Chelan National Recreation Area.  Water is collected from the Stehekin River through a hose near the bank with a bar screen.  Water is pumped by a jet pump and filtered through 2 - 20 micron cartridge filters operated in parallel to a series of 5 micron cartridge filters.  Liquid sodium hypochlorite is added after filtration and the water flows through five chlorine contact tanks to a hydropneumatic tank that provides water pressure to the residence.

The Brownfields water system is a Non-Public Groundwater System serving three residences in the Stehekin Area of Lake Chelan National Recreation Area. Two of the homes are seasonal, one serves a full-time employee. The system consists of a drilled well that pumps water to a treatment building where it is disinfected with liquid sodium hypochlorite. The water is then stored in an 88-gallon hydropneumatic storage tank until needed.

description: This year-round non-community system consists of a 60-foot-deep 8  cased well drilled in 1992, submersible pump, continuous disinfection, and a 40,000-gallon steel water storage reservoir. Water flows by gravity to the Visitor Center, to comfort stations in the campground, to NPS residences, and to the small maintenance facility. DOH requires a certified WDM1 because the park assumed a hydraulic connection to surface water during the GWI determination process (possibly due to the well fts proximity to surface water) and installed disinfection to achieve CT6, which was reviewed by an ODW engineer in 2008 (although treatment has never received official ODW approval).
point_of_use: Visitor Center, NPS residences, maintenance facility

description:
The system has a ground water source well located in the Idaho Spring area at an approximate elevation of 5,050 feet. A 3-phase, 10 hp submersible motor pumps well water at approximately 25 gpm. The pump house is served by single phase power and a variable speed controller with a phase invertor which provides three-phase power to the well pump motor. The well water is continuously disinfected via a chemical feed pump injecting a sodium hypochlorite solution prior to exiting the pumphouse near the well site. Elevated levels of iron measured in 2007 prompted the Park to begin injecting Aqua Mag via an additional chemical feed pump. Aqua Mag is a sequestering agent that binds to the soluble iron to keep it in solution. However, the Aqua Mag injection pump failed in 2017 and test results from 2018 show no iron post chlorination in the pump house. The Washington State Department of Health has accepted the removal of the Aqua Mag injection and has since approved a unidirectional flushing program to replace iron treatment.

Following injection of the hypochlorite solution, the well water is conveyed to a 100,000-gallon, two compartment underground concrete reservoir at an approximate elevation of 5,365 feet. The elevation difference of 315 feet results in system pressure of 135 psi in the pump house. The reservoir fill line includes two sections of pipe consisting of 2  HDPE and 3  Sch. 80 Galvanized Steel totaling approximately 4,000 feet. Water is supplied, via a 6-inch asbestos cement distribution pipe, to the Visitor Center and two adjacent fire hydrants. The Visitor Center is located at an approximate elevation of 5,150, or 200 feet below the reservoir. The elevation difference results in a static pressure of 85 psi at the Visitor Center. There are two separate metered connections within the VC. A smaller distribution line continues from the VC to serve two picnic areas/comfort stations approximately one mile west of the Visitor Center along Hurricane Ridge Road. This connection is not metered and the pipe serving this area consist of 2  HDPE and 2-¬Ω  Galvanized Steel.
source_location_refs: NPS Buildings Layer - Hurricane Water Pump House

description: This year-round non-community water system consists of two 6  diameter screened wells drilled in 2014, two well pumps (South Well   25 gpm; North Well   15 gpm) designed to run simultaneously, two completely redundant package water treatment units with chemical feed systems capable of treating 40 gpm to reduce iron and manganese below Secondary Maximum Contaminant Levels (SMCLs), a 100,000 gallon underground concrete clearwell reservoir, and distribution system pumps with variable frequency drives to maintain the target distribution system pressure. For water treatment, sodium hypochlorite, sodium hydroxide, and ferric chloride are injected into the combined raw intake water pipe; polymer is injected just prior to influent entering the unitized treatment system (UTS) consisting of flocculation, sedimentation, and filtration. Water distribution from the 100,000-gallon reservoir is controlled by a PLC panel and four distribution pumps informed by achieving a target discharge pressure of 55 psi. This system serves a concessioner operated lodge, restaurant, cabins, and staff housing, as well as to the NPS residential area, Visitor Center, NPS maintenance compound, fire hydrants, campground comfort stations, hose bibs, and a trailer dump station. DOH requires a certified WTPO2 and WDM1 to operate this system.

water_system_name: Util Lake Crescent Barnes Point Water System
fmss_system_name: Util Lake Crescent Barnes Point Water System
description: The domestic water system consists of two 8  diameter screened wells, two well pumps equipped with adjustable speed controllers, two hypochlorination systems with an inline static mixer, a pipe loop contactor, a small pressure tank, and a distribution system with all the necessary valving. The domestic well pumps were replaced in 2012 and are dual voltage submersible vertical turbine pumps. Design criteria was based on 100 gpm flow capacity at a TDH of 144 feet.  Each well pump is also equipped with an adjustable speed pump controller, connected to a pressure transducer located at the outlet to the distribution system, so that pumps operate in a multi-pump lead-lag configuration to maintain a constant distribution system pressure of 62 psi. An 81-gallon pressure tank provides small drawdown capacities on drive start-up and provides a ‚ cushion  for the entire system at normal operation (and provides compressibility upon shut-down). The nominal 14-inch high density polyethylene (HDPE) DR17 pipe loop contactor was designed and installed to provide plug-flow conditions. The pipe loop system length distributed on a set of support racks totals almost 171 feet in length. A step-dose tracer test was conducted in March 2019 to verify the geometry and baffling of the pipe loop contactor and to document contact time (T) at various flows. At a peak flow rate of 140 gpm, disinfectant contact time is 8.1 minutes and requires a minimum free chlorine residual of 0.74 mg/L. A third supplemental well provides unchlorinated water for fire protection purposes. The supplemental well system consists of a well and pump, pressure sensor, flow meter with double check valve, and fire hydrant with a sampling station. Both the domestic wells and the (unchlorinated) supplemental well utilize the same pipe distribution system. DOH does not require a certified operator for this system.

water_system_name: Util Lake Crescent Log Cabin Water System
fmss_system_name: Util Lake Crescent Log Cabin Water System
description:
This seasonal system is served by a surface water intake from Lake Crescent. Water is pumped to the
treatment building for filtration by prefilters, a 5 micron cartridge intermediate filter, and a Strainrite Giardia and crypto barrier filtration system installed in 2007 that meets the surface water alternative filtration requirements for Washington state. After continuous disinfection, the water is pumped to a single 30,000 gallon factory coated glass-fused-to-steel bolted tank (installed in 2017) for distribution to the concessioner operated lodge, restaurant, and grocery store, a number of cabins, an RV park, and staff housing. The existing float control system was reinstalled in the new tank. The surface water intake facilities were inspected by a contracted dive team in 2021. DOH requires a certified WTPO1 to operate this surface water system.
point_of_use: concessioner operated lodge, restaurant, and grocery store, a number of cabins, an RV park, and staff housing

description: This seasonal non-community system consists of a 35-foot drilled well drilled in 1956 (with flow restricted to 5 gpm), a submersible pump, continuous disinfection, and a 5,000-gallon HDPE water storage tank. his system uses AquaMag, a blended phosphate solution, to sequester high levels of iron and manganese. The system has one 2 HP booster pump located after the storage tank to pressurize the system, followed by 8 Amtrol WX-302 pressure tanks (two rows of 4 tanks each). DOH requires a certified WTPO1 to operate this system.

This year-round noncommunity system consists of a 30-feet deep bored well, submersible pump, continuous disinfection, and a 40,000 gallon steel water storage reservoir.  Since there are no hills in the immediate area, high service pumps take water from the reservoir to pressurize a bank of hydropneumatic tanks for distribution.  The system serves an NPS staff housing area, a maintenance building, a Ranger Station, comfort stations and hosebibs in a large campground, and a comfort station at the beach. DOH does not require a certified operator for this system.

water_system_name: Util Quinault Graves Creek Water System
fmss_system_name: Util Quinault Graves Creek Water System
description:
This system consists of a spring source that flows by gravity to the treatment building for filtration through a 50 micron cartridge prefilter, a 5 micron intermediate cartridge filter, and a bag filter system.  After continuous disinfection, the treated water flows to a polyethylene water
storage reservoir for distribution by gravity to a comfort station and hosebibbs in the campground, and to an NPS seasonal quarters.  The State of Washington determined that  this spring source is not directly surface influenced according to MPA testing.
point_of_use: comfort station and hosebibbs in the campground, and to an NPS seasonal quarters

description: This year-round non-community system consists of a 100-foot-deep well, submersible pump, continuous disinfection, and hydropneumatic pressure tanks. Due to elevated turbidity, color, and iron content, raw water is passed through a 5 micron bag filter to remove iron particulate prior to disinfection, and orthophosphate is fed continuously to reduce the effects of iron (i.e. staining of fixtures) on the system. In 2018, the water treatment process was relocated to a new precast concrete water treatment building with new water treatment equipment. In 2019, the park replaced all 1  and 2  PVC distribution piping with 2  HDPE pipe, plus installed a new 130 linear foot section of in-ground 12  HDPE pipe for increased disinfection contact time. The current submersible well pump, which was installed in December 2017 to replace in-kind the previous pump, is a three-wire 0.5 HP Franklin Electric 10FV05P4-3W230 series V submersible pump set at a depth of 91 feet that provides approximately 13 GPM under 110 feet of total dynamic head. The well pump provides pressure for the treatment process, which consists of a 5 micron bag style filter that removes iron particulate immediately upon entering the treatment building. A second 1 micron final filter was added in series following the initial 5 micron pre-filter; Strainrite fts AquaRite Vessels (model AQ2-2F) are coupled with AquaRite Prefilter & Filter Bags (SWT1-P2S-8T & HPM97-GC-2SS), and differential pressure of 25 psid is used to determine the need for filter exchange. Filtered water is injected with Aqua Mag blended phosphate (8 oz per gallon of water) to sequester unfiltered iron and reduce discoloration and staining associated with finished water. After filtration and sequestration, water is disinfected with a 12.5% sodium hypochlorite solution (100 mL per gallon of water). Chemical feed pumps are controlled through a switched outlet so they are energized when the well pump is initiated to prevent chemical overfeed. Two 120-gallon pressurized well tanks located inside the treatment building maintain approximately 40 psi pressure throughout the distribution system. DOH requires a certified WTPO1 to operate this system.
source_location_refs: Coordinates of the pump house were used.

description: A surface water inlet that is located along a steep trail serves this year-round non-community system. Water flows by gravity to the water treatment building. The water is filtered by a 30 micron cartridge prefilter, a 3 micron intermediate cartridge filter, and a series of Strainrite Giardia and crypto barrier filtration system installed in 2007 that meets the surface water alternative filtration requirements for Washington State. After continuous disinfection the treated water flows to a 44,000 gallon concrete water storage reservoir and a loop of large diameter underground water pipe that adds to the chlorine contact time. This system provides service to a Ranger Station, a concessioner operated lodge, restaurant, grocery store, a large number of cabins, staff housing, an RV park, comfort stations, hose bibs, and a trailer dump station in a campground. The pressure at the treatment building is 120 psi. DOH requires a certified WTPO1 and a certified CCS to operate this surface water system.
point_of_use: Ranger Station, a concessioner operated lodge, restaurant, grocery store, a large number of cabins, staff housing, an RV park, comfort stations, hose bibs, and a trailer dump station in a campground

description: The 8-inch diameter, 40-foot deep well at Staircase was constructed in October 1979 and is cased to a depth of 37 feet. There is no well screen and no perforations; all water enters the well through sand and gravel layers surrounding the bottom opening. The well is located approximately 200 feet from the Skokomish River, within the valley bottom. In 2017, the park replaced the 8000 gallon corroded steel water tank with a 10-foot diameter, 6000 gallon rotational molded polyethylene water storage tank. In 2020, the park replaced 3700 linear feet of 2" galvanized and PCV pipe with 2" HDPE as well as 11 valves within the housing area (beyond the bridge). The existing chlorination process, which involves mixing 12.5% sodium hypochlorite solution and setting the hypochlorination injection to operate with the well pump, remains unchanged. The system provides potable water to a 47-site campground with two flush toilet comfort stations, two seasonal employee residences, and a Ranger Station. Potable water is typically made available to the public May through September. DOH does not require a certified operator for this system.
point_of_use: 47-site campground with two flush toilet comfort stations, two seasonal employee residences, and a Ranger Station

description: 85 foot deep well delivers water to a small campground with pressure tank and pressure switch controlling flow and pressure. The campground is not frequently used.

This year-round system (referred to as the "public system") serves the concessionaire-operated Chateau Lodge, the Chalet gift shop/concession staff housing building, the concessionaire staff dormitory, and the entrance information booth/public restroom building. It consists of a surface intake, treatment facilities including a Tekleen 50/25-micron automatic back-washing pre-filter one bank of 5-micron cartridge polishing filter and a Strainrite giardia/crypto barrier bag filter, hypochlorinator for disinfection, a 75,000 gallon-concrete water storage tank, a 50,000-gallon steel tank and distribution piping. Water flows by gravity from the intake to the water treatment building where it is filtered and disinfected. Backwash water from the prefilter is discharged out the side of the treatment building to daylight. After continuous disinfection the treated water flows to the water storage tanks before distribution. The distribution system consists of 2 and 3-inch galvanized iron pipe. The intake on Lake Creek is within the National Forest, in an area that has been logged, and there is periodic heavy siltation from runoff that causes premature filter plugging.
point_of_use: concessionaire-operated Chateau Lodge, the Chalet gift shop/concession staff housing building, the concessionaire staff dormitory, and the entrance information booth/public restroom building

description: This system (referred to as the old "private system"/ the O&M Plant) consists of two surface water intakes on a very small unnamed tributary (3 gpm) to Lake Creek that flows by gravity to a 1,000-gallon concrete settling tank, then by gravity to two 1,250-gallon polyethylene storage tanks, and then to the water treatment building. The water is filtered through a 5-micron Aqua Pure cartridge prefilter, and a bag filter system. After continuous disinfection the treated water flows to a 5,000-gallon concrete underground water storage reservoir for distribution by gravity to the Headquarters building, maintenance shop, seasonal residence (quarters #5), and the duplex building via a 6-inch main and 2-inch service lines.
point_of_use: Headquarters building, maintenance shop, seasonal residence (quarters #5), and the duplex building

description:
The Parkwide water system serves all facilities in the Park including the Visitor Center, administrative offices, restrooms, maintenance area, residential area containing 20 houses and 10 VIP RV sites, a 208 site campground, resource management building, and visitor and resource protection building.

Water is pumped from two drilled wells   well # 4 (or the south well) and well # 6 (or the north well) Well # 4, or the south well, was drilled during 1957 and is equipped with a submersible pump. A large, five-foot by five-foot square concrete apron surrounds the wellhead. Well # 6, or the north well, was rehabilitated during 2019 with the addition of a new submersible pump. A large, three-foot by three-foot square concrete apron surrounds the wellhead. The wellhead is enclosed inside a green cylinder attached to the square concrete apron that is secured with a lock. Potable water from these two tells enters the pumphouse, where it is treated through an automatic sodium hypochlorite injection system. Potable water from the pumphouse is pumped to a 100,000-gallon above ground potable water storage reservoir. The well pumps are controlled with an automatic reservoir level control system. Potable water is then transferred by gravity to either the 50,000 gallon above ground potable water reservoir that services the campground, or direct distribution to the Visitor Center, administrative, maintenance, and housing areas. Potable water enters the distribution system through a 6-inch asbestos cement (AC) water. Flow to the 50,000 gallon above ground potable water reservoir is controlled by a float valve.  The 50,000 gallon above ground potable water reservoir services the campground distribution system and an employee duplex. A 4-inch PVDF bypass line is present if needed so that either tank can be taken offline.
point_of_use: Visitor Center, administrative offices, restrooms, maintenance area, residential area containing 20 houses and 10 VIP RV sites, a 208 site campground, resource management building, and visitor and resource protection building

description:
Big Spring Area: The campground, cabins/concession dining lodge area, maintenance area and ranger station are served by the same water source.  The source consists of deep water well.  The water distribution system conveys potable water through various sized water mains from the pump house.  A 100,000-gallon water storage reservoir and a 20,000-gallon water storage reservoir provide adequate water storage for all of the buildings and campgrounds at Big Spring.  A booster pump station is utilized in filling the reservoirs within the district system. The booster station has an existing backup well, which is not being utilized at this time.  The two reservoirs provide gravity pressure to the water distribution system serving all the loops of the campground, dining lodge, cabins and maintenance buildings.  Each loop of the campground has a comfort station and there are water hydrants scattered throughout the campgrounds. Updates on the system have been completed. New items include a new electrical panel, panel controls and complete system
water lines.

Round Spring Area: This system consists of a community well, 50,000-gallon and a 10,000-gallon water storage tank and chlorination system.  The system serves campgrounds, picnic areas, ranger station and maintenance buildings. The water storage reservoirs supply water by gravity to various sized water distribution mains.  The 10,000-gallon water storage tank is taken off line during the winter months.

No positive bactis in 2012 and good nitrate/nitrite levels.

description:
This Public Non-Community System (PWSID #137002) receives water from the City of Corpus Christi through a 15-mile water transmission line.  The storage and distribution system serves the Malaquite Beach facilities including the beach pavilion, RV campground, Gulf Ranger Station, employee residences, lifeguard station, and fee entrance station.

Potable water from the City of Corpus Christi flows from the Park boundary to a 100,000-gallon concrete water storage tank at Mile-and-a-Half.  Flow into the tank is controlled by an automatic valve, which is energized by float switches on the tank.  The Park monitors the chlorine residual of the water entering the tank and leaving the tank to the distribution system, and re-chlorinates as necessary.  The City of Corpus Christie has a chlorine boost station located immediately outside the park boundary and immediately prior to the park's service connection. The City of Corpus Christie gets its water from Choke Canyon Reservoir, Lake Corpus Christi, Lake Texana, and the Colorado River

A primary booster pump and two backup pumps at the Mile-and-a-Half reservoir pump water from the reservoir to the View Tower reservoir.  This pump creates a constant pressure in the line between the reservoirs.  When the View Tower reservoir is filling, the pressure in the water line drops causing a pressure switch to energize the Mile-and-a-Half booster pump.  When the View Tower reservoir is full, the automatic fill valve at View Tower closes, the pressure in the water transmission line rises causing the Mile-and-a-Half booster pump to de-energize.

The View Tower structure contains a 300,000-gallon concrete water storage reservoir, an emergency generator, a diesel operated fire pump, a pressure tank and booster pumps.  The upper part of the structure which houses the pumps, controls and generator is constructed of cinderblock walls with a brick face and also serves as the park's hurricane shelter for staff and visitors.  These facilities are designed to re-chlorinate and re-pressurize the system at Malaquite Beach as well as provide fire flow capacity to the system. Mercury float switches in the reservoir and a timer switch actuate a valve that controls the flow into the reservoir from the Mile-and-a-Half reservoir.  The timer is set to open the valve in the morning depending on the water level in the tank. Three booster pumps pressurize water from the View Tower reservoir into the pressure tank before it enters the distribution system.  Sodium hypochlorite solution is injected into the reservoir based on the chlorine residual concentration in the outlet line.  In the event of a power failure, the emergency generator provides power.
point_of_use: Beach pavilion, RV Campground, Gulf Ranger Station, Employee Residences, Lifeguard Station, Fee Entrance Station

water_system_name: 18 Sleeping Dog Road Park Housing Water System
description: The park purchased the house at 18 Sleeping Dog Road in 2020 for park housing from a private resident.  The water system consists of a well and pressure tank located inside a pumphouse that is mostly below grade.  The water system is not chlorinated.  The water system serves the park house and another private residence.  The housing unit will not be occupied until the home is connected to the County's community water system (estimated in 2024).

This non-public system serves the "Big House" and the Casita.  The well, located in the pumphouse, delivers water directly into a 10,000-gallon, galvanized steel water storage tank also located inside the pumphouse.  The well is powered with a solar array and grid backup.  A Dosmatic Superdose 20 hypochlorinator injects liquid sodium hypochlorite into the storage tank inlet line for disinfection.  The water is pressurized by three each 3 HP variable speed booster pumps and a pressure tank prior to the distribution system. The boiler and irrigation system at the Big House are both equipped with a RPZ backflow assembly.

This system serves the Headquarters Administration Building and picnic area comfort station.  Water from the well is pumped to the water treatment building where it is chlorinated using a Dosmatic SuperDos 20 hypochlorinator and pressurized in a 120 gallon Wellmate pressure tank.  Contact time is provided by a 120 gallon contact tank.  Water is used primarily for the public restrooms in the comfort station.  Water is also provided to the Administration Building, Maintenance Shop, and 2 RV sites.  The Maintenance Shop has a boiler equipped with a dual check valve backflow preventer with intermediate atmospheric vent..  The well is located on the west side of the picnic area.  An unchlorinated waterline serves 2 yard hydrants for making adobe blocks.
point_of_use: Headquarters Administration Building, maintenance shop, two RV sites, and picnic area comfort station

description: The trading post water system was installed in 2019/2020 and consists of a drilled well, 45 gallon steel pressure tank, 120 gallon composite chlorine contact tank, and a Dosatron D14WL3000NIE hydraulically operated chlorinator that injects sodium hypochlorite into the water system for disinfection.  The water system fills a plastic water storage tank located in the basement that is used for the fire suppression system.  This system only serves the Trading Post building, which consists of office space and a conference room for park staff.
point_of_use: Trading Post building, which consists of office space and a conference room for park staff

description: This system serves the Visitor Center restrooms and drinking fountain. A single 120 gallon pressure tank provides pressure and a 120 gallon contact tank provides contact time. The well pump is energized with a pressure switch.
