description:
Petrified Forest National Park purchases water from the Navajo Nation Tribal Utility Authority. Water is delivered to the northern boundary of the Park through an 8-inch water main. Water from the NTUA enters the Park through the master meter house and travels to one 500,000-gallon underground concrete potable water storage reservoir serving the Painted Desert area, located at the northern end of the Park. Potable water from this 500,000-gallon underground concrete potable water storage reservoir is pumped through a 6-inch water main to the Rainbow Forest area, located at the southern end of the Park. A pumphouse is located between the Painted Desert area and the Rainbow Forest area to monitor the water pressure and quality through the water main.

Master Meter House: Water from the Navajo Nation Tribal Utility Authority (NTUA) is pumped into the master meter house, located at the northside of the Park. Static water pressure is maintained at 120 PSI. A float inside the underground, 500,000-gallon concrete potable water storage reservoir (Painted Desert) activates a valve, located inside the master meter house, to open and fill this underground, reservoir with potable water.

Painted Desert Area: Once the valve from the master meter house opens, potable water is sent to the 500,000-gallon underground potable water storage reservoir, located on a hill to the north of the Painted Desert Visitor Center. This underground, concrete potable water storage reservoir consists of two, separate 250,000-gallon concrete compartments that can operate independently. Each compartment is filled utilizing float-controlled valves that open the water main to fill each compartment. Potable water is gravity fed to the Painted Desert area on the north side of the Park, serving the visitor center, Painted Desert Inn, Park headquarters, gift shop, Painted Desert Diner, the maintenance shop, and Park housing. This reservoir has a combined drain/overflow line. Additionally, potable water is distributed from this 500,000-gallon underground potable water storage reservoir to the southern end of the Park.

Puerco Pumphouse: Potable water leaves the Painted Desert 500,000-gallon underground potable water storage reservoir and travels to the Puerco Pumphouse (historic name kept by the Park) through a 6-inch water main for distribution to the southern end of the Park. The Puerco Pumphouse was constructed during the early 1990s. After entering the pumphouse, potable water travels through a valve to monitor the pressure and flow. A backup chlorine booster injection system is installed on the line downstream from the valve to increase the chlorine residual in the potable water distributed to the southern section of the Park. Rainbow Forest Area: Potable water is pumped from the Painted Desert 500,000-gallon concrete underground potable water storage reservoir through the Puerco Pumphouse into the 300,000-gallon concrete underground potable water storage reservoir located at the Rainbow Forest area in the south side of the Park. This underground potable water storage reservoir consists of two, separate 150,000-gallon concrete compartments that can operate independently. Each compartment is filled utilizing float-controlled valves that open the water main to fill each compartment. Potable water is gravity fed to the Rainbow Forest area on the south side of the Park serving the visitor center, museum, comfort station, snack bar, gift shop, and Park housing.
point_of_use: visitor center, painted desert inn, park heeadquarters, gift shop, painted desert diner, maintenance shop, park housing
source_location_refs: Google Earth coordinates for the visitor center used

description:
The park is supplied water by a ~1,600-foot deep well located behind the two administration buildings.  The 10 hp pump is located at approximately 300 feet below the surface. The water is softened and then chlorinated prior to being delivered to a buried 25,000 gallon concrete reservoir located on Pea Ridge about 4,700 feet north of the visitor center.  The water system has approximately 5,360 feet of steel/ cast iron pipe.  From the reservoir, the water distribution lines provide water to the visitor center, picnic area, and two administrative buildings.

PHS report indicates that the park possibly connected to city water in 2017, though that contradicts information about the 1600 ft deep well that is also used for water supply. Need to clarify details about this system.
point_of_use: visitor center, picnic area, two adminstrative buildings

description: This system consists of an outdoor well with a submersible pump. The well is housed inside a concrete cylinder and the discharge pipe is wrapped in insulation to protect it from freezing (water discharged from casing above the frost line). The well casing extends approximately two feet above ground. The surfacing around the casing is unimproved. The system has a water softener but no continuous disinfection/chlorination. The well is activated by a pressure switch and there is a steel pressure tank located in the treatment/utility room. This was a year-round system that servs the Five Forks Maintenance Shop, which is not continuously staffed with employees.

description: This system consists of a well located outdoors with a submersible pump (Goulds 7L515). The well is housed under a fake landscape rock and it is wrapped in insulation to protect it from freezing (water discharged at top of casing above the frost line). The well casing extends about two feet above ground and has a concrete apron. The well cap is a split ring style with a vent installed. Treatment consists of a water softener (CSI Model MS64) but there is no continuous disinfection via chlorination. The well is activated by a pressure switch and there are two steel pressure tanks located in the treatment room. This was a year-round system serving the Five Forks Visitor Contact Station.

park_name: Perry's Victory and International Peace Memorial
description: The Village of Put-in-Bay water system receives its drinking water from Lake Erie. The Village has its water intake and treatment plant on the south side of the island.  The Village completed in 2007 an Ohio EPA source water assessment of its intake and surrounding water drainage. Possible sources of contamination have been identified and corrected. In addition, an expanded emphasis on algal control with .231 inch screening of organics improves water quality to the filters.  New intake with air burst for zebra mussel control and expanded filtration with automated control provide the Village with excess capacity and storage for peak periods. Information on your drinking water may be obtained by contacting David R. Deleeuw, Water Superintendent at 419-285-8545. The Ohio EPA Northwest District Office in Bowling Green, Ohio has regulatory over sight of Put-in-Bay's water system. The Ohio EPA number in Bowling Green is 419-352-8461.
climate_change_water_resources: https://elpc.org/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/2019-ELPCPublication-Great-Lakes-Climate-Change-Report.pdf

description: This system serves a comfort station, drinking fountain,.  It consists of a groundwater well, disinfection equipment, two 120-gallon buried chlorine contact chambers, water distribution piping, and a 20,000-gallon above ground welded steel water storage tank.  A photovoltaic system with emergency propane backup generator provides electric power to all facilities.  Water is pumped by the well pump through a vault housing the disinfection equipment where sodium hypochlorite solution is injected.  The disinfected water then passes through the contact chambers before entering the distribution system piping.  There is no dedicated fill line to the water tank.  The water tank provides storage only, not volume for chlorine contact.
source_location_refs: Coordinates are for the comfort staion since no well was in our records.

description: This year-round system serves the park facilities at Bear Gulch and Chalone Creek.  It consists of a drilled well with submersible pump, sodium hypochlorite disinfection system, a 20,000-gallon above-ground welded steel water storage tank serving the Chalone area, and a 60,000-gallon above-ground welded steel water storage tank serving Bear Gulch facilities.  When called by the low water level probe in the Bear Gulch water tank, the Pilar well pump is energized.  Water is pumped from the well through the treatment building where sodium hypochlorite solution is injected before entering the distribution system.  The pump turns off when the high water level probe in the Bear Gulch water tank sends the signal that the tank is full.  There is no dedicated fill line to the Bear Gulch tank.  A float valve at the Chalone water tank controls flow into the tank.  A pressure reducer located in a separate building in the Chalone residential and maintenance area decreases the pressure between the Bear Gulch system and the Chalone water tank.
point_of_use: Bear Gulch and Chalone Creek facilities
source_description_links: Well Information and Key aq. Report: https://irma.nps.gov/DataStore/DownloadFile/476632

description: This year-round system serves the facilities at the Pinnacles Ranch Campground including a convenience store, swimming pool, yard hydrants throughout the campground, four comfort stations, an RV dump station, and a manager fts residence.  It consists of a drilled well with submersible pump, well house, chlorine disinfection equipment, a 50,000-gallon glasslined bolted steel water storage tank, and distribution system piping (4-inch pvc).  A level sensor in the water storage tank controls flow into the tanks.  At low water level, the well pump energizes and pumps water to the tank until the level sensor stops the flow and the pressure switch reaches shutoff pressure.  Water then flows by gravity through the distribution system piping to the campground facilities.  A reduced pressure backflow preventer is installed on the line leading to the old ranch.  A booster pressure system  raises pressure to the pool comfort station during periods of high demand.
point_of_use: convenience store, swimming pool, yard hydrants throughout the campground, four comfort stations, an RV dump station, and a manager fts residence

description: The water system serves the facilities at the new visitor center complex including the visitor center building, and residential structures for park staff.  It consists of a drilled well with submersible pump, water treatment building containing equipment for disinfecting the water supply, a potable water tank, a fire suppression/cooling water tank, and separate distribution piping for potable supply and fire suppression.  When called by the water tanks, the well pump is energized.  The water is pumped from the well through the treatment building where sodium hypochlorite is injected.  The treated water is pumped up to the water tanks for storage.  The water in the fire suppression tank will also be used for cooling the buildings on site.

water_system_name: CG Grand Marais Ranger Quarters Water System
description: Connected to city water.  Burt Township Water Department (Grand Marais) gets its water from underground, known as Ground Water, by way of two wells. They are both from the same watershed known as the Betsy-Chocolay Watershed. The U.S.G.S. cataloging unit # is 0402021. Upstream of our watershed is the Dead-Kelsey watershed, while downstream is the Waiska Watershed. The #1 well was abandoned in November of 2006 and is no longer is use. #2 well and our new #3 well are our main production wells. The new #3 well was installed in 2006. Wells #2 and #3 supplied 100 percent of our water for 2020. No chemicals are added to the water for treatment purposes before delivery to Grand Marais customers.

description:
The NPS operates the Transient Non-Community Water System on tribal lands.  Water system PWSID# is for a Tribal System (Kaibab Band of Paiute Tribe has primacy).  The well is located approximately 2 miles north of the visitor center.  The well is a 230 foot deep drilled well with a submersible pump.  The water after being pumped goes through a well house where it is injected with liquid sodium hypochlorite then transferred to a 500,000 gallon buried concrete storage tank through HDPE lines.  The storage tank has custom made tamper proof lids.  The water line does have connections to the tribal water system that are fitted with RPZ backflow preventers to prevent backflow into the NPS lines.

Backflow preventers are tested annually by a contractor that checks the fire sprinkler system.
point_of_use: Pipe spring facilities and tribal water system

description: The Bear Valley Visitor Center, comfort station, conference building, and several park administration buildings are served by a metered connection to the North Marin Water District water system.  The system consists of a metered connection to the NMWD, a reduced pressure backflow preventer at the service connection, a booster pump, 70,000-gallon bolted steel above ground water storage tank, and distribution system piping.  No additional treatment is provided.  The booster pump at the water service connection increases pressure to the park distribution system.  The water flows directly into the park distribution where it is utilized by water demand.  The water not consumed discharges into the water storage tank, which floats on the system.  The water tank provides storage for domestic potable use and fire flow.
point_of_use: Bear Valley Visitor Center, comfort station, conference building, and several park administration buildings

description: The water system serves five homes, horse stables, and the Stewart Horse Camp.  The facilities consist of an artesian well with submersible well pump, a 1¬Ω-inch dedicated fill line from the well to the water tank, a 9,200-gallon polyethylene water storage tank, a 5,000-gallon polyethylene water storage tank, and three 2,500-gallon polyethylene water storage tanks, three hypochlorinator, five 1¬Ω-horsepower booster pumps each equipped with a pressure tank, and PVC distribution piping.  A float switch in the water tank controls the well pump.  The source is a 194-foot, 6-inch drilled well installed in 1997 that periodically flows at about 10 gpm artesian.  The well is screened from 80 feet to 194 feet below the top of the casing.  It is located within 20 feet of surface water and has not been tested for surface water influence.  However, the Primacy Agency considers this a groundwater source.  The submersible well pump, when called by the float switch in the 9,200-gallon tank, pumps water from the well through the pumphouse where the hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite solution.  The disinfected water enters the 9,200-gallon tank, which provides storage and chlorine contact time.  Two pressurization systems with booster pumps and hydropneumatic tanks are used to deliver water to two areas.  One pressure system provides water to the Stewart Horse Camp.  The other delivers water to the remaining facilities.  The booster pump for the second pressure system pumps water to two 2,500-gallon tanks and one 5,000-gallon tank.  Individual pressure systems to four of the residences are fed off of these tanks.  Another booster pump pumps water to a 2,500-gallon tank which in turn provides water by gravity to the fifth residence.
point_of_use: five homes, horse stables, and the Stewart Horse Camp
source_location_refs: NPS Buildings Layer - Five Brooks Pumphouse

description: The water system serves three residences.  It consists of a surface water intake, 200-gallon polyethylene sedimentation tank, 1 ¬Ω inch hdpe water line, a 3,000-gallon polyethylene raw water storage tank, a booster pump, a 5,000-gallon polyethylene treated water storage tank, and distribution piping.  Water collected at an unprotected wooden intake structure flows by gravity first through the sedimentation tank to remove large particles, then to the 3,000-gallon tank.  A booster pump the water to the 5,000-gallon tank located on top of a hill overlooking the property.  Liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected into the water as it is pumped up to the 5,000-gallon tank.  Flow into the 5,000-gallon tank is controlled by a float switch in the tank that energizes the booster pump at low water level and de-energizes it at high water level.

description: The water system serves a single family residence.  It consists of a spring water source, a 5,000-gallon concrete water storage tank, a booster pump and pressure tank, and hypochlorinator.  Water flows by gravity from the unsealed spring box to the water storage tank.  The booster pump pressurizes the water for use in the house.  Chlorine is injected into the water for disinfection before entering the house.

This system serves the Pacific Coast Learning Center and fire management office building.  It consists of a drilled well, hypochlorinator for disinfection, an 8,000-gallon redwood water storage tank, a booster pump and pressure tank for fire suppression, and a separate booster pump and pressure tank for the fire management offices.  Water from the well is injected with sodium hypochlorite solution before it is discharged into the water storage tank.  The storage tank provides volume for contact time and water storage.  The booster pump and pressure tank pressurize water for use in the fire management office.  A separate pump and pressure tank provide fire protection.

description: The system provides water for Drakes Beach, North Beach, South Beach, and several ranches.  It consists of two shallow 45-foot 6-inch diameter drilled wells with capacities of 70 gpm each located at Gallagher Ranch, a treatment building with hypochlorinator, a 20,000-gallon buried concrete water storage tank, a 50,000-gallon buried concrete water tank, and distribution piping.  The system is controlled using a timer.  When called the well pump is energized and pumps water from the wells to the treatment building where a flow switch energizes the hypochlorinator to inject sodium hypochloride.  The treated water is diverted to two separate underground concrete reservoirs.  The water distribution system consists of various diameters of galvanized pipe and PVC.
point_of_use: Drakes Beach, North Beach, South Beach, and several ranches

description: The water system serves to a home, a commercial wharf, and the lifeboat station.  The source of this water is a 94-foot, 8-inch diameter drilled well with a capacity of 3 gpm.  The wellhead is enclosed in a pumphouse.  The water is treated for iron and manganese removal, and chlorinated before being pumped to a 25,000-gallon redwood storage tank via a dedicated fill line.  The older redwood tank has a plastic liner.  The water is fed by gravity to the facilities.
point_of_use: home, a commercial wharf, and the lifeboat station

description: This system serves the North District Operations Center.  It consists of a 200-foot 8-inch diameter well with submersible pump, disinfection equipment, a 6,000-gallon redwood water storage tank, a pressure system consisting of a booster pump and pressure tank, and distribution piping.  Water is pumped at a rate of about 13 gpm from the well through the treatment building where sodium hypochlorite solution is injection and discharges into the water storage tank.  A float switch in the tank controls the well pump.  A booster pump and pressure tank pressurize the water for use in the building.

This water system serves Residence 168.  It consists of a connection to the Stewart Horse Camp water system, a 500-gallon polyethylene water storage tank, a booster pump and hydropneumatic tank, and a hypochlorinator.  Water flows from the Stewart Horse Camp water storage tank to the 500-gallon tank at the residence.  Flow is controlled by a float valve in the 500-gallon tank.  The hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite into the water as it flows into the 500-gallon tank.  The booster pump, controlled by a pressure switch, pressurizes water for use in the residence.  Since this water is unfiltered surface water, the residents use bottled water for potable purposes.

This system provides water to both Glen Camp and Wildcat Camp.  It consists of a drilled well located at Glen Camp, chlorine disinfection equipment, 2-inch hdpe water transmission main, an upflow filter, a 3,000-gallon polyethylene water storage tank, and distribution piping to spigots serving each campground.  Water is pumped from the well through the upflow filter into the storage tank via a dedicated fill line.  The tank provides storage and chlorine contact time.  Liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected in the line near the wellhead.  Treated water then gravity flows to each campground via distribution piping.  All power for this remote system is from solar panels.  A float switch in the water tank automatically controls the operation of the well pump.
source_notes: Depth of the well is unknown but Water Resource Management Plan (1995) reports a horizontal well.

description: The water system serves a single family residence.  It consists of an unprotected spring, a 4,000-gallon polyethylene water storage tank, 2 booster pumps, a cartridge filtration system containing a 5 micron and 1 micron absolute filter, flow switch, disinfection equipment, a 500-gallon polyethylene water tank, and a pressure tank.  The first booster pump delivers water from the wooden spring box to the large water storage tank.  The untreated water flows by gravity to the treatment building where a booster pump pumps it through the two filters.  The flow switch energizes the hypochlorinator which injects sodium hypochlorite solution before discharging into the smaller tank.  The small tank provide volume for contact time.  A second booster pump then pressurizes the water for use in the distribution piping.

This system was installed in the late 1990's and consists of a 4-inch drilled well cased with 4-inch PVC, a 1,000-gallon polyethylene water storage tank, distribution piping to a single spigot that serves a small campground.  Water is pumped from the well to the storage tank.  The system is batch chlorinated in the storage tank as needed to maintain the required chlorine residual.  All power for this remote system is from solar panels.  A float switch in the water tank automatically controls the operation of the well pump.

This system is currently not in operation.  Water is hauled to the existing water tank and batch chlorinated to maintain minimum free chlorine residual.
source_notes: Depth of the well is unknown but Water Resource Management Plan (1995) reports a horizontal well.

description: This system serves the Hostel, Education Center, Limantour Beach, Coast Camp, and five park homes.  It consists of an 8-inch drilled well with a submersible pump that produces about 22 gpm, a hypochlorinator, an iron and manganese filtration system, a 55,000-gallon buried concrete water storage tank, and 6-inch PVC distribution piping.  Water is pumped from the well into the treatment building where it is chlorinated.  It then passes through the green sand filters to remove the iron and manganese.  The filter backwash effluent is discharged to a septic tank/drainfield system.  Filtered water is pumped to the water tank for storage and contact time before it is gravity fed to the distribution system.
point_of_use: Hostel, Education Center, Limantour Beach, Coast Camp, and five park homes

description: This water system serves Residence 164.  It consists of a horizontal well, a 1500-gallon polyethylene raw water storage tank, a booster pump and hydropneumatic tank, and a hypochlorinator.  Water flows from the horizontal well to the 1500-gallon tank.  Flow is controlled by a float valve in the 1500-gallon tank.  The booster pump, controlled by a pressure switch, pressurizes water for use in the residence.  Sodium hypochlorite is injected immediately before entering the house piping.
The system consists of a well, pressure tank, chlorine injector and a 500 gallon storage tank.

This Non-Public water system serves a single residence.  It consists of a drilled well, 5,000 gallon polyethylene water storage tank, hypochlorinator, booster pump with hydropneumatic tank, and distribution piping.  When the water level in the water storage tank reaches pump-on, the well pump energizes and fills the tank until the float switch reaches pump-off.  The untreated water flows by gravity to the 500 gallon tank located beneath the deck.  As it flows into this tank sodium hypochlorite is injected.  The tank provides volume for chlorine contact.  A booster pump and hydropneumatic tank pressurize the treated water for use in the residence.

description: The function of the Water Distribution Division is to transmit a supply of potable water through approximately 270-miles of various types and sizes of mains, valves, and hydrants to consumers at an economical rate.  The Distribution System dates back to the 1800's and the Water Distribution Division is constantly updating and replacing mains, valves, and hydrants to help ensure an uninterrupted flow of water to customers.  Main breaks, service leaks, and hydrant damage occurs from various causes and requires constant attention and repairs.  The Water Distribution Division installs all new service to areas within the system (i.e. new hook ups when a new house or commercial building is built).

water_system_name: 325323 Water System Green Mountain Onahu CRD
description: System went inactive in 2016.  The Green Mountain Water System services: 8 seasonal cabins; 3 RV sites with hookups; and a trail crew dorm.  Water is obtained from a drilled well located adjacent to the treatment building.  Water flows from the well through a pitless adapter to the treatment building where liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected.  Water is then pumped to a 5,000 gallon glass lined bolted steel tank.  From the storage tank water flows through gravity to the upper housing area and back down to the treatment building where it enters the rest of the distribution system.  Major upgrades to the system occurred in 2010 including removal of the well house, a new storage tank, and reconfiguring the distribution system.
point_of_use: 8 seasonal cabins; 3 RV sites with hookups; and a trail crew dorm
source_notes: Well was deconstructed prior to the well data being collected.

description: This system has been abandoned since 2019. The Longs Peak campground system services: a campground with 26 sites without hookups; 4 hose bibs in the campground; 1 comfort station in the campground (only used during summer); 1 host RV site with full hookups; a seasonal residence; a ranger station; and a comfort station in the upper area. This is a surface water system from Alpine Brook. Water is collected from a foot valve in the brook that flows through a check valve to an AO Smith 1/2 HP pump to an H2Pro (WH8) Expansion Tank to a water meter then through a 20 micron cartridge prefilter to a GE Homespring (UF211) ultrafilter, then through a backpressure valve with liquid sodium hypochlorite being injected to a 8,736 gallon glass lined bolted steel water tank. From the water tank water is distributed to both the upper and lower areas. There is an abandoned well located near the office but the treatment equipment has been removed.
point_of_use: Campground with 26 sites without hookups; 4 hose bibs in the campground; 1 comfort station in the campground (only used during summer); 1 host RV site with full hookups; a seasonal residence; a ranger station; and a comfort station in the upper area

water_system_name: 325351 Water System Homestead Ranch NSR CRD
description:
The Holzwarth (Never Summer Ranch) system services: two residences; a lunch room; and interpretive buildings.  Water is obtained from a drilled well that flows through a check valve to a Well-X-Trol hydropneumatic tank and then has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected into the water, prior to flowing into a galvanized welded steel chlorine contact tank with an air relief valve.  After the contact tank water flows through a meter to the distribution system.

In 2009 the classification of the system was changed from a Transient Non-Community Water System (CO0325351) to a Non-Public System and many of the service connections were turned off.
point_of_use: two residences; a lunch room; and interpretive buildings

description: The Deerhaven Ranger Station system supplies three residences.  Water is obtained from a drilled well with the pump placed at 120 feet.  Water flows from the well through a check valve and meter to a 20 micron cartridge prefilter and then has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected.  Water then flows into a fiberglass Wellmate WM-12 pressure tank and a fiberglass Wellmate WM-UT450 chlorine contact tank before going into distribution.  The water system used to be classified as a public water system (CO0307205), but was reclassified as a non-public water system in 1991.

The Headquarters West system services a housing area, maintenance area, corral, and Kawuneechee Visitor Center. Water is provided from a drilled well that flows through a check valve then through an Adedge Filter system for manganese and Rockwell Meter before it has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected. After chlorination, water flows to either the distribution system or to a 100,000 gallon in ground concrete storage tank that floats on the system.
point_of_use: housing area, maintenance area, corral, visitor center

description: Water for the Headquarters East system is obtained from one of two wells located in Moraine Park adjacent to the Big Thompson River. Each well has a submersible pump and the lines from each well meet in a common vault that then transfers the water to a treatment building located in the Moraine Park Campground. In the treatment building water flows through a check valve and has lime injected and then flows into a mixing tank with an air relief valve. It then has liquid sodium hypochlorite added as it progresses to a second mixing tank with an air relief valve. The water then progresses to an ion exchange tank with MTM (manganese dioxide filtering media) and garnet media. The line then splits with one line running to Tank A and the other to Tank B. The line proceeding to Tank A flows through a meter then to a 50,000 gallon tank. Tank A services the campground and the Moraine Park Livery. The Tank A portion of the system is a seasonal operation. The line heading to Tank B, branches off and feeds three residences and a museum building. The line continues onto Tank B which is a 10,000 gallon in ground concrete tank. Tank B then flows to the High Drive area which services 7 residences and fills a 3,000 gallon water storage tank from the bottom. Tank B also flows to a 100,000 and 150,000 gallon underground concrete tanks that are operated in series but can be isolated. These tanks service the headquarters, housing, maintenance, and livery operations in the headquarters area.
point_of_use: headquarters, housing, maintenance, and livery operations in the headquarters area.

It then has liquid sodium hypochlorite added as it progresses to a second mixing tank with an air relief valve. Tank B also flows to a 100,000 and 150,000 gallon underground concrete tanks that are operated in series but can be isolated. These tanks service the headquarters, housing, maintenance, and livery operations in the headquarters area.
point_of_use: headquarters, housing, maintenance, and livery operations in the headquarters area.

description: The Green Pipeline system services six seasonal NPS residences and seven inholding residences.  The system consists of a drilled well, water is received from the well and flows through a Master meter and check valve to an air relief valve.  After the valve, liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected into the water and the water flows to a 5,000 gallon fiberglass storage tank.  From the storage tank water flows to the distribution system.  The water system used to be classified as a public water system (CO0335324), but was reclassified as a non-public water system in 2006.
source_description_links: Well information and key aq report: https://irma.nps.gov/DataStore/DownloadFile/550867

water_system_name: 325743  Water System Timber Creek Campground CRD
description: The Timbercreek Campground system services: a 97 unit campground without hookups but having hose bibs spread throughout; a duplex residence; four comfort stations; and a RV dump station.  Water is obtained from a drilled well located in a wellhouse that flows through a check valve to a Hersey Meter and has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected.  After chlorination water flows through a ductile iron and HDPE line to the storage tank and distribution system.  The storage tank is a 42,000 gallon glass lined bolted steel tank that floats on the system.  There is a SCADA system installed between the tank and the well controlling when water is produced.

For the NPS system, water is obtained from a drilled well that flows through a Badger meter, a check valve, has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected, then flows into a Well-X-Trol WX251 pressure tank. From the pressure tank it flows to two galvanized steel chlorine contact tanks operated in series and then to the distribution system which is composed of galvanized lines.

description: The Leifer cabin had been previously serviced by both a hand dug well and a drilled well. This water system (CO0325471) went inactive in 1991. It now receives hauled water from Estes Park delivered by an independent hauler. Water is received in a 1,000 gallon plastic tank that flows through a check valve to a Best (Model 94L105) pump to the residence.

description: The water system at Aspenglen campground services a campground (4 comfort stations, 50 campsites without hookups, and 2 seasonal residences) and the Smith Sisters area (2 seasonal residences).  Water is obtained from a drilled well and flows through a pitless adaptor to the treatment building.  In the building water flows through a meter and has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected into the water.  The water can then flow through valves to either the campground, which continues to the campground storage tank (34,000 gallon glass lined bolted steel tank) feeding the campground facilities or to the Smith Sisters line.  The Smith Sisters line flows through a check valve, is pressurized by a hydropneumatic tank and then flows through 2 - Wellmate SP-50 (264 gallon capacity) tanks operated in series to aid in chlorine contact time.  The water then flows through a separate meter and to the Smith Sisters residences.
point_of_use: Aspenglen campground services a campground (4 comfort stations, 50 campsites without hookups, and 2 seasonal residences) and the Smith Sisters area (2 seasonal residences)

description: The McGraw Ranch Area consists of a research center, 1 permanent house, and 9 seasonal housing units, and a lab used by researchers.  Water is obtained from a drilled well that flows through a meter and has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected as it goes to  an in ground concrete 60,000 gallon storage reservoir.  From the storage tank the water flows back to the distribution system in an 8 inch HDPE line.  The water system used to be classified as a public water system (CO0235384), but was reclassified as a non-public water system in 1988.
point_of_use: Research center, 1 permanent house, and 9 seasonal housing units, and a lab used by researchers

description: The Bighorn Ranger Station is supplied by a drilled well that transfers water through an HDPE line to a check valve then to a meter. Water then flows through a prefilter has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected and into a Wellmate WM-12 hydropneumatic tank. It also flows into a Wellmate UT-120 chlorine contact tank.  Water then flows through one of three galvanized lines to the Ranger Station, Trailer, or Entrance Gate. The water system used to be classified as a public water system (CO0335144) but was reclassified as a non-public water system in 2005.

The Twin Owls system services a NPS residence.  The system consists of a drilled well that flows to a meter and check valve, then has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected into the the water and into a Wellmate SP50 fiberglass chlorine contact tank.  From the chlorine contact tank water flows to an Aire-E-Tainer Model 244 hydropneumatic tank then through copper lines in the house.  The water system used to be classified as a public water system (CO0335761), but was reclassified as a non-public water system in 2016.

The Bear Lake Area is serviced by a 92 foot drilled well that is located approximately 50 feet from surface water.  Water comes from the well and flows through a check valve to a meter.  After the meter, liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected into the water.  Water is then transferred to a 5,000 gallon fiberglass storage tank through an HDPE line.  As water is needed it flows back from the tank to the treatment building to a drinking fountain.
source_notes: Drilling log reports that the aquifer is allvium, but the well was drilled from 205-265 ft in an unknown conglomerate, possibly the Troublesome Formation
source_location_refs: Coordinates for the Ranger Kiosk used since no well data was available.

description:
The water system at Glacier Basin Campground services a 149 site and 14 group site campground without hookups, and a couple of host sites with hookups, an RV dump station, 11 comfort stations, hose bibs throughout the campground, a ranger station, two seasonal residences, the livery and bunkhouses at Glacier Creek, and a picnic area and comfort station at Sprague Lake.

Water is obtained through a drilled well and passes through a filter housing (used to contain a strainrite bag filter (HPM99-CC-2SR)) to a UV system (Sanitron Model #S2400B) to a meter and a check valve and has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected into the water.  The water line then splits with one lateral running to the Glacier Basin Campground, where the water flows through an automatic valve, a check valve, a meter, and then to a 57,000 gallon glass lined bolted steel water tank that then flows back to the distribution system.

The other lateral first flows past the livery to Sprague Lake.  The water passes through an automatic valve and a meter at the treatment building then progresses downstream.  At the livery the water is teed off of the main line and flows through a 50 micron cartridge filter to a variable feed drive pump to two Air-E-Tainer (Model 566) hydropneumatic tanks and then splits to the men's bunkhouse and through a pressure reducing valve to the women's bunkhouse.

The line continues further downstream to Sprague lake.  As the water enters the Sprague Lake area it flows through a check valve, a meter, and then to a 5,000 gallon above ground fiberglass tank that floats on the system.
point_of_use: Glacier Bay Campground, ranger station, two seasonal residences, livery, bunkhouses, picnic area, comfort station

description: The Hidden Valley area is serviced by water from a drilled well that flows through a meter to a check valve and has liquid sodium hypochlorite injected to a buried 26,000 gallon fiberglass tank.  Water then flows back down by gravity to either the distribution or fire systems.  The system services a comfort station.

water_system_name: 335510 Water System Mill Creek Ranger Station
description:
The Mill Creek Ranger Station is serviced by a drilled well that produces water at 1.8 GPM.  Water flows from the well through a check valve to a water meter.  After the meter liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected into the water.  Water then flows into a Mark IV CM202 hydropneumatic tank then to a welded steel tank with an air relief valve.  From the first tank it can flow to a second welded steel tank with air relief valve or into a 1/2 horsepower motor to a Well-X-Trol WX251 hydropneumatic tank or to the distribution system.

The system serves a year-round ranger station and a seasonal residence.  The water system used to be classified as a public water system (CO0335510), but was reclassified as a non-public water system in 1989.

description: The Visitor Center water supply is from a well that was drilled in 2010 with a total depth of 360 feet.The well pump is activated by a pressure tank switch. There is a chemical feed system for disinfection with sodium hypochlorite and a 120 gallon contact tank. The staff kitchen skin has a reverse osmosis unit and sediment filter. The upstairs is a living quarters unit with 2 bedrooms, a bathroom, and kitchenette. The water at this location has had an exceedance of uranium above the EPA SDWA maximun contaminant limit (MCL) of 30Œºg/L and radon above the 4,000 pCi/L (note: generally, radon is more of an inhalation concern than ingestion concern). Past water sample results can be located on the ACAD 2018 report conducted by the Office of Public Health. Signs have been posted indicating the water is non-potable since it was discovered that uranium and radon exceed the MCL and the park is currently exploring treatment options.The water is used year-round by the staff that live on site.

water_system_name: Rincon Mountain District Fire Facility Water System
description: The Rincon Mountain Fire Facility, located to the north of the Visitor Center, is supplied by potable water from the Tucson Community Water System. Potable water enters along the north side of the fire facility base through a 6-inch cast iron water main. Potable water passes through a meter then a 6-inch Febco LF860 reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device prior to entering the fire facility. Potable water supplies one men's restroom with two toilets, one urinal, one shower, and one sink; one women's restroom with two toilets, one shower, and two sinks; one eye wash; one utility sink; one water fountain and bottle fill; and a fire suppression system.

description: This transient, non-community water system serves the Tucson Mountain District (West District) of the Park. The well providing water for the Tucson Mountain District water system is a 660-feet drilled well. The submersible pump, Goulds model no. 10GS50 is located approximately 600 feet below this surface, and pumps at a rate of approximately 16 gallons per minute. The wellhead is located in an enclosed locking structure to the northeast of the water treatment room. A four foot by four foot concrete apron surrounds the well. The enclosed metal structure housing the wellhead is secured with two locks. Water from the drilled well enters through a meter, passes through a check valve, undergoes treatment using liquid sodium hypochlorite, and then is sent to distribution through a 6-inch main line which decreases to 3 inches at the inlet of the 50,000-gallon underground potable water reservoir. Water is then distributed throughout the Park using a 3-inch PVC line. This water system serves the Red Mountain Visitor Center, comfort station at the Visitor Center, Operations Building, Education Building, living quarters 31, and living quarters 32.
point_of_use: Red Mountain Visitor Center, comfort station at the Visitor Center, Operations Building, Education Building, living quarters 31, and living quarters 32

description: All water for the Rincon Mountain District (RMD) of Saguaro National Park (SAGU) is provided by the Tucson Water municipal source. Water is delivered by the municipal source through an 8-inch HDPE water main. Water then passes through an 8-inch reduced pressure zone (RPZ) backflow prevention device and then branches to various service connections in the developed area of the Park. One branch of the water main supplies the maintenance building, while the other 6-inch water main branch supplies the headquarters and Visitor Center buildings.

description: The Manning Camp Water System is a non-public water system that serves the cabin at Manning Camp, located on Mica Mountain at approximately 8,000 feet of elevation. No potable water serves this cabin and water is only provided when there is a demand for water (e.g., firefighting or trail work). Use of Manning Camp is seasonal, with an operation period typically from late March to mid-November. The water source a Manning Camp is a natural spring. There is a 15-foot-deep hole that is a reliable source of water during the drier months. Water is pumped from the source into a small concrete box with percolation holes to trap organic matter. After passing through the percolation holes, spring water enters the pumphouse where it first passes through a 50-micron filter, then a 5 micron filter, and finally through a 1-micron absolute filter in series. Following filtration, filtered spring water is pumped to an above-ground 2,600-gallon plastic potable water storage tank. There is a spigot next to this non-potable water storage tank that will drain the contents inside this storage reservoir. Non-potable water is then gravity fed to a cabin for staff use only. A spigot is provided next to the cabin for staff use. Additionally, a rain catchment cistern is provided adjacent to the cabin spigot to collect non-potable rainwater for additional water supply. Staff stated this water is not used for culinary, drinking, or hygienic purposes. This rainwater catchment cistern is not connected to the spring water source.
The park is served by the municipal water system (Oyster Bay Water District).

Direct connection to health hazard with no/improper backflow protection in place (DL 5)
Backflow device for direct connection to health hazard not checked annually by certified backflow tester (DL 4)

description:
This system consists of a drilled well (ID 34N/3W-2P2), submersible pump, meter, continuous chlorination, chem. Pro-OX Commercial Iron Filter System. The static water level was 53 ft.   This system provides service to two VIP trailer pads, a comfort station at the Visitor Center, and two new drinking fountains and a water filling station at the Visitor Center.  There are future plans to add six RV camp sites with water/sewer hookup. 
A new Visitor Center was built in 2020/2021 with a grand opening in May 2021.  Staff have installed a new pressure tank two years ago. Staff were adding additional straps to make the tank more secure.  Staff upgraded the iron filter (Pro-OX Commercial Iron Filter System) two weeks before our site visit.  The last filter has exceeded its lifespan.

description: The San Cristobal Rainwater System collects rainwater from a catchment system integrated within the Castillo San Cristobal.  The catchment system includes the historic cistern system original to the fort.  Water is stored in a series of four cisterns, each with a capacity of 145,000 gallons.  Rainwater first enters the first two cisterns, which operates jointly, and then is pumped to the final two cisterns.  From the final set of cisterns, water is pumped through a 5-micron cartridge filter, carbon filter, UV disinfection system, two hydropneumatic tanks (120 and 150 gallons) and into the distribution system that is dedicated to flushing toilets and urinals within Castillo San Cristobal.  The water delivered by this system is labeled as non-potable.
point_of_use: Flushing toilets and urinals within Castillo San Cristobal

description: The San Juan National Historic Site receives water from the San Juan Water System, which is administered by the Autoridad de Acueductos y Alcantarillados.  This municipal water system serves all potable water sources within the Park, which includes five staff quarters, Park Headquarters, water fountains, comfort stations, and visitor centers at both the Castillo San Felipe del Morro and Castillo San Cristobal locations.  At three locations, the Park has additional systems to store and treat the municipal water supply to ensure that water is available during periods of service disruption.  (1) At Castillo San Felipe del Morro, municipal water is used to fill a cistern.  Water is drawn from the cistern and pumped through two 80 gallon hydropneumatic tanks, a 5-micron cartridge filter, and four 200 gallon hydropneumatic tanks.  This system has a chlorine injection system after the 5-micron filter, but its use has been discontinued.  (2) The municipal water used at the Castillo San Cristobal Visitor Center flows into four 600 gallon plastic tanks, pumped (dual pumps) through two 120 gallon hydropneumatic tanks, a 5-micron cartridge filter, and into the Visitor Center distribution piping.  This system has a chlorine injection system after the 5-micron filter, but its use has been discontinued.  (3) The municipal water used for the remainder of services at Castillo San Cristobal municipal directly serves faucets and is also used to fill a 145,000 gallon cistern.  From the cistern, water is pumped through two hydropneumatic tanks (85 and 120 gallons), two 5-mircron cartridge filters, and a 125 gallon hydropneumatic tank before returning to the San Cristobal potable water distribution system.  This system has a chlorine injection system after the 5-micron cartridge filters, but its use has been discontinued.
point_of_use: all potable water sources within the Park, which includes five staff quarters, Park Headquarters, water fountains, comfort stations, and visitor centers at both the Castillo San Felipe del Morro and Castillo San Cristobal locations
source_notes: Filters are not 1 micron absolute or smaller or doesn't remove cryptosporidium (DL 5). Municipal water that is stored in large cisterns is filtered through 5-mircon cartridges filters prior to use. The cisterns have the potential for slow turnover, chorine dissipation, and entry of vectors (i.e. insects, rodents, etc.). In addition, the initial water quality as delivered from the San Juan Water System is questionable.

park_name: Santa Monica Mountains National Recreation Area
description: The water system serves a ranger residence.  It consists of a groundwater well, treatment building containing a hypochlorinator, a 10,000-gallon bolted steel water storage tank, and distribution piping.  Water is pumped from the well through the treatment building where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution before it discharges into the water storage tank via a dedicated fill line.  The tank provides storage and chlorine contact for the water before it enters the distribution system.  The well pump is controlled by a float valve located in the water storage tank.  The hypochlorinator is energized when the well pump is called.
source_notes: Depth of the well is not known, however in Levy and Korkosz (1997) It is reported that due to limited rainfall, all water comes from consolidated bedrock.

park_name: Santa Monica Mountains National Recreation Area
description: This system serves the contact station, two park employee residences, a maintenance shop, a basketball court, the Ranch House, the pool house, and a group campground.  It consists of a 6-inch diameter, 200-foot deep well, a hypochlorinator for disinfection, a 100,000-gallon bolted steel water storage tank, a 10,000-gallon bolted steel water storage tank, two booster pumps, and distribution piping.  Water is pumped from the well by the submersible pump, through the treatment building where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution, and discharges via a dedicated fill line into the 100,000-gallon water storage tank.  Water then flows by gravity to serve the contact station, two park employee residences, the maintenance shop, basketball court, Ranch House, pool house, and the group campground.  A booster pump pumps water from the distribution piping into the 10,000-gallon water storage tank, which provides storage for one residence.  This pump is controlled with a high/low water level valve.  A small booster pump pressurizes the water for this residence.  This pump is energized by a pressure switch on the discharge side of the pump.

description: This system provides water only for employee hand washing at the visitor contact station.  A water system consists of a well (drilled 2006), 80 gallon pressure tank, and service line to the visitor contact station.  A 50 micron and 1 micron absolute water filter were installed in the pumphouse in 2017 due to concerns that well may be influenced by surface water from the 2010 microscopic particulate analysis (MPA).  The MPA test indicated a low risk per the EPA Consensus Method and the State did not require filtration.  The well pump is controlled by a pressure switch, which operates the pump between 40 and 60 psi.  The 210 foot, 4-inch diameter drilled well is installed adjacent to the pumphouse in the picnic area.  The distribution system consists of a 1¬Ω-inch polyethylene water service line to the visitor contact station.

description:
The Quarai water system is a Public Non-Community Transient system that serves the visitor center and adjacent comfort station.  The system consists of a well, water treatment system, a 1,050-gallon polyethylene water storage tank, pressurization system, and a water service line.  A booster pump and pressure tank pressurizes the distribution system.  The water is disinfected using sodium hypochlorite solution.  Electrodes located in the water storage tank are hard wired to a control panel to initiate well pump cycles. There are three electrodes for pump-on, pump-off, and low water level alarm.  

 The pressure system consists of a 1 horsepower booster pump and a 45-gallon bladder-type hydropneumatic tank.  A pressure switch that is set to operate the pump between 40 and 60 psi controls the booster pump.
point_of_use: visitor center and adjacent comfort station
source_location_refs: Coordinates of the visitor center were used.

description:
The Gran Quivira water system is a Public Non-Community Transient water system that provides water to the visitor center, adjacent comfort station, residential area, and 2 RV sites.  The system consists of a well, pumphouse, water storage tank, and water distribution system.  A submersible well pump pumps water through the pumphouse (constructed in 1999) where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite.  It then passes through a dedicated 2-inch PVC water line to fill a 50,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank.  The tank was renovated in 2020.  Water flows by gravity from the tank into the water distribution system, which consists of 6-inch asbestos cement (AC) pipe that is reduced to a 2-inch PVC pipe from the old visitor center to the new visitor center.  System controls consist of electrodes located in the water storage tank that are hard wired to a control panel located in the pumphouse.  Three electrodes control pump-on, pump-off, low water level alarm, and high water level alarm.

A below grade double-check valve assembly provides backflow protection for the visitor center interior fire suppression system.
point_of_use: visitor center, adjacent comfort station, residential area, and 2 RV sites

description: The majority of this park was served by this well which includes the visitor center, maintenance shop, the administrative building/ranger station, and the cultural resources site. The well was located outdoor. The well head stood 22 inches above ground and was equipped with a well cap cover that was of a sanitary design. The well had a submersible pump and water flowed into the pump house where it flowed into a sediment filter, into a sulfur removal system which included 2 tanks, and to a pressure tank. Water then entered the lower level of the visitor center where a chlorine solution was directly injected into the line. The water was stored in 6- 600 gallon storage tanks. There was a notice on the taps and fountains that stated: " Water Contains Sodium In Excess of 20 mg/L And Should Not Be Consumed By People On Severely Restricted Sodium Diets."

description: This public community system uses surface water from Alder Creek springs and ground water from Alder Creek Well to provide water to the Ash Mountain Developed area.  The well water is disinfected with sodium hypochloride prior to entering the aboveground 3 50,000 concrete tanks.  The spring water has a polymer added and then flows through mixed media pressure filters and is disinfected with sodium hypochloride prior to entering the aboveground concrete reservoir. An additional 100,000 gallon reservoir at the housing area receives water from the concrete reservoir.  Backwash water from the pressure filters is collected in an 8000-gallon steel tank and gradually emptied into the sewer system. Average daily water use for the system is 25,000 gallons per day (gpd) with peak summer use typically 60,000 to 70,000 gpd. This system has a turbidity limit of 0.14 NTU and collects 5 bacteriological samples per month of operation.
db_comments: There are 2 nearby wells in USGWD but unclear which, if either, is the source. Used nearby wells for likely aquifer reference thoiugh.

This water system serves Big Meadows lodge, restaurant, wayside, campstore, cabins, and campground (including two RV sites and an RV dump station). The source for this water system is primarily a spring, with wells used as a backup source. Water from the spring source flows into spring box # 1 and spring box # 2. After the spring boxes, water flows into a 7,500-gallon buried concrete reservoir with a submersible pump. Well # 9 is located inside building 17-34. This well has a submersible pump, a sanitary well cap and casing raised 12 inches from the ground and is operated by a timer. Well # 14 is located inside building 17-35. This well has a submersible pump, a sanitary well cap and casing raised 12 inches from the ground and is operated by a timer.The timers operating these pumps prevent over pumping. Treatment consists of disinfection with sodium hypochlorite. Chemical feed equipment is located inside the spring house and the well buildings. The chemical feed pumps are activated when the source pumps activate. Finished potable water is stored inside three, buried, concrete storage tanks: two are 50,000 gallons and one is 100,000 gallons. These tanks are equipped with automatic tank level controls. Potable water is distributed to the distribution system gravity. Additional wells at this site include well # 16 (inside building 17-36) and well # 17 (outdoors).

The source for this water system is primarily a spring, with wells used as a backup source. Water from the spring source flows into spring box # 1 and spring box # 2. After the spring boxes, water flows into a 7,500-gallon buried concrete reservoir with a submersible pump. Well # 9 is located inside building 17-34. This well has a submersible pump, a sanitary well cap and casing raised 12 inches from the ground and is operated by a timer. Well # 14 is located inside building 17-35. This well has a submersible pump, a sanitary well cap and casing raised 12 inches from the ground and is operated by a timer.The timers operating these pumps prevent over pumping. Treatment consists of disinfection with sodium hypochlorite. Chemical feed equipment is located inside the spring house and the well buildings. The chemical feed pumps are activated when the source pumps activate. Finished potable water is stored inside three, buried, concrete storage tanks: two are 50,000 gallons and one is 100,000 gallons. These tanks are equipped with automatic tank level controls. Potable water is distributed to the distribution system gravity. Additional wells at this site include well # 16 (inside building 17-36) and well # 17 (outdoors).
point_of_use: Big Meadows lodge, restaurant, wayside, campstore, cabins, and campground (including two RV sites and an RV dump station)

description: This is a spring system that serves the comfort station and yard hydrants in the Dundo picnic area. The system includes a spring box, an overflow box (that drains into the nearby creek), a 26,000-gallon underground concrete reservoir tank, and disinfection equipment, which is located in an underground vault. Each yard hydrant in the picnic area is currently equipped with a boil water advisory sign for users.

source_notes: 2022 PHA report recommends these three options for the water system: drill a well to replace the spring water, install a filtration system prior to disinfection, close the water system and install vault toilets at the picnic area. description: The system consists of two wells located in two different pump houses. Well #1 is located in pump house #17-10 and has a submersible pump, sanitary well cap, and casing raised at least 12 inches above the floor. Well # 2 is located in pump house #17-11 and has a submersible pump, sanitary well cap, and casing raised at least 12 inches above the floor. Each pump house contains disinfection equipment (sodium hypochlorite). The well pumps are activated by tank level controls. Water is treated and then pumped to a 50,000-gallon high-lift tank. description: The primary source for this water system is a spring. Additionally, a drilled well serves as a backup source for this water system. The spring has a 5,000-gallon concrete, underground storage tank with two pumps, one submersible and one turbine (reported as alternating with the turbine pump operated in the winter). Well #2 is located inside building 17-16. It has a submersible pump and casing raised a minimum of 12 inches above the ground. This well is operated by a time clock scheduled to run two hours per day. The capacity of this well is approximately 24 gallons per minute. Treatment consists of the addition of polyphosphate and disinfection with sodium hypochlorite. Chemical feed equipment is installed inside the spring house and the well building. Chemical feed pumps activate when the source pumps turn on. Finished potable water is stored inside three potable tanks: a 200,000-gallon metal above ground tank that overflowed into a 50,000-gallon buried concrete tank that overflowed into another 50,000-gallon buried concrete tank. These tanks were equipped with automatic level controls. Potable water flows by gravity to the distribution system. Other wells at this site are well #1, located inside building 17-37; well #6, located inside building 17-33; and well #5, located inside building 17-32. This system supplies the Skyland lodge, cabins, restaurant, and comfort stations.
point_of_use: Skyland lodge, cabins, restaurant, and comfort stations

description: The entirety of Stones River National Battlefield receives potable water from the City of Murfreesboro Water Resources.  The water supply originates from the East Fork Stones River and from Percy Priest Reservoir. The Stones River Water Treatment Plant has the capacity to treat 20 million gallons per day of drinking water.  On an average day, over 10 million gallons of drinking water is softened, disinfected, filtered and fluoridated.  The plant features state-of-the-art equipment such as 0.1 micron membrane filtration, granular activated carbon contactors, and onsite hypochlorite generation.  This municipal water supply serves the Visitor Center, two staff housing units, an office, and maintenance shop.
point_of_use: Visitor Center, two staff housing units, an office, and maintenance shop

description:
The water system at the Monument serves the visitor center, residences, maintenance shop, the Crater Comfort Station, and the Bonito Forest Service campground located across the road from the visitor center.

Water for the Monument is purchased from the Doney Park Water Company.  The facility is classified as a distribution system which requires a minimum of two bacteriological water samples monthly. The Doney Park Water Company does not chlorinate the water, which is acceptable under the Regulations of the Arizona State Department of Environmental Quality.  The staff at the Monument installed a hypochlorinator at the base of the reservoir.  Chlorination provides residual protection for any future contamination. Water for Sunset Crater is obtained by pumping water each evening from a pumping station located at Doney Park through the 2.5 miles of water line.  The pumping is started by a time clock.  An automatic chlorinator adds chlorine.  There is no automatic control; a valve is opened at the inlet of the system before Doney Water begins pumping.  The reservoir is a 50,000 gallon, 100-foot elevated steel tank that was erected in 1965.  The chlorinator is located in a building constructed at the base of the water tower.

The park used to have its own public water system (AZ0403715), but this system was taken offline in 1977.
point_of_use: visitor center, residences, maintenance shop, the Crater Comfort Station, and the Bonito Forest Service campground located across the road from the visitor center
source_description_links:
https://www.doneyparkwater.com/, PHS Report,

description:
The old abandoned existing water system consists of two hand-dug wells under the influence of surface water, pumps, cisterns and various sized distribution piping. The wells are located approximately one half mile east of the Preserve.  These older wells are constructed with mortared stone and are approximately 20 feet in depth.  The stone allows water to seep into the well.

There are also two old historic springs that are fenced to keep livestock and people out.  The springs are not used for potable water.

description:
The park water systems is connected to Strong City water system that consists of three wells (~350 deep) and serves about 250 customers one of which is the rural water company that serves an additional 100 plus locations ( 1MG/month).
The park has 435 feet of 3-inch PVC that connects to the Strong City/Rural Water system.  The mainline is treated with chlorine from an LMI chemical injection pump as it flows through the pipe that is connected to two Grundfus booster pumps, one 200 gallon pressure tank, and a 60,000 gallon underground reservoir.  The reservoir serves as potable water supply and fire suppression supply.

description:
This transient, non-community water system serves all the facilities in the Park including the Visitor Center, the picnic area comfort station, the Park headquarters building, the four Park housing units, and the Park maintenance buildings. The source of water is from a well drilled during 1963 (ID # 55-629108). This well is drilled to a depth of 140 feet at a diameter of 6 inches. This 6-inch casing is torch perforated from a depth of 30 feet to the bottom of the 140-foot drilled well. A sanitary seal is installed to a depth of 5 feet. Water is pumped from the well, located inside the water treatment house, through a submersible pump. Water undergoes a softening process utilizing a Advanced Water Systems water softening, installed in 2000, and is then treated with a sodium hypochlorite solution. Potable water is sent to a 50,000-gallon buried concrete potable water storage reservoir. Potable water flows by gravity to the picnic area comfort station, the maintenance buildings, and the four Park housing units. A booster pump inside the pumphouse lifts water from this 50,000-gallon buried concrete potable water reservoir to a 25,000-gallon above ground welded-steel potable water storage reservoir located uphill from the visitor center. Float switches inside the buried concrete potable water storage reservoir control the submersible well pump. A float switch inside the 25,000-gallon above ground welded-steel potable water storage reservoir.

The distribution system consists of 1.25-inch galvanized pipe, 4- and 6-inch asbestos cement (AC) pipe, and 2-inch schedule 80 PVC pipe.  Four-inch AC pipe runs from the 50,000-gallon storage tank to the picnic area comfort station, maintenance area, and residential area.  A 4-inch PVC line runs from the transfer pump located in the pumphouse to the visitor center parking lot.  A 6 inch AC line connects the 25,000 gallon storage tank to the distribution system
point_of_use: isitor Center, the picnic area comfort station, the Park headquarters building, the four Park housing units, and the Park maintenance buildings

description: This transient non-community (TNC) water system serves the Mission/Visitor Center (North) side of the Park including: the Visitor Center (kitchen sink, three restrooms) and the Boundy House residence (kitchen sink, one toilet, one restroom sink, and a shower). Water is supplied from a 125 foot deep drilled well with a static water level of 13 feet. This well is identified as well # 1. A submersible pump set at 60 feet with a capacity of 12 gallons-per-minute (0.5 horsepower) supplies the well. Water is then treated with a sodium hypochlorite solution utilizing a Pulsatron A-Plus series injection pump. Following chlorination, the potable water line splits. One line is pumped through Pump # 1 to an Well-X-Trol Amtrol 100 PSI pressure tank. The other line is pumped through Pump # 2 to an Well-X-Trol Amtrol 100 PSI pressure tank. Potable water from this line passes through a Febco 825YA reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device prior to it fts storage inside a 50,000 underground, concrete potable water storage reservoir where it is held for distribution. Potable water is distributed through the Mission/Visitor Center (North) side of the Park through 6-inch PVC main lines and HDPE service line piping.
point_of_use: Mission/Visitor Center (North) side of the Park including: the Visitor Center (kitchen sink, three restrooms) and the Boundy House residence (kitchen sink, one toilet, one restroom sink, and a shower)

description: This non-public water system serves the Maintenance/Administrative (South) side of the Park including: the maintenance complex (fire suppression system, four hose bibs, one toilet, one restroom sink, and one eye wash), the casita (kitchen sink, one toilet, one restroom sink, and a bathtub), and the administrative office (fire suppression system, one breakroom sink, one toilet, and one restroom sink). Water is supplied from a 138 foot deep drilled well with a static water level of 13 feet. A submersible pump with a capacity of 70 gallons-per-minute (5 horsepower) supplies the well. Water is then treated with a sodium hypochlorite solution and pressurized through a 1,000-gallon pressure tank, located adjacent to the wellhead. Potable water is then sent to either storage inside an above ground, plastic, 1,800-gallon potable water storage reservoir; or sent for storage for fire suppression. Following storage inside the 1,800-gallon potable water storage reservoir, water is sent for distribution. All potable water for fire suppression passes through a Febco LF825Y reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device and is then pumped using a diesel motor to a 50,000-gallon, above ground welded steel potable water storage reservoir for fire suppression.
point_of_use: Maintenance/Administrative (South) side of the Park including: the maintenance complex (fire suppression system, four hose bibs, one toilet, one restroom sink, and one eye wash), the casita (kitchen sink, one toilet, one restroom sink, and a bathtub), and the administrative office (fire suppression system, one breakroom sink, one toilet, and one restroom sink)

description: This transient, non-community water system serves the entire Tuzigoot National Monument. This includes the Visitor Center; comfort station with a men fts restroom, women fts restroom, and custodial closet; the maintenance shop; the Park irrigation system; and the two Park housing units. The source of this water system is an approximately 160-feet deep, 6-inch drilled well located inside the water treatment building, located underneath the visitor center. Water is pumped from this well through two, high-frequency submersible pumps. Each pump is equipped with a 5 horsepower Baldor motor. Water is pumped into a water treatment building, where it first passes through a meter and is then injected with a liquid sodium hypochlorite solution for disinfection. Potable water is pumped into the adjacent water pressurization building where it is pressurized utilizing a NAT ftL BD bladderless pressure tank, with a maximum pressure of 80 PSI. Pressure switches control the operation of the booster pumps. Following pressurization, the potable water line splits with one AC and ductile iron line supplying the fire suppression system; while the other AC and ductile iron line provides potable water to the 50,000-gallon below-ground concrete potable water storage reservoir. A Watts 2000B ¬æ-inch reduced pressure zone (RPZ) assembly backflow prevention device is installed on the potable water line supplying the fire suppression system. The liquid level inside the 50,000-gallon below-ground potable water storage reservoir is controlled through a float system.
point_of_use: Visitor Center; comfort station with a men fts restroom, women fts restroom, and custodial closet; the maintenance shop; the Park irrigation system; and the two Park housing units

description:
Missouri American Water supplies quality drinking water to more than 365,000 customers in St. Louis County, St. Charles County and northern Jefferson County. Approximately 80 percent of our surface water comes from the Missouri River, which borders our service area on the north and the west. Nearly 20 percent comes from the Meramec River in south St. Louis County. Both rivers provide a plentiful supply of water that responds well to conventional, though rigorous, drinking water treatment processes.

Missouri American Water occasionally purchases a small quantity of water from the City of St. Louis Water Division, which also uses the Missouri River as a source of water. For more information about this water supply, contact the City of St. Louis Water Division at (314) 868-5640.

description: This is a public water system. It serves public restrooms and drinking fountains at a year-round picnic area. The source of this water system is from a drilled well. This well was drilled around 1999. The casing of the wellhead is raised approximately 18 inches above the ground. The wellhead is equipped with a protective cap and a properly functioning gasket. A concrete apron surrounds the entire wellhead. A submersible pump (pump information not posted) pumps water into the heated water treatment room. Three, 119-gallon Flex~Lite Pressure Tanks (set to 50/55 PSI), model FL4C; two water softeners; a filter; chlorination injection; and four, 119-gallon Mix Master Contact tanks (baffling factor 0.7), model BAF120; are inside the water treatment room. The chlorine pump only operates when it senses water flow. The backwash line is not plumbed directly to the sewer; there is an air break into the floor sink. This distribution system is equipped with an alarm in the event water pressure drops below 20 PSI.
point_of_use: Public restrooms and drinking fountains at a year-round picnic area

The source of this water system is from a drilled well, with an approximate drill date sometime during 2003. The casing of the wellhead is raised approximately 12 to 24 inches above the ground. The well is equipped with a protective cap and a properly functioning gasket. A concrete apron surrounds the entire wellhead. A submersible pump (model # 10GS05412C, set at 50 feet, and installed in 2011) pumps water into the water treatment room, located in the basement of the seasonal quarter‚Äôs unit. Pumped water from the well enters the basement of the facility and flows into a pressure tank. This water flows into two neutralizing filters and then into one water softener. Chlorine is injected into the water. The chlorinated water passes through three, 119-gallon MixMatch contact tanks, model BAF120.

description: This is a non-public water system. This water system supplies a private quarter's house located on top of the adjacent hill. The source of this water system is from a drilled well. The casing of this wellhead is raised approximately 12 to 24 inches above the ground. The wellhead is equipped with a protective cap and a properly functioning gasket. A concrete apron surrounds the entire wellhead. A submersible pump (pump information not posted) pumps water into the water treatment room located inside the basement of the spring house. This spring house is located at the bottom of the hill, and staff stated potable water is not supplied to the spring house. After entering the water treatment room, water flows into an 80-gallon Xtrol pressure tank, then through a chlorine injection point, and then into an 80-gallon contact tank. The chlorine pump only operates when it senses flow. The UV unit inside this water treatment room was removed in 2018.

The water system is currently classified as a non-public water system. It only has 5 active connections (ranger station, upper A frame housing unit, lower A frame housing unit, Baca Lodge housing unit, and maintenance shop) and serves about 17 staff members. The water system consists of a surface water intake, treatment building, gravity storage tank, and distribution system. The well is no longer in use and the park is utilizing a temporary surface water intake from La Jara Creek as the water source. The treatment system was originally designed for surface water source but the old infiltration gallery was destroyed due to erosion. Treatment now includes filtration (depth filters, 5 micron, 1 micron, and 1 micron absolute filters) and chlorination. Water is produced at a rate of 7 to 22 gpm, depending on how clean the filters are. Treated water is pumped to a 30,000 gallon welded steel water storage tank that is equipped with a tank mixer to keep the tank from freezing in the winter. Water flows by gravity from the water tank into the distribution system that serves buildings that are primarily used by park staff and VIPs. Future plans for visitation and usage have not been determined.
point_of_use: ranger station, upper A frame housing unit, lower A frame housing unit, Baca Lodge housing unit, and maintenance shop (17 staff members)

description:
The water system is classified as a public transient non-community system. All of the facilities at the Monument (eight service connections) are served by the water system.

Water is obtained from a drilled well approximately 1,800 feet deep, located in a well house approximately 200 feet from the visitor center.  A minimum of two samples are required per month. 
 
Water is chlorinated via a hypochlorinator (12.5% liquid sodium hypochlorite) that is located in the pumphouse.  The water is pumped to a 55,000-gallon steel reservoir, which is a 100-foot elevated steel tank erected in 1961.  Some new distribution lines were installed in 2010.
The park receives water from the City of Waco, TX.

Well 2 is 406 feet and Well 3 is 507 feet. The filtered water is chlorinated and sent to the booster pump station and thru the distribution to the two 38,000 gallon storage tank. A project is in process to pump water from Brandy Creek to Dry Creek (PMIS # 316429).
