description: This system serves BLM Power, WAPA, and the picnic area comfort station.  It receives water from the penstocks supplying water to the Judge Francis Carr Powerhouse.  The system includes a treatment building containing a multimedia pressure-filter and post-chlorination equipment, an aboveground fused glass-coated bolted steel water storage tank, and 4-inch ductile iron distribution system piping.  Water flows from the penstocks through the treatment building.  Polymer is injected before the filters, and chlorine is added after filtration to maintain a chlorine residual in the system.  Treated water from this reservoir flows by gravity to the powerhouse, the picnic area comfort station, and a booster pump station that lifts the water to a second stainless steel water storage reservoir at a higher elevation for distribution to several park residences.  The treatment system operates automatically with liquid level controls in the reservoir that are hardwired to the plant controls.  Filter backwash water is temporarily stored in a 5,000-gallon tank and is used to irrigate the parking area.  The surface water source turbidity for raw water is grab-sampled and treated water is continually monitored at each individual filter and as combined filter effluent (CFE).  Chlorine residuals of the treated water are continually monitored before it is pumped to the water tank.  Plant shutdown alarms are set at CFE at 0.3 NTU, low free chlorine at 0.4 ppm and high free chlorine at 3.0 ppm.
point_of_use: BLM Power, WAPA, and the picnic area comfort station
source_location_refs: Google Earth Coordinates for hydropower plant

description: This system provides treated water to two group campsites. Each campsite has a multi drinking fountain station with water tap and self-closing valves. Previously, water flowed by gravity from the spring to a 3,000-gallon polyethylene water storage tank where sodium hypochlorite in injected by a Dosatron into the system. This source spring was damaged in the Carr Fire. Currently, the park is hauling water to the water storage tank. The disinfected water then flows by gravity through the distribution system piping to the campground. Eventually, the park plans to pipe water from the Brandy Creek system to this system (PMIS #316429).
source_notes: Previously this was sourced from a spring but the spring was damaged in the Carr fire.

fmss_system_name: UTIL - French Gulch Qtrs 515 Non-public Water System
description: This system serves a permanent park residence and consists of a spring, a concrete storage tank, a water treatment room in the barn next to the residence, distribution piping, and a 10‚Äù cartridge filter at the house.  The treatment room contains a 1-micron absolute micron cartridge filter (GE Osmonics SXE-10-EHS), hypochlorinator, booster pump and pressure tank.  Water from the spring enters an infiltration gallery and flows by gravity to the concrete storage tank.  It then flows to the treatment room where the booster pump pumps it through the filter and the water is chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite solution.  The pressure tank provides minimal storage and volume for chlorine contact time.  Chlorine residuals are monitored about three times per week.  A residual of 0.5 to 1.0 ppm is maintained at the residence.
This system consists of a drilled well, submersible pump, continuous disinfection, an iron treatment filter, and a hydropneumatic pressure system.
source_location_refs: Coordinates of the Ranger Station used.

description: This system serves the headquarters and maintenance complexes and to the Visitor Information Center Overlook.  It consists of a raw-water intake from Whiskeytown Lake, a treatment building containing two multimedia pressure-filters, and post-chlorination equipment, a 60,000-gallon welded steel above ground water storage tank, and distribution system piping.  A 15 horsepower submersible pump at the lake intake pumps water to the treatment building where polymer is added to aid in filtration.  It then passes through the filters to remove turbidity.  The filtered water is chlorinated and then flows to the water tank via a dedicated 4-inch ductile iron fill line.  The water tank provides storage and chlorine contact time.  Treated water flows by gravity from the tank to the facilities.  The system is operated manually with a time clock set as necessary to keep the tank full.  Filter backwash water is used to irrigate the lawn around the headquarters building.  The surface water source turbidity for raw is grab-sampled and treated water is continually monitored.  Chlorine residuals of the treated water immediately after filtration are also continually monitored.    The distribution system consists of 4-inch ductile iron pipe and 6-inch PVC pipe. In 2020, a mixer was added to the water storage tank.
point_of_use: headquarters, maintenance complexes, Visitor Information Center Overlook

description:
This system serves three buildings, one used as seasonal staff housing, and two park residences that have been converted to offices.  This system was completed and put on line June 2005.  It combines the previously separate systems feeding #324, and the one feeding #318 and #321.  It consists of a drilled well with submersible pump, a treatment building housing sodium hypochlorite injection equipment and automatic telemetry controls, 2-inch water distribution line, and a 10,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank.  Water is pumped from the well through the treatment building where sodium hypochlorite disinfectant is injected.  The treated water then enters the distribution system piping.  Chlorine contact time is accomplished primarily in the distribution piping before reaching the buildings.

Also includes well #333 and a 3" waterline.

description:
Whitman Mission water system (ID #NP980) is a group A, transient, non-community public water system. Water source is a deep well drilled in September 1961 and the well head is housed in a dedicated room in the Utility building. The well was completed with 12-inch steel casing to 524 foot depth and open borehole from 524 feet to 755 feet. Silt and clay layers were encountered in the borehole from 15 ft to 517 ft, providing a highly effectively seal of the water bearing formation. The water bearing formation is the Frenchman Springs Member of the Wanapum Basalt, a member of the Columbia River Basalt Group. At the well location, this formation appears from 517 feet to 755 feet and static water level at well completion was 35 ft. The well yield was recorded as 77 gallons per minute with draw down of 310 feet.  

A five horse-power submersible pump was sized to pump at 29 GPM and installed at 342 foot depth.  In 2014, the park added 105 feet to the depth of the pump due to air entering the line during extended run times.  The pump depth is now 447 feet.  A wellhead assessment was completed in 2014 to ensure no impact from site conditions and fuel tank.  Water is chlorinated and pumped to a 50,000 gallon cast-in-place concrete tank which is located approximately 1100 feet east of the well head and 100 feet above in elevation.  The water system services the Park Visitor Center and administrative office, a single family residence, Park Utility Building, and three drinking fountains during the summer.  

The water system also irrigates approximately 3.5 acres of lawn from April through October. Water production averages 10,800 gallons per day in the summer and 900 gallons per day in the winter. Potable use averages 500 gallons per day in the months of May through October and 300 gallons per day in the months of November through April. The majority of summer consumption goes to irrigation of lawn which has been replaced with native grasses with the expectation of water demand to decrease significantly once the grass is established.
point_of_use: Visitor Center and administrative office, a single family residence, Park Utility Building, three drinking fountains, irrigation

description:
This water system is classified as a Public Non-Community water system.  The system provides potable water to the visitor center, gift shop and snack bar, public restrooms, maintenance area, and residential area consisting of four houses.

Water for the park is obtained from Holloman Air Force Base (AFB).  The park taps the 16-inch AC water supply line opposite the main gate to the base on the south side of New Mexico Highway 70/82.  The system consists of a connection vault at the tap to the AFB water system, about 6.6 miles of 4-inch PVC water main installed in 2003, a treatment and controls building, an elevated water storage tank, and distribution system.  When water in the storage tank drops to a set low water level, a pressure switch located in the treatment building energizes a solenoid to open the isolation valve that connects the two water systems.  When the water level in the elevated storage tank reaches high water level, the pressure switch in the treatment building closes the isolation valve shutting off the flow to the tank.

Holloman Air Force Base, which draws water from a large well field in the Bores, San Andres, and Douglas areas, and from the City of Alamogordo, New Mexico is the source of water for the park.  The well field is located east of the AFB and approximately eight miles south of Alamogordo.  Water provided by the AFB is disinfected by a hypochlorinator with sodium hypochlorite solution.  The hypochlorinator, located in the treatment building, is energized when low water level in the storage tank turns on the pressure switch and is de-energized when the switch is turned off when high water level is detected.  The water storage tank provides adequate contact time for disinfection.  A 100,000-gallon elevated water storage tank provides storage for the park.  Constructed in the 1960's, the tank (interior and exterior) was most recently repainted in 2019.
point_of_use: visitor center, gift shop, snack bar, public restrooms, maintenance area, residential area with four houses
source_location_refs: Google Earth coordinates for the White Sands visitor center

water_system_name: WICA Primary Water System, U-04 - Elk Mtn Campground Water System, U-09 - Picnic Area Water System
fmss_system_name: WICA Primary Water System, U-04 - Elk Mtn Campground Water System, U-09 - Picnic Area Water System
description:
The water system serving the park consists of two deep wells (one well is new) where water is pumped through a new well house and then booster pumped to a combination of four buried concrete reservoirs totaling 500,000 gallons.  The tanks are located on a hillside above park headquarters.  The water is chlorinated just prior to the buried reservoirs. The tanks provide gravity pressure service to all of park headquarters including a visitor center/administrative building, housing, apartments, maintenance area and picnic area.  At the buried concrete reservoirs, treated potable water is booster pumped up the hill to a smaller ground level steel water storage tank.  This tank supplies pressurized water to the distribution system serving the campground.  The facilities at the campground are comfort stations, camp-tender's cabin and several water hydrants.
Elk Mtn Campground Water System is now part of a larger park water system.  This system serves a seasonal campground.  This system has 10,000 gallons of storage in two tanks.
Picnic Area Water System is now part of a park wide water system. The water is shut down during the winter and opens in May.
db_comments: Originally, there were three systems for this park within this database. I combined them into one primary system because the descriptions stated that two of the systems were connected to the larger park system. I kept the identifing information for all three systems and separated them by commas. The well report in the source description link states the formations the well is in at different depths, but doesn't include screen interval information.

description: Town of Seneca Falls municipal waster and sewer department supplies services to Visitor Center and the Elizabeth Candy Stanton House. This visitor center is a 3-story building with a basement and penthouse (totalling 5 levels). The basement of the visitor center houses the dry fire suppression system, incoming city water lines, chlorination system and sand filtration for the water wall. The basement also has a eyewash, safety shower, and a slop sink. First floor has men's and women's restrooms. Second floor has a water fountain that is currently not in use. Third floor has office cubicles, 2 bathrooms and 1 shower, 1 kitchenette, and a dishwasher. The office space on the third floor did have a reverse osmosis water bottle fill station for employees. The only water treatment is for the recirculating water wall which is treated with pool chemicals and has a sand filter.

description: Wright Brothers National Memorial receives potable water from the Dare County Water System.  This municipal water system provides water to all Park facilities, which includes the Visitor Center, Pilot Comfort Station, Pavilion Comfort Station, Fee Station, Maintenance Shop, Eastern National Administrative Building, and Temporary Visitor Center.

The well is shut off (not been officially decommissioned). All water for this system is purchased from Glennallen Water Works (ADEC approved). The ADEC approved water is treated but not chlorinated. The park hauls water, 3,000 gallons at a time, two times a week. The water is dumped into a raw water tank for 12-24 hours. They add potassium permanganate then water is filtered thru green sand filters to activated charcoal to 10-5-1 (2) micron filters before chlorination. Water is then stored in finished water tank for distribution. The RO system has been down for 2 years. Getting rid of RO in new system design. The CO2 remover was bypassed as well as orthophosphate and soda ash. There is a 10,000-gallon concrete water storage tank. The water flows from the storage tank to the distribution system that serves the Visitor Center, Headquarters/Admin, Comfort Station, and water treatment plant. The original system was served via a drilled well that has elevated levels of iron/manganese and TDS. Therefore the treatment system is extensive. The process in order of treatment is as follows: 4,000 gallon prechlorine aeration-settling tank; potassium permanganate addition and then sent through a greensand filter, an activated charcoal filter, 10, 5, 1 micron prefilters, an antiscale RO unit with 5 membrane filters; the water is then sent through a gas stripper; orthroposphate is added, then soda ash; the effluent from the plant is then chlorinated. The antiscale RO unit with 5 membrane filters; the water is then sent through a gas stripper; orthroposphate is added, then soda ash portion of the system is not online. They have not use these components for at least 2 years. There is a 10,000-gallon concrete water storage tank. The water flows from the storage tank to the distribution system that serves the Visitor Center, Headquarters/Admin, Comfort Station, and water treatment plant. The Park has started to purchase water from Glennallen Water Works (ADEC approved) to help meet the demand of the system. The current system cannot meet the demand. The park purchases water once a month in the winter and two-four times a month in the summer. The ADEC approved water is treated but not chlorinated. The park hauls water, 3,000 gallons at a time. The water is dumped into a raw water tank for 12-24 hours. The well water is also mixed into the raw water tank for treatment. They add potassium permanganate then water is filtered thru green sand filters to activated charcoal to 50-10-5-1 (2) micron filters before chlorination. Water is then stored in finished water tank for distribution. The RO system has been down for 2 years. Getting rid of RO in new system design. The CO2 remover was bypassed as well as orthophosphate and soda ash.

description: This seasonal system has a surface water source (National Creek), 2 pre-filters (50 and 5 micron), and 1 Harmsco absolute filter for Giardia. Continuous disinfection is achieved via a HE Anderson Series S proportional pump.  There is a single 500-gallon water storage tank. The water then flows via gravity to the Park employee's housing, the break room, dairy barn/maintenance shop, (general store and recreational house are fire suppression only). Cyclonic separator is leaking but they have one on order and is now replaced.

description: This system is not owned by the park, it is owned by the City of Kennecott. The Park treats water for the two houses it owns.  The city has 13 houses with 11 service connections, while the park has 2 connections. Surface water-National Creek Water is retrieved from a pressurized cone from National Creek to buried lines and treat it with 50-5-1 Micron filtration and continuous disinfection. description:
All of the facilities at the Monument, except the Doney Picnic Area, are served by a single water system.  No water is provided at the picnic area.  The facility is classified as a Public Transient Non-Community Water System.  Water is obtained from a drilled well, approximately 905 feet deep, located near the maintenance shop. The water is routinely sampled twice a month for bacteriological quality.  
Reverse osmosis (RO) units and water softeners are located in each residence to improve the aesthetic qualities of the water for drinking and cooking. The water is chlorinated with a premixed hypochlorite solution in the pumphouse. The free residual of the chlorine is tested daily.

The water is pumped through a four-inch PolyVinyl Chloride (PVC) line to two glass coated, bolted-steel, reservoirs on the hill above the visitor center.  One of the reservoirs is a 57,000-gallon reservoir, the other is a 52,000-gallon reservoir. The reservoirs are piped and valved to permit either parallel or series operation. The reservoirs are normally drained through the six-inch Asbestos Cement (AC) distribution line.
point_of_use: all facilities except the picnic area

description: The Apollinaris Spring is an historical feature with a picnic area.  There are two spring systems at Apollinaris.  The lower spring serves only the historic feature across the road from the picnic area and is signed to recommend purification if used for potable purposes.  The upper springs have been used in the past for potable purposes.  However chlorination caused aesthetic problems and potable use was discontinued.  Currently, the spring serves only the flush valves and sinks at the comfort station.  International non-potable signs have been installed at the sinks.

description: Beartooth Road Camp is outside the park on the Beartooth scenic highway.  Its primary function is as a base for road plowing operations.  There is an employee dorm/mess hall, seasonal trailer, and maintenance shop.  The water for this site is supplied from two springs located above the facility.  The lower spring flows by gravity to the chlorinator building from where it is pumped to the upper spring collection box via a two-way line.  The upper spring collection box flows to a wetwell and by gravity back to the treatment building where it is chlorinated.  Floats in the upper spring collection box activate flow from the lower spring.  A meter driven LMI hypochlorinator adds sodium hypochlorite.  Two 275- gallon galvanized tanks provide contact time.  There is no storage for fire protection.

description: The East Entrance area includes a fee collection station, ranger station, two quad housing units, a small office building, and storage sheds.  The water source is a 76' deep well with pitless unit drilled in 1996.  Water is pumped from the well and chlorinated before entering a 20,000-gallon clearwell beneath the pumphouse. description: The North Entrance Station is adjacent to the community of Gardiner, MT and is served by the community water and wastewater systems. The community water system is chlorinated and the entrance station is included in the routine monthly NPS bacteriological monitoring program.

Pebble Creek is a NPS-operated campground with no electrical power. Potable water is supplied to the campground from two wells with hand pumps. The system is classified as public non-community transient groundwater.

Source
Well#1 is a shallow hand pump well estimated to be 24 feet deep. The well is located near the braided stream bed of Pebble Creek approximately 120 feet from the main channel of the creek and approximately 60 feet from a small side channel.

Well#2 is a shallow hand pump well estimated to be 22 feet deep. The well is located near the braided streambed of Pebble Creek approximately 60 feet from the main channel of the creek.
Treatment APF chlorinators are used for disinfection. Both systems have small (approximately 50-gallon) plastic storage tanks for contact time. Water is pumped from the hand pump to a small treatment vault where sodium hypochlorite solution is applied via the d APF chlorinators followed by a small approximately 50 gallon plastic storage tank. Chlorinated water then flows from the tank back to the pump faucet when the system is pressurized by hand pump operation.

description: The Northeast Entrance area includes a fee station, maintenance shop, one permanent residence, a four-plex, two trailers, and several small storage buildings.  A 76-foot deep drilled well is the potable water source.  The well head is in a pit below grade.  Silting has reduced the well yield from the original capacity.  Water is pumped to a 5,000-gallon concrete reservoir that supplies system pressure.  Float switches in the reservoir activate the well pump and a W&T liquid chlorinator simultaneously.  The water system is classified as non-public, groundwater.  There is no evidence of surface water influence.

description:
The canyon area has several lodges, a variety of cabin rental units, a general store with grocery and restaurant, an Adventure Store, service station, visitor center, large campground, RV dump station, horse corral, and public laundry/ shower facility. Housing for NPS and concessionaire personnel is a variety of single family residences, dormitories, cabins, and a large trailer court. Most of these facilities are open on a seasonal basis. Winter activity includes a snowmobile warming hut and a winter camping concession.

Water is obtained from an intake structure on the Yellowstone River.  A raw water pump station is located in a vault at the river with three 30 HP pumps that pump water to a hypalon-lined open 1,000,000 gallon raw water settling reservoir located at the water treatment plant (WTP).  Water flows by gravity from the settling reservoir to the WTP, which was constructed in 1984-85.  The WTP is a conventional surface water filtration plant with coagulation/flocculation, sedimentation, and mixed-media filtration.  Soda ash is added for pH adjustment prior to coagulation/flocculation.  A polymer is also added at the beginning of the plant to aid in coagulation/flocculation and a filter aid is added prior to the filters to aid in filtration.  Liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected for disinfection.  A 24-inch diameter water main was installed after the WTP to achieve adequate chlorine contact time.  The filters are backwashed into one of two hypalon-lined backwash lagoons as needed.

The permit from EPA requires 95% of turbidity readings to less than 0.3 NTU, with 100% of turbidity readings less than 1.0 NTU.  The average daily water demand is approximately 230,000 gpd in the summer.  The WTP requires a level 2 water operator.  Treated water is pumped to three buried concrete reservoirs (250,000 gallon, 500,000 gallon, and 750,000 gallon) or to distribution. There is no dedicated fill line to the reservoirs.
point_of_use: lodges, cabin rental units, a general store with grocery and restaurant, an Adventure Store, service station, visitor center, large campground, RV dump station, horse corral, and public laundry/ shower facility,single family residences, dormitories, cabins, and a large trailer court

description: The west entrance area includes an entrance station, backcountry permit office, several permanent and trailer residences, a corral, a maintenance shed, an employee dorm, and a concessionaire warehouse.  Water is purchased from the community of West Yellowstone, Montana. This is a public community ground water system regulated by the State of Montana. The system is not disinfected. The NPS collects 3 bacteriological samples per month from the portion of the distribution system serving NPS and concessionaire facilities.

description:
Mammoth Hot Springs water system serves a full array of visitor services, many permanent and seasonal housing units, NPS and concession administrative offices, and a large maintenance area.  The system is classified as a public community surface water system.  An intake structure diverts surface water from Panther Creek and the Gardner River.  Water quality tends to be better from Panther Creek, so this is the primary water source.  Water is piped approximately 4 miles to the water treatment plant (WTP) and goes through a hydroelectric turbine before it enters an open raw water reservoir near the WTP.  

The WTP is a conventional filtration plant with coagulation/flocculation, sedimentation, and mixed media filtration with a design capacity of about 1 million gallons per day.  Potassium permanganate is added oxidize iron and manganese and a polymer is also added to improve filtration.  The filter media is backwashed as needed into 2 backwash ponds for evaporation. Sodium hypochlorite solution is injected for disinfection and enters a 34,000 gallon baffled treated water contact chamber/clearwell at the WTP for chlorine contact (CT) prior to distribution.  Storage is provided by a 160,000 gallon underground concrete storage tank (Upper Pressure Zone Tank) located at the WTP, which is connected to the clearwell.  A million gallon concrete underground storage tank with hypalon liner is located approximately 2 miles from the WTP, floats on the distribution system, and is filled after distribution system demand is met.

The permit from EPA requires 95% of turbidity readings to less than 0.3 NTU, with 100% of turbidity readings less than 1.0 NTU.  Water demand ranges from about 150,000 gpd in the winter to 450,000 gpd in the summer. The water treatment plant requires a level 3 water operator.  The park operates an EPA certified laboratory out of the Mammoth WTP that performs bacteriological testing for the entire park.
point_of_use: permanent and seasonal housing units, NPS and concession administrative offices, and a large maintenance area
treatment_system_type: Chlorination, Coagulation, Flocculation, Potassium Permanganate, Polymer
source_location_refs: Coordinates for the diversion dam on Gardener River (source waters) were used from Cox ER. 1973.

description:
Slough Creek is a NPS operated campground. There is no electrical power available. Potable water is supplied to the campground from two wells with hand pumps. Construction and depth details of the 2 wells are unknown but they are estimated to be about 40 feet deep. APF chlorinators are used for disinfection. Both systems have small (approximately 50-gallon) plastic pressure tanks for contact time. The system is classified as public non-community transient groundwater.

Well#1 is located on the north end of the campground and is approximately 110 feet from the nearest high water bank of the braided bed of Slough Creek. Well #2 serves the southern end of the campground and is greater than 200 feet from the nearest high-water bank of the braided bed of Slough Creek.

description: The Bechler area consists of a contact station, ranger residence, generator and water treatment building, maintenance shed, barn, and corral. There is an NPS-maintained picnic area at nearby Cave Falls.  A 60-foot deep well with a pitless adapter is the potable water source for Bechler.  Water is pumped to the generator building where it is chlorinated by an APF water driven chlorinator.  Three hydropneumatic tanks provide system pressure.  A 275-gallon tank provides contact time.  The water system is classified as non-public, but Bechler is at a trailhead and public use of water from the yard hydrant is significant.

description: Source: The water source is two springs. The "new" spring (1988) is pumped to a stand pipe in the older spring box which flows by gravity to a 60,000 gallon clear well at the chlorinator building. The new spring was completely rebuilt and deepened with a new spring box and pump in 2016. Three MPA tests were completed in 1992 for these springs and they were designated as low risk for surface water influence at that time. As of 2022 testing, the MPA results indicate GWUDI and the park is currently working with EPA and develop filtration project at the location. Storage Treated water is pumped from the clear well to a 200,000 gallon buried concrete reservoir that is located above the Roosevelt corral area. description: The South Entrance area consists of an entrance station, ranger station, a permanent employee residence, several trailers for seasonal staff, and a picnic area. The system is classified as a non-public groundwater system.  The water supply is a spring next to a stream. The source has been a reliable water supply although it has historically had high fluoride levels.  The spring flows by gravity roughly ¬Ω mile into a 20,100 gallon clearwell under the pumphouse where it is chlorinated.  A control valve in the pumphouses closes when the tank is full and opens when the tank calls for water based on the water level in the clearwell (read by a pressure transducer).  There is no overflow for the clearwell.  The water system is pressurized with 3 EA 5 HP booster pumps and 3 EA 264 gallon pressure tanks. There is no filtration. Average daily water demand is approximately 1,200 gpd.
point_of_use: entrance station, ranger station, a permanent employee residence, several trailers for seasonal staff, and a picnic area

description: Grant Village is one of the major developed areas of Yellowstone National Park. A full array of visitor services is available. There is a significant number of NPS and concessionaire housing units of various types. Most operations are seasonal.  The water source for Grant is Yellowstone Lake.  Water is taken directly into an intake box by a screened line extending into the lake. Two submersible pumps in the intake box supply the water treatment plant (WTP). The WTP is operated as a multiple media direct filtration plant using polyaluminum chloride.   Sodium hypochlorite is used for disinfection.  The system is classified as public, transient, non-community water system.  There is a dedicated fill line to two storage tanks. One is a large insulated steel tank and the other is a buried, two compartment concrete reservoir. The tanks pressurize the distribution system.
source_location_refs: Coordinates for the pump house used. Pump house is noted as direct Raw Water Intake and therefore is the draw point.

description: The Madison Junction area has a concessionaire-operated campground with a RV dump station, picnic area, NPS housing, and a maintenance area.  The source of water is from six infiltration galleries.  Water flows by gravity from the infiltration area to a clearwell located underneath the pumphouse, from which water is pumped to an insulated 105,000 gallon welded steel water storage tank.  Level controls in the tank activate the pump and a chlorinator, which doses to the clearwell.  A dedicated line fills the steel tank. Fluoride levels in the water system vary up to about 6 mg/l. This can cause serious mottling and fluorosis in developing teeth of children and is a violation of the primary drinking water standard MCL of 4.0 mg/l.  The operator certification level for this system is a Level 1.
point_of_use: campground with a RV dump station, picnic area, NPS housing, and a maintenance area
source_notes: Source of water is an infiltration gallery. For our purposes this is classified as shallow groundwater with the type being the original source, and the aquifer is the top formation for this area.
source_location_refs: Coordinates of the pump house were used.

description:
The Tower Falls area contains a concessionaire store/snackbar, concessionaire employee dorm, and campground.  The water source is a spring that provides a dependable supply of high quality water.  All flow in the system is by gravity. The system is classified as public, transient, non-community water system. There is a small reservoir between the spring and the main reservoir that serves as a pressure reducer by providing an air gap.  Floats in the main reservoir activate a solenoid valve in order to maintain the liquid levels.  The same circuit activates the W&T liquid chlorine pump which discharges into the reservoir fill line. The reservoir is between the spring and distribution system.

Three MPA tests were completed on the spring in 1992 (two in June and a third in October).  Based on those tests the source was placed in the low to moderate risk category, and no further testing or improvements were recommended unless some change in water quality was observed.
point_of_use: concessionaire store/snackbar, concessionaire employee dorm, and campground

Sodium hypochlorite is injected at the pumphouse before entering a 13,300 gallon bolted steel water storage tank via a dedicated fill line.

description:
Source: Water is provided by a generator powered well pump located approximately 150 feet from the lake. The 6 inch casing well is reported to be 139 feet deep with well screen from 109-139 feet. The well produces ~50 gpm. No well log is available. An MPA completed in June 2006 assigned a low risk to this source.

Treatment: The system is manually operated based on the tank level as indicated by a float. The well pump activates a W&T liquid chlorinator.

Storage: A 13,300 gallon bolted steel water storage tank was installed in 2001. There is a dedicated treated water fill line to the tank. The water system is classified as public, non-community, transient.

Distribution: Lewis Lake CG is an NPS-operated campground. The water system serves six different campground hydrants. A seasonal residence (NPS owned trailer) has recently been removed from service.

description:
Source: The water source is a groundwater spring located approximately 0.6 miles from the ranch buildings. The spring is located next to Rose Creek that flows past the ranch buildings and the spring box overflow empties into the creek. There is a fenced spring collection area with a spring box that has collection laterals, a transmission main, and a standpipe drain/overflow. Outside the fenced area on the hillside there is a second spring box that the transmission main flows through. Historic drawings of the system identify the second box as a settling box. The settling box appears to have an inlet line, a screened outlet line and a standpipe overflow. The raw water transmission main is a 6 inch iron pipe. An MPA was completed on this system in 2006 resulting in a low risk classification.

Treatment: A chlorinator feeds sodium hypochlorite in a vault near the residences. Three 75-gallon tanks provide contact time.

Distribution: The Lamar area consists of two permanent NPS residences, the Yellowstone Institute facilities including a bunk house/mess hall, several cabins and director's residence, restroom and shower building, and horse barn/corral. All the system pressure is from the spring box head. The fire hydrants are fed from the raw water main prior to chlorination.
point_of_use: two permanent NPS residences, the Yellowstone Institute facilities including a bunk house/mess hall, several cabins and director's residence, restroom and shower building, and horse barn/corral

description:
This water system serves a full array of visitor services, NPS and concessionaire housing, a medical clinic, a campground and maintenance facilities in Fishing Bridge, Bridge Bay, and Lake Village. Water is supplied from 3 springs at Lake Village and two wells at Fishing Bridge spring.  The Lake spring system is a set of collection lines and collection boxes that flow to a common collection box and then to the clearwell at the pumphouse.  Sodium hypochlorite is injected into the clearwell for disinfection.  Water is pumped from the clearwell into the distribution system with two 20 HP booster pumps, which are controlled by the water level in the water storage tanks.

The 2 wells, drilled in 2009, at Fishing Bridge are located near the pumphouse.  Well #1 (closest to the pumphouse) is a 6-inch diameter well with PVC casing to a depth of 315 feet, with 0.050 slot PVC screen from 105 to 285 feet (alternates between 20 ft  lengths of screen and casing) and from 300 to 315 feet.  Well #1 has a bentonite sanitary seal to a depth of 3 feet and pumps at a rate of 110 gpm.  Well #1 is an artesian well, with artesian flow between 5 and 10 gpm.  Fluoride was measured at 3.7 mg/L in Well #1, which is below the MCL of 4 mg/L.  Well #2 is a 6-inch diameter well with PVC casing to a depth of 215 feet, with 0.050 slot PVC screen from 95 to 135 feet and from 135 to 215 feet (alternates between 20 ft lengths of screen and casing).  Well #2 has a bentonite sanitary seal to a depth of 28 feet and pumps at a rate of 85 gpm.  Groundwater from the wells are pumped into a clearwell at the pumphouse. Sodium hypochlorite is injected into the clearwell for disinfection.  Water is pumped from the clearwell into the distribution system.

The booster pumps at both pumphouses pump water into the upper pressure zone, which consists of a 450,000 gallon buried concrete water tank at Fishing Bridge and the water tanks at Lake Village (500,000 gallon buried concrete water tank & 540,000 gallon welded steel water tank).  Water then flows to the lower pressure zone through an altitude valve and into the 100,000 gallon welded steel water storage tank in Bridge Bay.  There are no dedicated fill lines.  The operator certification level for this system is a Level 1.

description:
The Old Faithful area is one of the major developed areas of the park.  A large array of visitor services are available.  There is also a housing area and a maintenance facility.  Potable water for Old Faithful is obtained from a raw water intake structure on the Firehole River.  Water flows by gravity to the water treatment plant (WTP).  The raw water quality varies dramatically and is difficult to treat consistently.  The raw water contains fluoride more than the secondary (aesthetic) standard of 2.0 mg/l but below the primary (health) standard or MCL of 4.0 mg/l.  A lined open 750,000 gallon sedimentation basin was constructed in 2007 that can be used during spring runoff when turbidity in the river is high. The system is a public community surface water system.  

The WTP is a conventional surface water treatment system with coagulation/flocculation, sedimentation, and mixed media filtration.  A polymer is added before filtration to improve the filtration process.  Ferric chlorine is added prior to filtration to help remove arsenic, but this increases the pH.  The treated water tends to be corrosive (pH about 6.0), so sodium hydroxide is added to the clearwell to increase the pH and help address elevated lead and copper concentrations in the distribution system.  Treated water is disinfected with liquid sodium hypochlorite as it flows into a 40,000 gallon clearwell.  There is no dedicated fill line to the reservoir but calculations show that CT requirements are being met largely because of the large WTP clearwell.  Filters are backwashed into 2 percolation/evaporation basins as needed.

The permit from EPA requires 95% of turbidity readings to less than 0.3 NTU, with 100% of turbidity readings less than 1.0 NTU.  The average daily water demand is approximately 250,000 gpd in the summer and 50,000 gpd during the winter.  The WTP requires a level 3 water operator.  The WTP has a capacity of about 450 gpm, or 324,000 gpd when operating 12 hours/day.  Water from the WTP is pumped to a 1.6 million gallon buried concrete reservoir that floats on the distribution system.
treatment_system_type: Chlorination, Coagulation, Flocculation, Sodium Hydroxide, Polymer, Filtration

description:
Facilities at Norris include a campground, government housing area, small maintenance shop, and a museum/office at the geyser basin.  Water is supplied from an infiltration gallery and raw water pump station located adjacent to the Gibbon River.  A surface water treatment plant constructed in 2006 and rated at 50 gpm treats the water via a conventional filtration plant with coagulation/flocculation, sedimentation, and mixed media filtration.  Sodium hydroxide is added to adjust pH and a polymer is also added to improve filtration.  The filter media is backwashed as needed into the wastewater collection system. Sodium hypochlorite solution is injected for disinfection and enters a treated water contact chamber/clearwell at the WTP for chlorine contact (CT) prior to distribution.  After treatment, water is then pumped to a buried 100,000 gallon buried concrete reservoir.

The permit from EPA requires 95% of turbidity readings to less than 0.3 NTU, with 100% of turbidity readings less than 1.0 NTU.  The average daily water demand is approximately 10,000 gpd in the summer.  The water treatment plant requires a level 3 water operator.
point_of_use: campground, government housing area, small maintenance shop, and a museum/office at the geyser basin
treatment_system_type: Chlorination, Filtration, Coagulation, Flocculation, Sodium Hydroxide, Polymer
source_notes: Source of water is an infiltration gallery. For our purposes this is classified as shallow groundwater with the type being the original source, and the aquifer is the top formation for this area.

description: The water system serves a drinking fountain in the HSC, flush toilets, showers, employee laundry, the kitchen facilities, and a drinking fountain and two flush toilets in the backpackers campground.  It consists of a surface water intake in the Merced River, treatment building with chlorine disinfection equipment and a sand filtration system, a 2.5-gpm slow sand filter, a 1,500-gallon polyethylene surge tank, a 1,500-gallon polyethylene contact tank, a 1,550-gallon polyethylene storage tank, and distribution piping.  Raw water flows by gravity from the intake to the treatment facility.  An air relief valve at the high point in the line bleeds air to prevent air lock.  Before entering the building a separate line directs some of the raw water to the six flush toilets.  Another connection directs raw water to the hydrant by the septic tank for wash down.  The remaining raw water passes through the sand filter and into the surge tank.  When the water in the storage tank reaches low level and the surge tank low level float for booster shut off is above low water level, the booster pump pumps water from the surge tank through a 10-micron cartridge pre-filter and a 1-micron Giardia filter, is chlorinated and discharges to the chlorine contact tank then to the storage tank.  Flow through the bag filters is typically 14 gpm with new bag filters.  Water is then gravity fed to the distribution system.  All pumps and controls are powered by a photovoltaic system.  The water intake structure is built at the end of a channel off the river.  During normal river flows, water flow over the intake structure continually brings in fresh water.  During periods of low flow or drought, water does not flow through this channel.  A 100-foot extension must be connected to the intake to obtain water that is not stagnant.
point_of_use: a drinking fountain in the HSC, flush toilets, showers, employee laundry, the kitchen facilities, and a drinking fountain and two flush toilets in the backpackers campground

description: The water system consists of a small creek fed by surface springs located in a meadow above the backpacker campground area, a 2-cell concrete block slow sand filter, a treatment building housing disinfection equipment, an 800-gallon concrete tank, and distribution piping.  It serves a hose bib at the backpacker camp, a hose bib at the NPS camp, horse corral, and the high sierra camp facilities including the kitchen, showers, laundry, and drinking water fountain.  The surface water flows by gravity through a 2-inch HDPE line to the slow sand filter, which is capable of producing 6 gpm (3 gpm/cell), and is subsequently chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite solution.  Raw water is monitored at a tap before the sand filter and filtered water is monitored at the chlorination shed for turbidity.  The treated water flows into an 800-gallon concrete tank located under the treatment building for storage and contact time.  Water is then gravity fed to the distribution system.  Raw turbidity samples are taken before raw water enters the sand filter.
point_of_use: a hose bib at the backpacker camp, a hose bib at the NPS camp, horse corral, and the high sierra camp facilities including the kitchen, showers, laundry, and drinking water fountain

description: This system serves HSC facilities including an employee shower, employee laundry, a drinking fountain, several sinks, the kitchen facility, and horse corral.  It consists of a spring box, two 800 gallon bolted steel storage tanks, and distribution piping.  The source for this system is a spring that has been determined by the State not to be under the influence of surface water.  The water flows by gravity through a 2-inch PE line to the water tank, and then to the distribution piping.  Water from the spring flows unregulated into the tank and overflows whenever the flow rate exceeds usage.

description: This non-public systems provides non-potable water to a ranger residence and bunkhouse.  It consists of a gasoline powered trash pump with lake intake, a 10,000-gallon redwood water storage tank, galvanized iron distribution piping.  The water in the water tank is periodically batch chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite.  The water is not filtered.  When water is needed, the pump is manually started and water pumped from the lake to the water tank via a dedicated line.  Water flows by gravity from the tank to the residence and bunkhouse via galvanized iron pipe.

This seasonal system serves the Yosemite Institute, the service station and general store, campground comfort stations, and residences.  It consists of a drilled well, treatment building with disinfection equipment, a 50,000-gallon bolted steel storage tank, and distribution system piping.  A submersible pump pumps water from the well to the treatment building where a hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite solution.  The disinfected water then enters the distribution system.  Since there is no dedicated fill line to the water tank, the tank does not provide chlorine contact time.  The pipe between the treatment building and the first point of use provides contact time.

A separate non-public water system serves the fire lookout.  It consists of a drilled well, iron and manganese removal system, chlorine disinfection, one polyethylene water storage tank for potable uses, and two open water tanks for fire suppression.
point_of_use: Yosemite Institute, the service station and general store, campground comfort stations, and residences

description: This year round system serves the Big Oak Flat Entrance Station office, visitor center and administrative offices, comfort station, NPS campground, and 30 residences in the government housing area.  It consists of a drilled well, treatment building with equipment for injecting chemicals for disinfection and pH adjustment for control of lead and copper levels, a 100,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank, and distribution system piping.  A submersible pump delivers water from the well to the treatment building where a hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite solution into it.  The disinfected water then enters the distribution system.  Since there is no dedicated fill line to the water tank, the tank does not provide chlorine contact time.  A timer controls operation of the well pump
point_of_use: Big Oak Flat Entrance Station office, visitor center and administrative offices, comfort station, NPS campground, and 30 residences in the government housing area

description: This seasonal system serves a ranger residence and four 2-bed dorms with four flush toilets, washing machine, and showers.  It consists of a drilled well, two pressure tanks, disinfection equipment, and distribution piping.  Well water is pumped through the treatment building where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution.  The distribution piping before the first point of use provides chlorine contact time.  The pressure tanks provide minimal storage before the treated water enters the distribution piping.
point_of_use: ranger residence and four 2-bed dorms with four flush toilets, washing machine, and showers

description:
This seasonal system serves the Tuolumne Lodge, store, NPS campground, visitor center, NPS staff housing, and concessions housing.  It consists of a dam water impoundment on a creek, a sedimentation/flocculation/screen basin, chemical addition for flocculation, two multimedia pressure filters, hypochlorinator for disinfection, a 100,000-gallon welded steel above ground water storage tank, and a 10,000-gallon bolted galvanized steel backwash holding tank.  When called by a float switch in the water tank, water flows by gravity from the source intake structure into the sedimentation basin where polyphosphate is injected for flocculation.  The floc settles in the basin.  The water then flows through three screens of increasingly finer mesh to the water treatment building where a pump increases pressure to enhance flow through the two pressure filters.  The filtered water is then injected with sodium hypochlorite solution and discharges into the water tank via a dedicated fill line for contact time and storage.  The filtered and disinfected water is then fed by gravity to the facilities.  A backwash pump forces treated water from the water tank back through the filters, discharging into the backwash tank where sediments settle.  Backwash water is then discharged to the sanitary sewer.

A separate water system provides water to NPS staff housing during the winter.  It consists of a drilled well, 3 filters (50 micron, 1 micron, carbon), a pressure tank, and distribution system.
point_of_use: Tuolumne Lodge, store, NPS campground, visitor center, NPS staff housing, and concessions housing
db_comments: Description notes a second system that is used in winter. Unclear if that is included within another entry or should be added as a second row for this system.

description: This seasonal system serves the White Wolf Lodge, store, NPS campground, NPS staff housing, and concessions housing.  It consists of a groundwater well, treatment building housing disinfection equipment, a 20,000-gallon bolted steel above ground water storage tank, and distribution piping.  A timer controls flow from the well into the tank.  When the timer energizes the well pump water is pumped through the treatment building where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution.  The treated water is then discharged directly into the distribution piping.
point_of_use: White Wolf Lodge, store, NPS campground, NPS staff housing, and concessions housing

description: The Ostrander Hut is a backcountry facility used during the winter season.  It provides shelter for approximately 40 visitors and a resident host.  Water for the host residence is drawn from the lake by a submersible pump and delivered to the hut water through a 2-inch PE pipe.  The raw lake water then passes through a 50 micron filter and ¬Ω micron filter before being stored in an 80-gallon galvanized steel water tank located on the second floor of the hut.  The pump is connected to the intake line and operated manually to fill the water storage tank.  The pump is removed when the tank is full.  Water flows by gravity to the sink in the host's kitchen.  The water is not disinfected.  Visitors obtain drinking water by melting snow.  They are instructed to boil and/or filter it before using for potable purposes.

description: This seasonal system serves seasonal residences (4 apartments) and a comfort station.  It consists of a drilled well housed in an insulated fiberglass enclosure, a pumphouse containing two pressure tanks, two bag filters (1 micron, 5 micron) disinfection equipment, and distribution piping.  Well water is pumped through the treatment building where it is chlorinated with sodium hypochlorite solution for disinfection.  The distribution piping before the first point of use provides chlorine contact time.  The pressure tanks provide minimal storage before the treated water enters the distribution piping

description: This year round public community water system serves all the facilities in Yosemite Valley.  It consists of three drilled wells (Well #1, Well #2, Well #4), three treatment buildings, a 2.5 million-gallon above ground concrete water storage tank, and distribution system.  A submersible pump in each of the wells pumps water to its own treatment building where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution.  The treated water is then discharged directly into the distribution system.    The water storage tank floats on the system.  All facilities are centrally monitored and controlled by a SCADA system.

description: This year round public community water system serves all the facilities in Yosemite Valley.  It consists of three drilled wells (Well #1, Well #2, Well #4), three treatment buildings, a 2.5 million-gallon above ground concrete water storage tank, and distribution system.  A submersible pump in each of the wells pumps water to its own treatment building where it is disinfected with sodium hypochlorite solution.  The treated water is then discharged directly into the distribution system.    The water storage tank floats on the system.  All facilities are centrally monitored and controlled by a SCADA system.

description: This year round system serves the ski area, maintenance building, and a residence.  It consists of a drilled well (#1), a 90,000-gallon bolted steel water storage tank, sodium hypochlorite disinfection, and distribution system.  The well pump is operated on a timer.  When called, the well pump pumps chlorinated water directly into the distribution system after being disinfected.  There is no dedicated fill line to the water tank, so the tank does not provide contact time.  However, the pump is run at night when there is no water demand, allowing for adequate contact time in the distribution piping.
point_of_use: ski area, maintenance building, and a residence

description: This seasonal public non-community serves three comfort stations at the Bridalveil Creek Campground.  It consists of a drilled well, treatment building with disinfection equipment, a buried steel water storage tank, and distribution system piping.  A submersible pump pumps water from the well to the treatment building where a hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite solution into it.  The disinfected water then enters the distribution system.  A portable generator provides power to operate the well pump and treatment equipment.  It is operated daily for about 10 hours.  There are no automatic controls.  Since there is no dedicated fill line to the water tank, the tank does not provide chlorine contact time. The distribution piping before the first point of use provides chlorine contact time.
point_of_use: three comfort stations at the Bridalveil Creek Campground

description: This year round system serves a comfort station and employee residence, and consists of a well, chlorine disinfection, two 125,000 gallon storage tanks, and distribution system piping.  Water flows to the chlorination shed where a proportional flow hypochlorinator injects sodium hypochlorite solution.  The treated water flows into the water tank for storage and contact time before entering the distribution system.  The residence uses the water for non-potable purposes and is provided bottled water for drinking and potable uses.
source_location_refs: Coordinates for the Ranger Station were used.

description: This seasonal system serves the concession store, snack bar and restrooms, visitor comfort station, and seasonal employee residential area.  It consists of two surface water sources, a treatment building containing a pressure sand filter, bag filtration, and hypochlorinator, a 300,000-gallon welded steel water storage tank, a 10,000-gallon bolted steel water storage tank, and distribution system piping.  At the beginning of the season, water from the Pothole Meadow surface water intake flows by gravity to the treatment building where polymer is injected before flowing through a pressure sand filter.  The filtered water is then disinfected using sodium hypochlorite solution and flows into the large water tank for storage and contact time.  The treated water then feeds the distribution system by gravity.  The sand filter is backwashed manually as needed.  The backwash water is discharged down the slope to daylight.  As conditions dry and the Pothole Meadow source is unable to meet demand, the source at Union Point is used.  Water behind an impoundment dam flows by gravity via a 4-inch pvc line to a holding tank at Union point.  A 10 hp positive displacement pump lifts water via a 4-inch galvanized line to the 10,000-gallon tank by the treatment building.  A booster pump in the treatment building then pumps the water through the treatment building where it is filtered and disinfected before being discharged into the large water tank.  The system has no automatic controls.   A valve is throttled to control flow.  When the tank is full and flow in exceeds demand treated water flows out the tank overflow and discharges to daylight.
point_of_use: concession store, snack bar and restrooms, visitor comfort station, and seasonal employee residential area

description: This year round water system serves a comfort station at the Upper Grove, a comfort station and gift shop at the Lower Grove, and a comfort station and employee residence at the South Entrance Station.  It consists of two spring boxes constructed during the 1930's that collect water from a spring groundwater source located above Rainier Creek, 4-inch cast iron transmission line from the springboks.  The water splits and goes to a 10,000 gallon water storage tank in the Upper Grove and a 45,000 gallon water storage tank at the South Entrance, and distribution piping.  During the winter season, the upper storage tank is bypassed and the water flows to the tank at the Lower Grove.  Water flows from the spring through the distribution system to the lower treatment building, where it is disinfected.  The treated water is then fed to the facilities at the South Entrance Station.
point_of_use: comfort station at the Upper Grove, a comfort station and gift shop at the Lower Grove, and a comfort station and employee residence at the South Entrance Station
source_notes: Technically 2 spring boxes but same source

description: This year round water system serves the hotel, general store, service station, and residences in Wawona.  It consists of a surface water intake from the South Fork of the Merced River, conventional (coagulation/flocculation, filtration, disinfection) water treatment plant (WTP), and two pressure zones.  The lower zone has a 250,000-gallon welded steel water tanks and a 250,000-gallon bolted steel tank.  The upper zone also has two 250,000-gallon welded steel water tanks.  Water flows by gravity from the source to the WTP where it undergoes conventional treatment.  The treated water is stored in a clearwell at the WTP where adequate contact time is achieved.  The treated water is then pumped from the clearwell into the two pressure zones by two submersible pumps, one to each zone.  Neither zone has a dedicated fill line from the treatment plant to the water tanks.  A vault containing a pressure reducing valve and a booster pump allows water to be transferred between the Upper and Lower zones in the event of an emergency or shortage of water in either zone.
point_of_use: hotel, general store, service station, and residences in Wawona.

description: The Coal Creek water system consists of a surface water source intake about 120 yards from the camp. Surface water flows to a 500 gallon Poly clear well tank which used to;overflow back to the stream. It currently does not. The raw water from the clear well is pumped via a ¬Ω HP pump through a series of filters (20 um, 5 um, and taste odor filter) before a final filter. The final filter was a HIF Harmsco filter canister containing seven (7) 1 um absolute filters. The system's distribution is controlled by a pressure switch and an 85 gallon pressure tank with pump on at 30 psi and off at 50 psi. The water supplies one kitchen sink, 2-showers, clothes washer, and 3 cleaning sinks. There is an old, dried up well located on the property. It has dried up and the Park currently has no plans to use this. The park plans to drill another well, and there is currently a project in place to design a new system.
point_of_use: one kitchen sink, 2-showers, clothes washer, and 3 cleaning sinks.

description: This system is supplied by a well located in a building with one office and a shower. During the summer, a washhouse is also supplied. There are signs posted in the washroom that state, "Boil any water you use to fill any water bottles or other containers for at least two minutes." During the summer, the system has the ability to be chlorinated. Water is pumped from the well through 10- and 5-micron filters and sent to be stored in two in 2 plastic storage tanks located in another building. The treatment equipment is located in this second building.  During the winter, the raw water goes through the 10- and 5-micron filters and services only the shower located in the building with the well. Currently, the system is not chlorinated in the winter due to freezing of the pipes and the location of the disinfection equipment. The equipment cannot be relocated in the building with the well due to space limitations. This system also supplies water for the Hilltop House year-round.
point_of_use: building with office and shower, washhouse

description:
The majority of users in the Park are served by a community water system comprised of four springs: Birch Creek and Watercress Spring and the Upper and Lower Grotto Spring. The Wylie Spring has been taken off-line due to damage from a rock slide.

The Birch (105 GPM) and Watercress (40 GPM) springs typically serve the area from the mule corrals down.  Water is collected from both springs and runs to individual concrete 3 chamber settling weirs to allow for the sand to separate from the water.  After the settling chambers the water is combined and injected with liquid sodium hypochlorite.  Water is then stored in a 1,000,000 gallon storage tank.  From the storage tank, water flows to a hydropneumatic tank for increased water pressure then to the distribution system. A Baldor 140 GPM pump is available to send water from the 1,000,000 gallon storage tank to the Grotto 300,000 gallon storage tank as needed.

The upper canyon is served with water from the Upper Grotto.  The Upper Grotto (60 GPM) produces water that flows to a 3 chamber sand trap to a 5 micron cartridge filter and 1 micron bag filter and then is injected with liquid sodium hypochlorite. The Lower Grotto produces water at 60 GPM that flows to a sand trap (a 5,000 gallon storage tank) to a 5 micron cartridge filter then a 1 micron bag filter and then is injected with liquid sodium hypochlorite.  After chlorine injection, the water from both the Upper and Lower Grotto is combined and piped to a 300,000 gallon storage tank.
source_notes: The location of Watercress Spring was not found within the POD database.

Kolob Canyon has a well that is 206 feet deep fitted with a pitless adapter and sanitary grout seal.  The water from this well has been high in TDS, iron , and sulfates (within MCL Ranges) in the past.  Although the water is safe to drink, it is not aesthetically pleasing.  From the well, the water is chlorinated and flows to a 12,000 gallon buried concrete storage reservoir.  From the storage reservoir water is pressurized by two Well-X-Trol hydropneumatic tanks operated in parallel.  This water supply is used for flushing toilets and cleaning purposes.  Bottled water is sold to visitors in the visitor center.
source_notes:
A project is in place to greatly increase the size of this system. The project will include new distribution line, a new 50,000 
gallon tank, and new restrooms.

description: The East Side System is served by a well with a pitless adapter.  Water is produced at 10 GPM.  Water can flow to either the storage tank or directly to the treatment area if needed.  The system has a 40,000 gallon storage tank with and average usage of 400 gallons/day.  From either the well or storage tank, water flows to the chlorination room, where it flows through a 5 micron cartridge filter and is injected with liquid sodium hypochlorite.  After chlorination, it flows to a 200 gallon hydropneumatic tank then to the distribution system.
point_of_use: General domestic and other park purposes
source_notes: East Entrance Well. Previous state water right id for this well was 81-514 and was replaced by state water right id 81-4467

description: The Temple of Sinawava is fed by a spring that flows at approximately 25 GPM.  Water flows from the spring to a 5,000 gallon storage tank.  From the raw water storage tank water flows through a meter and the untreated water is piped to the toilets while the drinking water flows through a 5 micron then a 1 micron cartridge filter in series.  After filtration liquid sodium hypochlorite is injected into the water through a HE Anderson venturi chlorination pump.  Water then flows through a 125 gallon reaction tank and through 40 feet of 12 inch C900 pipe (to provide chlorine contact time).  This water source services a restroom and drinking fountain.
